# Routing for AI Training (and inference) Clusters by Petr Lapukhov


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fstorage.googleapis.com%2Fsnipd-public%2Fsideload%2Fsideload_cover.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: Routing for AI Training (and inference) Clusters by Petr Lapukhov
- Show: Your uploads
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/aceb6127-c397-4c7c-89b7-946b1b19f7fc)
- Episode publish date: 2025-12-01
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> High-performance cluster networking for GPU systems has been traditionally associated with large-scale pre-training. As time passed, post-training and distributed inference applications brought their unique requirements as well. This talk surveys the training/inference parallelism-induced traffic patterns from training/inference of LLMs and the impact on evolution of scale-out and scale-up network. For details check out https://chinog.org
</details>

- Show notes link: [open website](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t7d3gTQ6WHo)
- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Snips


### [04:31] Early Data Center Networking Shift


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:01 - 04:31)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/676a19a4-7659-4563-8455-cd2cf016c519)


**Early Data Center Networking Shift**

- Petr Lapukhov recounts joining Microsoft around 2010–2011 and working on Bing infrastructure with 100k servers as the norm. 
- They moved from brittle L2 designs to using BGP after OSPF proved unreliable in large clusters.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So, first of all, a lesson from 2011-2010. Was the time when I got lucky and I randomly joined Microsoft, the group that worked on the search infrastructure, Bing search engine. And back then it was a big joke because nobody liked Bing except maybe Steve Ballmer. So people thought we were a bit of outliers. So we had a lot of freedom back then to build infrastructure on our own. At that time, you know, this was also 2010, 11, people were crazy about OpenFlow, SDN, and everyone thought it was done for Cisco and everybody else. We were going to run these controllers and manage everything. But anyways, on the application side, the things were pretty straightforward. You had a lot of machines. Interestingly enough, look at the power per server, which is around 200 watts. These were like small machines like blades you plug into the sleds you plug into the chassis. Keep this number in mind because you will compare it to GPU systems later. So the scale was just, you know, massive back then already. 100k was norm in a big data center. The power for building was maybe 20 or 30 megawatts. And you can get about 100k servers easily. It means CPU endpoints. But traffic, as I mentioned, was mostly fan out, fan in. So, query comes in, you fan out to the servers, they respond back, maybe do more queries, but it looks like a big cobweb of requests flying back and forth. But the consequence was, it wasn't that traffic heavy, honestly. Back then also, next thing came up, which was Hadoop and data mining. People realized they can build cheap clusters to process lots of data from the click streams, other data, to build the indexes and so on. And that was much more traffic heavy, as we'll see. The good part of that time was, if you remember Cisco and everybody else was still like dominant networking. It was all this L2 design based with what's called VPCs, right? Virtual port channels. Unfortunately, I think now we're kind of confused with VPC as Amazon's VPC, but anyways. So, back when it was pretty big, my first experience with VPC was we turned off the cluster, it didn't work. So, it was like 10k machines in one VLAN, we'll spend multiple switches. People boot the servers, they don't boot because of the app storms, you couldn't even do digital discovery, basically. So, at that time, people quickly realized it's not really working and we need to do something real quick. There was, of course, a lot of work in ITF around TRIO, Fabric Path, proposing the scalable architectures for L2, but I think most people just realized, you know, we have to make it work Anyways and try to build something based on routing. So, fun story, of course, we began using OSPF and ICES. I don't know why ICES, because back when ICES was only supported by maybe Cisco and some models. But anyways, OSPF didn't work. In my experience, we started using OSPF in these big clusters. It just was so many bugs implementation, it just didn't work. And BGP was available already. And so, you know, one thing after another, it became de facto standard. Myself and a few other guys, we published this RFC. Well, back when it was a draft, it became RFC on BGP design for data centers, which to my surprise, big surprise, became almost, you know, a norm in data centers.</blockquote>
</details>

### [09:33] TCP: Reliable But CPU Intensive


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (06:35 - 09:33)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/73977693-fcee-44aa-9042-5df5b71a9af1)


**TCP: Reliable But CPU Intensive**

- TCP was the unsung backbone of data-center networks because it handled multipath and small-flow workloads robustly. 
- But TCP's CPU cost and copy-heavy design made operators look for kernel-bypass and NIC-offload alternatives.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>I think people often overlook in networking, talking about routing, is transport, TCP. I think TCP was probably, I wouldn't say unsung hero, but it was this dark energy beyond data centers which made it all work because it's proven to be very robust and resilient in this Environment, especially multi-flow splitting. And it was a lot of work around 2010 and 2000s to make TCP more efficient data center. So there was a whole movement how to make TCP more DC friendly. One thing I want to notice here is that TCP is by no means the most efficient transport on the host side. TCP does a lot of copies of data on purpose, because it's the design. You have to separate concerns from your user space. And it does chew a lot of CPU cycles. So, of course, at the time, there was many people who were like, well, CPU is being taken by TCP. We need to move TCP to the NIC, a flow of TCP to the network card. But all of its efforts mostly died out because it was really hard to support that. Trying to move TCP stack to the NIC was a pretty tough idea. Many have tried. I think Chelsea was still doing this, if I'm not mistaken. But eventually this idea died out. Melanox back then was a different support company of NVIDIA, did offer RDMA NICs, which means RDMA is not TCP, of course. RDMA is transport in the NIC, but the API to this transport was different than the sockets, right? And it offered these whole things like kernel bypass, CPU savings, but nobody wanted to operate this back then because it was just too much headache, you know? First of all, RDMA was built for InfiniBand, right? Then Mellanox said we can do this thing also for Rocky, you know, for Ethernet. Unfortunately, Rocky V1 was on EVLAN scale, so you could not really run it at bigger fabrics. And at that time, Google didn't publish anything, but there was a lot of rumors that Google tried it and didn't work. So this thing stuck around, you know, like 10 years later, when I started trying to do Rocky at Facebook, people came and said, well, Google tried this 10 years ago and it didn't work, So why would we do this again? But we'll see. Importantly, the only problem we really encountered with TCP was in-cast, right? When you have requests converging back to the same server, you see all the packet drops. Anticlimactic, the solution was mostly to tune the timeout values on TCP, and that solved a lot of problems very, very quickly. And there was a lot of suspicion that we're going to have lots of big fat flows in the fabric, which now materialized. I think the eventual conclusion was that traffic would receive application had lots of small flows, they don't really consume too much of networking bandwidth.</blockquote>
</details>

### [12:52] GPU Data Path Evolution


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (11:15 - 12:52)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/920ca335-e478-40f1-b308-f141355779f0)


**GPU Data Path Evolution**

- Petr describes GPU-CPU-NIC data paths where GPUs originally copied via CPU memory, then NVIDIA's GPUDirect shortened the path. 
- This moved most of the data path to the NIC and created a black-box debugging challenge for operators.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>And GPU is green, CPU is blue because at that time it was mostly Intel CPUs and of course, NVIDIA is green. The key point here is that if you want your GPU to get traffic from another GPU with data, you had to copy data through CPU memory. So, this was the first path. You have to move data to DDR, copy back to GPU, and this was a very lengthy path. And, I mean, it did work pretty well, either way. I don't want to, like, you know, say anything bad about it. But, of course, people were like, well, can we shorten this path and just move data directly through PCci switch to to the gpu so this is how i think gpu direct began um i've got this like Date maybe 2012-14 when we was released in cuda but that was the thing that changed things for tcp dramatically right if you copy from cp memory you can use tcp and kernel in networking If you have to move data to gpu you have to make it talk to the nick so now a networking stack is truncated right there's no more kernel involved almost all data movement happens between The nick which runs the rdma stack and the gpu itself right so the kernel and cpu still involved to start the workload and do the transfer, but most of the data path was done on the NIC. Which also creates an interesting question, like what is happening in the NIC, right? Because you wouldn't get specs openly saying this is the implementation, you know, this is the, you know, CPU complex on the NIC. It's actually RISC-5 cores and everything else. It was pretty closed, right? So compared to debugging TCP in the kernel, this was a black box.</blockquote>
</details>

### [16:48] Collectives Drive East–West Traffic


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (15:08 - 16:48)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/070f6af2-c479-4716-ae7b-97359d671a74)


**Collectives Drive East–West Traffic**

- Collectives (e.g., all-to-all) map computation graphs to network flows and create predictable, heavy east–west traffic patterns. 
- These collective patterns change design priorities from north–south to dedicated east–west fabrics.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So for NVIDIA, it was Nickel. Nickel means NVIDIA communication library, I think, or collective library, I actually forget the name, but it's nickel. At that time, of course, our guys, well, back then it wasn't our guys, but people in NVIDIA built their own library because they can, even though there was probably 10 other libraries In the industry to communicate across the nodes in a group fashion. But the big idea was that Nickel implements a graph of computations, sorry, data movements of computations. Let's say a collective means you have 16 GPUs that need to shuffle data to each other to realize, let's say, much multiplication. Because the whole idea of training was you take a big model, you take your data, you split them into pieces and move them around to different GPUs. I mean, that's, of course, already trivializing, but the whole idea is that it's all about you shard the data or you shard the weights of a model across the GPUs. But as a result, when you do computation, you have to move data back and forth to merge the results together. And quite intuitively, this looked very much like Hadoop for all graphs, so it wasn't all that unfamiliar to people. So now, the important part is that a collective pattern, they say all to all, we move data across everybody, maps onto the network flow. And in the network, you may realize the flow, say, as a ring or like as some other graph, for instance. But let's see, what was basically the biggest consequence of this change?</blockquote>
</details>

### [20:14] Fabric Schism In GPU Racks


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (16:58 - 20:14)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f510dd25-9e54-43c0-9894-b8da70ff25f9)


**Fabric Schism In GPU Racks**

- Modern GPU boxes include many NICs per GPU and Envilink mini-fabrics inside racks, creating multiple overlapping fabrics to manage. 
- This 'fabric schism' forces operators to treat north–south and east–west networks separately.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So this is the HGX server. HGX means something hyperscale. Actually, I don't even know. I think inside the company would often argue what HGX means. But anyways, the HHIND100 board is 8 GPUs which connect over Envilink, right? There's some numbers here but don't really matter. It's just the numbers of bandwidth of these GPUs. The next system has way more GPUs than this one. But this is probably today the dominant deployment. So I want to highlight quickly before we move to collectives and nickel flows is Envilink fabric became the probably also another dark matter of GPU networking. It was introduced in a Pascal generation and slowly grew over into becoming a very big piece of GPU infrastructure. What was it, in Hopper, we had like, does it say 18 links per GPU? I think we can pack them on six switches, right? In the black well, you have now 18 switches in the rack, which are not visible most of the time, but they were there, and each of these devices is 28 terabit, right? So the evolution went very fast from just direct links between GPUs to the small mini fabrics in each rack. And these mini fabrics, they participate in traffic flows, as we'll see. So here, this slide shows you how would nickel traffic flows map onto the networks between GPUs. So here, I have these two orange and green rings. So this is how traffic actually flows between GPUs. You see it moves from one GPU to another over in the link, forms a ring, and then escapes out to the next node. And then closes the loop again and goes to the next node. So depending on your collective size, you may have very big rings which cross many main GPUs. And ring is only the one pattern, there may be other possible to realize the collective, but the essential piece is that you stitch together two fabrics, and the link, and your east-west Network. And in contrast to the old thinking, many companies, except Amazon, I think, realized that this RDMA network did be done separately. So as opposed to folding these networks together into one fabric, we now have these words say north-south and east-west fabric. I find this term pretty confusing, but they stuck around. So here you have a very simple diagram. On the right side, there's two servers, sorry, two GPUs, which connect over in the link. And they also fan out the connections to the east-west network. So you can imagine that every GPU has a NIC connecting to east-west fabric, right? And then CPUs connect the NICs to the north-south fabric. So now in your box, you have a bunch of NICs per GPU. Let's say eight NICs in HDX. Each NIC is, say, 400 gigabits per second. And then you have two or four NICs for north-south and also in the link, right? So I call it the fabric schism because now you have three fabrics to care about, right? And it became, I wouldn't say much more complicated, but became much more headachy to think about these things together, right?</blockquote>
</details>

**RoCEv1 Proof Of Concept**

- That prototype convinced skeptics that RDMA Ethernet deployments were feasible.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So this was the first cluster that, well, I built a few people back in 2019 when they started building something and when people thought that Rocky doesn't work. So we had to take one switch, which was, I remember fondly, it was, I think, Arista 7504. Yes, 7504 switch from Arista because it had 144 ports. This was a small prototype, but it was a bit historic back then because it showed that you can make Rocky work without any problems. So we plugged the NICs, it was Rocky V1, one VLAN, and it just worked out pretty well. And you can ask why the hell you have nine racks and not eight, because we used to have powers of two, so the ninth rack was for redundancy., it turns out we have so much silicon in one server, But then to fail more often than CPU machines, right? Because simply number of components is higher.</blockquote>
</details>

### [23:14] RDMA Reveals Elephant-Flow Risks


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (21:43 - 23:14)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/33883556-86bb-42d8-abc2-4d6b50b98e2a)


**RDMA Reveals Elephant-Flow Risks**

- RDMA/NICs push full line-rate instantly and expose large elephant flows that break ECMP assumptions. 
- Even a single congested link in a collective ring can slow the entire distributed job dramatically.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So, what changed RDMA coming to the stage, right? RDMA, as I mentioned, can push you full line rate in instant, right? TCP have ramp up, you know, all these nice things. TCP is living by clock, it has a CPU control loop. The NIC just copies the data on the wire and pushes the full line rate. So this was the first time you see the, you know, real elephant flows, full busy cycle on the link. And obviously, that does not play well with ECMP, because if you have two of these flows colliding in one link, you push back, right, and you slow down both senders. So even if the network is fully non-blocking, like one-to just one collision can slow down the whole collective, because as you remember, collective is a big ring, so if one of these Segments is slowing down, you slow down the whole collective. So it was a big headache, right? We've saw initially a lot of very bad performance behaviors with this topologies. Another thing is that now collective is a sum of its components. So if you have very long rings, they add up for latency. You have like 128 GPUs, you have 128 hops to traverse, and this adds up very, very quickly. You can overcome this, you can change collective type to do, you know, log data shuffling, like a tree style, but you now get in cast pattern, right? Ring has like very nice snake flow that doesn't have any contention, but incast with binary trees is pretty visible.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [25:53] Use Multi-Rail To Localize Traffic


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (24:41 - 25:53)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/ebafbb0f-4844-441e-8187-3bfef5a6914a)


**Use Multi-Rail To Localize Traffic**

- Use multi-rail (multiple uplinks to different leafs) to keep collective traffic local to lower-tier switches. 
- That confines contention to the first-tier switches and reduces spine congestion and latency.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>You may hear this very often, that people talk about rails, rails, rails, when they talk about these clusters. The real idea is very, very simple. It's that when your server connects to the next level of switching, it doesn't connect to one top of rack. You find out the links to multiple switches. Like in this diagram, each box at the bottom has eight nicks. So they connect to eight switches. It sounds trivial, right? But in reality, what happens is that now your traffic is load-bound at the root, right? You leave it, it already fan out statically to eight machines. And for most collectives, you can keep your traffic under these switches, right? So they never leave the switches above L1, which means all the contention management between flows happens in this L1 switch layer. So, this idea was, I think, pretty brilliant and very simple with some caveats, like if your collective has to leave this domain size, you still have to go above and contend for the uplink Resources. But that was very fruitful because it allows you to overcome a lot of the problems of load balancing because you push them to the first tier.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [27:24] Rails Trade Bandwidth For Reliability Risk


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (25:55 - 27:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/c40f7ca9-027d-4232-bb47-9eebe52deecc)


**Rails Trade Bandwidth For Reliability Risk**

- Moving to optical NICs and multi-switch rails improved bandwidth but increased component failure risk and optical reliability concerns. 
- Rail designs shift failure impact: a leaf failure now affects more GPUs than single top-of-rack setups.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>Now you're sort of connected to multiple switches, so you have to use the optical NICs, right? And optics in the NICs was always a big point of contention for many people because, you know, optics are less reliable, right? And copper is more reliable and cheaper, right? And lower power. So that was controversial, but it did work. So here I show how this materializes, right? You have two servers, one and N, they connect to two switches, and now your ring, traffic ring, see, it goes at one hop switch and goes to the other switch, sorry, and then goes to the next Server, not our switch. So you close all these rings and loops in one switch hop, which A, helps congestion, and B, helps your latency, obviously. That idea also scales, and if you see all the designs that we have for Spectrum X fabrics, which is our product, they are rail-based, right? Rail-based means you try to keep traffic as low as possible in the tree and only go above to the spine when you have to. But what it means is that traffic going to the spine volume-wise is much lower, assuming that you place your job correctly, assuming your communications mostly happens between these Groups of NGPUs, the pods, right? And then obviously the other consequence here is that if your leaf fails, it affects much more GPUs than before on top of rack switch. It's subtle, but it does matter.</blockquote>
</details>

### [29:08] Tune ECMP Or Adaptive Routing Carefully


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (27:24 - 29:08)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/a7d0e431-1182-47fb-b1d1-b6afe6be3ee8)


**Tune ECMP Or Adaptive Routing Carefully**

- If you choose top-of-rack copper-only designs, invest in fine-grained load balancing like ECMP or adaptive routing. 
- Tune and validate adaptive routing carefully because it requires packet spraying and NIC reassembly cooperation.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>Now, the other school of thought, which still exists, and I used to be a big fan of it, is that you just do top of rack switch. You don't do all the sleeves or else, right? You do only top of rack because it's copper, it's cheap, it doesn't flap, and it's pretty robust. The only consequence of this model that is very painful is that your top of rack switch has to load balance using ECMP or something more advanced, which is typically called adaptive Routing. That means that your flows get spread much more efficiently across the uplinks. And you can also add more uplinks to the RxWitch, which works actually everywhere. It gives you more redundancy. So this is the design we built at Meta first time in 2020, where cluster size was like 4K GPUs. It worked pretty well, you know, with some pain points making ECMP work, right, and making that different work. I would estimate it took a few months maybe of actual real work to get to a point where it became performant and without all the issues. And you know, mostly funny issues were primarily around like optics failures, link failures, imbalances and switches being down. Real quick, this is how a top of rec look like. So you can see flows here have to go up and fan out. So essentially you do rely on the perfect switch being able to load balance these flows effectively for you. Which I can claim, well, NVIDIA position that our switches are the best at that, of course. And of course, other people say, well, they also can do it, right? But you know, I'm not here for marketing.</blockquote>
</details>

### [30:38] Adaptive Routing Requires NIC+Switch Sync


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (29:16 - 30:38)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/dba3ccea-d515-4d8b-a3bd-ed36c26e18a9)


**Adaptive Routing Requires NIC+Switch Sync**

- Fine-grained load balancing and adaptive routing are essential in AI networks and require coordinated NIC and switch implementations. 
- Vendors controlling both NIC and switch hardware (like NVIDIA) gain an advantage in implementing adaptive routing.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>If you look at NVIDIA broad description, we always tout adaptive routing as the strength, which it is, right? Honestly, adaptive routing is hard to get right. There is no standards. It requires you to spray packets on the links, using flow that's all balancing, and then reassemble them into the blocks of data on the NIC side. So this requires doing work both in the network and in the NIC. So of course, whoever has the NIC has advantage. And luckily for NVIDIA, we had both, right, at the time. One thing I want to mention is that during this era of 2010 to 2020, many players got out of NIC market because it became pretty, I think, unprofitable. Stopped doing smart NICs, for example. Intel also killed their NIC products. So only NVIDIA and all this remained, I think, by the end of 2020s. And of course, people went, oh, shit, we can do the same thing, right? We can do the NIC. And I guess now many players tend to catch up. Well, not many. I think maybe Broadcom is they always have a NIC product, but they're never positioned initially as an AI NIC or RDMA NIC. And of course, you can imagine every company in the world, big CSP, like maybe four of them, they have their own products for the NICs, which we're trying to make work with AI fabrics.</blockquote>
</details>

### [31:59] RDMA Tuning Is Still An Art


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (30:45 - 31:59)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/ad94d3c2-6b6d-43aa-8eb5-bde9d75b88bb)


**RDMA Tuning Is Still An Art**

- RDMA congestion control and QoS remain bespoke and workload-dependent, lacking universal tuning playbooks. 
- Operators must treat RDMA tuning as an art and prioritize Nickel control packets in QoS to maintain performance.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>So first thing is that I mentioned RDMA is its own transport. There is no standards there, right? Well, there's a standard, the frame format, honestly. But if you make Broadcom NIC and Melanox NIC work together, actually it will work pretty poorly because they do things very differently inside. So initially RDMA would handle pocket loss by going back all the way to the lost pocket and just doing the recent of everything, which was very inefficient. Of course, people are not silly. They realized it's not efficient. They did a lot of optimizations on their own. There was no standards published. Lots of white papers, maybe. Some papers in CECOM from Microsoft saying you can make this thing work better if you do like QCN, right? But the essential part is that tuning these things is still like a bit of an art, right? Unlike this TCP, you may get some playbook, like I said, socket buffers set our parameters, this and that. Transport tuning is still like an art, right? It depends on the workload slightly somewhat, on the fabric scale, on the latencies. So every time you deploy something, you have to do some tuning, you know, so it works perfectly.</blockquote>
</details>

### [34:08] Power And Geography Constrain GPU Scale


[🎧 Play snip - 54sec️ (33:13 - 34:08)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b8948b26-b124-4be2-91e0-9cad1ab67b4b)


**Power And Geography Constrain GPU Scale**

- Scaling GPU clusters massively raises power and geographic distribution challenges that introduce latency asymmetry and fiber-run complications. 
- Training extremely large models may require spanning multiple buildings or regions, complicating fabric design.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Petr Lapukhov</b><br/><br/>It's taken over because mostly people are used to operating these switches and these networks. There's a few questions on how to build big, big clusters, like 100k GPUs. So remember I said 100k CPUs is like 20 megawatts. So 100k GPUs is about probably 150 megawatts of power. So you get very quickly 5x more power required to put these things together, which means they won't fit in one building usually, right? So you have to spread them out over across multiple locations. And that means longer fiber runs, like one kilometer, for example, And that creates a lot of interesting cases of asymmetries and latency increase, right? There is even, of course, more dreaming about let's connect together more GPUs, like one million. And that requires you to go across multiple regions, like across the continents, maybe even sometimes. Well, actually, not that far.</blockquote>
</details>

# David George - Building a16z Growth, Investing Across the AI Stack, and Why Markets Misprice Growth - [Invest Like the Best, EP.450]

## Episode metadata

<summary>Show notes</summary>
> My guest today is  David George . David is a General Partner at Andreessen Horowitz, where he leads the firm’s growth investing business. His team has backed many of the defining companies of this era – including Databricks, Figma, Stripe, SpaceX, Anduril, and OpenAI – and is now investing behind a new generation of AI startups like Cursor, Harvey, and Abridge.<br/>> <br/>>  This conversation is a detailed look at how David built and runs the a16z growth practice. He shares how he recruits and builds his team a “Yankees-level” culture, how his team makes investment decisions without traditional committees, and how they work with founders years before investing to win the most competitive deals.<br/>> <br/>>  Much of our conversation centers on AI and how his team is investing across the stack, from foundational models to applications. David draws parallels to past platform shifts – from SaaS to mobile – and explains why he believes this period will produce some of the largest companies ever built.<br/>> <br/>>  David also outlines the models that guide his approach – why markets often misprice consistent growth, what makes “pull” businesses so powerful, and why most great tech markets end up winner-take-all. David reflects on what he’s learned from studying exceptional founders and why he’s drawn to a particular type, the “technical terminator.”<br/>> <br/>>  Please enjoy my conversation with David George.<br/>> <br/>>  For the full show notes, transcript, and links to mentioned content, check out the episode page   ⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠here⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠   .⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠ <br/>> <br/>>   ----- <br/>> <br/>>   This episode is brought to you by ⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠     Ramp  ⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠⁠   . ## Snips


### [07:01] Chatbots Are Just The Beginning


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (05:40 - 07:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f4de3edd-b182-41d3-9504-455900a5f7d3)


**Chatbots Are Just The Beginning**

- Chat interfaces are provisional; AI will become proactive, multimodal, and long‑memory driven. 
- That shift opens vast, currently unpriced consumer economic upside.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Obviously the big topic that we're tackling and trying to figure out in the near future is the impact of AI. We've backed a ton of really exciting companies at every layer of the stack, and we can talk about that. And that's been part of our strategy from the model layer, infrastructure and tools, applications. I would break it apart into what consumers do and what enterprises do in the AI world. And then I have a bunch of views on how the world's going to be different as it relates to American dynamism, hardware plus software, robotics, autonomy, stuff like that. On the AI side for consumers, I think we need to be really humble about where we are right now. I don't think that we have yet found the dominant product in AI. We may have the dominant brand and OpenAI and ChatGPT has grown faster than anything in the history of technology. I think they reached the same scale as Google, something like four times faster, a billion people using it. And they're only monetizing a tiny piece of that, which I think is a really exciting dynamic. But I don't think that the future of how we interact with AI is going to be a chatbot. I just think that's way too limiting. I think the big shift will be what is reactive today to something that's proactive in the future. And chat GPT may be able to capture that. And I think they probably have the best chance of doing so. But I think the way that we interact with all this stuff is going to change dramatically. It's going to have long form memory. It's going to be multimodal and it's going to be proactive. It's going to offer us solutions in how we do things. So I'm super excited about that.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [08:03] P×Q Economics Scale AI Value


[🎧 Play snip - 46sec️ (07:16 - 08:03)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b8a72ee0-5729-439d-aa24-9ddc3271c11b)


**P×Q Economics Scale AI Value**

- Consumer AI is a P×Q business where time spent multiplies monetization per user over time. 
- Early low monetization can scale dramatically as product and ad/formats evolve.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>So I think it's instructive to look back at Facebook and Google. And I remember when we were in the private markets looking at investments in things like Snap and Twitter 10 plus years ago, and we would always sit and say, well, yeah, but Facebook and Google only monetize at X certain amount. And all the consumer internet businesses are P times Q businesses. And quantity has ended up being billions of users, two and a half billion users or more in each case. But we always said, oh, Facebook or Google, they make 20 bucks a user. So that's the upper bound. And fast forward 10 years later, and Facebook and Google make 200 bucks a user in the developed world. So when we look at things like ChatGPT, it's really fun to think about this. It's like, okay, how much time do people spend? What value do they get? How much consumer surplus is there? And how do we think about valuing that? And it's pretty open-ended, which is really exciting right now.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [09:24] Turn Model Capability Into Web Execution


[🎧 Play snip - 37sec️ (08:47 - 09:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0401ddd7-2c32-4b49-aba9-90ad7484d6ac)


**Turn Model Capability Into Web Execution**

- Build execution that lets models act on users' behalf across the web to solve real tasks. 
- Invest in product piping and guardrails because execution is harder than model quality alone.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>I did deep research on, you'll probably relate to this, a new baseball bat for my son. He's nine years old and it's pretty complicated. It needs to be a certain length and drop and all these certain specifications. And there's this year's version and last year's version. And if I had to do that on Google, it would be a total mess. I would struggle with it. Amazon, no chance because of the ads. Deep Research was really, really, really good at it. And it solved my problem for me. So light bulb went off for me at that moment. One, the models are going to get so much better. And two, to me, it's an execution problem of building the capabilities to go execute that stuff on your behalf on the web.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [12:42] Users Capture Most AI Surplus


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (10:51 - 12:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/d33b9031-c0ac-4c93-8bfa-a5d8c3aa4e60)


**Users Capture Most AI Surplus**

- Much of AI's productivity gains may accrue to end users rather than vendors, like historical tech shifts. 
- Startups can still build enormous companies even if most surplus goes to users.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>But maybe I'm slightly more skeptical about what their ultimate business models will be. So one of the really fun topics that people debate with high degrees of confidence that I have very low confidence in is what is the ultimate business models of these companies. And people put up these super compelling slides that are like, hey, you know, the whole software industry is only $400 billion. But look at how big white collar labor is. And we're going to go get a ton of that. And to me, that's a little bit hand wavy. So there's a couple of areas where the business model has progressed in a compelling way to go tackle that directly. So customer support is one. But because there's a very discreet task with very simple completion analysis that you can do. It's simple to price it on that. You can shift the business model from a seed-based thing for Zendesk or something to a new business model where if you successfully complete the task, you can charge on that. Maybe the next furthest developed area is coding, but it's not completion of a task. It's consumption-driven. And especially in the developer world, that whole world is used to paying things on consumption. It's how it has all shifted over the last 10 years. Everything else, I think, is pretty TBD. It's going to be very hard. And I think when you see major technological shifts, it's very tempting to say, oh, my gosh, there is so much economic value that all these companies are going to capture top down. The reality of doing it is much harder. And I always say to people, 90% of the technological surplus is going to go to the end users. Just start with that as the assumption, whether it's consumer, whether it's enterprise. A funny analogy that I heard from somebody else is how is the steam engine ultimately priced? It wasn't priced based on replacing 50 laborers. Competitive forces drove it to a certain price where there was an appropriate return on capital. But the vast majority of those productivity gains went to the end users of those machines, not the maker of the machines. So I think something similar will probably happen in the enterprise.</blockquote>
</details>

The reality of doing it is much harder. And I always say to people, 90% of the technological surplus is going to go to the end users. Just start with that as the assumption, whether it's consumer, whether it's enterprise. A funny analogy that I heard from somebody else is how is the steam engine ultimately priced? It wasn't priced based on replacing 50 laborers. Competitive forces drove it to a certain price where there was an appropriate return on capital. But the vast majority of those productivity gains went</blockquote>

### [17:35] Small Early Bet On Waymo Became Larger


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (15:50 - 17:35)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/a4e3d70d-d8d2-4e51-905c-ed19e57b6b5b)


**Small Early Bet On Waymo Became Larger**

- Andreessen Horowitz first invested small in Waymo in 2020 because it was promising but long‑dated. 
- They later expanded their position when Waymo proved consumer demand and had cars actively driving in 2024.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Our Waymo investment. We originally invested in 2020. They came to us to raise outside capital for the first time. So it's just purely funded by Google over time. And they thought it would be helpful for employees for hiring all that stuff, outside counsel, all that, diversify the cap table to bring on some outside investors. So some folks invested in it. We're the only VC firm that invested in it. We invested out of our first growth fund. And it was really fun because, I think this is seeing the future, taking the ride in 2019, it was doing some pretty amazing stuff. In retrospect, it could do unprotected lefts. It could avoid construction sites. And the thing it didn't know how to do actually was park. We got to a parking lot and it stalled and we had to override and go drive up to the front. But you could see signs that it was going to be pretty interesting, but it wasn't on the road. We knew they were going to be conservative about rolling it out. So Mark and Ben came to me and they said, hey, we got to do this Waymo investment. And I said, no, I don't like this at all. This is crazy. It's going to take 10 years. The valuation that we come in at is going to be really high. And they said, you know what? Don't care. Don't care. This is autonomous driving. Are you kidding me? This is the mother of all markets. If they have the thing that can drive cars autonomously, it's going to be worth a ton. Stop overthinking it. And my team, we had built all this analysis and why it would take forever and the economics were going to be strained. So we compromised and we made a small investment in Waymo at the time. And I was excited to be a part of it. I just thought the returns would be stretched. Fast forward five years later, at the end of 2024, they raised money again. And at the end of 2024, they had cars on the road. And it turned out, to your question, consumer preference slapped you in the face. Anyone who was in San Francisco who had the choice was taken to Waymo. But at that time, we had the chance to invest more money and it was working. So we took that opportunity to write a much larger check and invest.</blockquote>
</details>

### [19:26] Back Technical Founders Who Learn Business


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (18:20 - 19:26)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e817968c-34a1-44a7-83ca-8a061a971cd4)


**Back Technical Founders Who Learn Business**

- Prioritize backing technical founders who can evolve into great CEOs because they control product and early advantage. 
- Look for


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>And taste is very much, if I were to summarize in one line, I like to pay fair prices for great companies. And everyone would say they would like to do that. The art in that, I think, is recognizing where greatness may lie, where other people don't recognize that. Unpriced greatness? No, it's priced, but not to the fullest extent. So I've studied the history of technology companies and why they outperform and how they outperform. Often in growth stage investing, it's always on the growth side. It's like, hey, the growth side is where you get things really right. I tell the team that we can make a lot of mistakes on forecasting margins and business models and unit economics and all that stuff. But lots of people know how to do that analysis. That's out there. So where can you actually get edge? You can get edge from product insights, market insights, and people insights. So how do we maximize our likelihood of doing that? On the people side, I'll start there because that's probably the hardest to do. And I've gotten it right a number of times. And I think I have reasonably good taste in people. I really like a certain archetype of founder. I call him the technical terminator.</blockquote>
</details>

### [24:23] Untitled


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (23:20 - 24:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b8fc41e1-72d4-4100-93b6-f2a294ab7040)




---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>There's the scene with Alec Baldwin where he's running a sales contest, a boiler room setting, and he comes in, he's running a sales contest, and he walks in and he's like, okay, guys, New contest, here we go. First prize gets Cadillac. Second prize gets a set of steak knives. Third prize, you're fired. So we've adopted that as a way of describing most of the technology markets that we live in. So we happen to think, and I happen to think strongly, and my experience has been, the vast majority of market cap creation is going to go to the market leader. And this is probably underappreciated. We see this all the time with our peers in the growth investing industry, where they say things like, yeah, you know, even the number two player is going to be really viable. Maybe, but more often than not, that's not the case. That's obvious in network effect driven businesses, consumer companies, Google, Facebook, etc. It's less obvious in enterprise companies, but it happens just as often. There's no number two to Salesforce. Salesforce is Salesforce. Workday is workday. Service now is is service now. And you'd feel a lot of pain if you did the number two or God forbid the number three in those markets. In early days of technological shifts, markets tend to fragment in ways that we don't foresee.</blockquote>
</details>

### [34:35] Full Court Press To Win Figma


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (32:10 - 34:35)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/7146674b-4716-45bc-b2f1-bc129b6bdad6)


**Full Court Press To Win Figma**

- a16z pursued Dylan Field at Figma for years, doing board help and invites until Dylan was ready. 
- They debated market size but chose to invest, and it became a standout growth success.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>And he was like, we need this tomorrow. We got to invest in Figma. We need this tomorrow. I don't know how we didn't. We missed it. I was late to it. We just need a growth business. And it was a growth deal and we should have done it. And it's crazy. We did GitHub early. How did we not do this one? And he was just apoplectic. I need this. That was very encouraging, exciting. So day one, I told you, I knew the six companies in the portfolio. I also knew the five-ish companies that I really loved outside the portfolio. Roblox was one that I was close to. Figma was another. So from the moment I joined, we had done the full court press on Dylan. He came to our summit. It was Mark and Ben bear hugs. He was really into crypto. We bear hugged him on the crypto side. We did everything we could with him, helping him with a board search. We placed a person in our network onto his board. We were trying to do everything and trying to catalyze a deal. And he was like, I'll let you know when I'll let you know when. So COVID strikes and he calls us and he's like, now's the time. Oh my God. This was in the moment of COVID where we all thought the world was going to end and everything was screwed. Stock market was way down. I felt like, oh great, good timing. So at least we got the luck. So he came and pitched, we had done all the work and we're having the debate as a team. And me and my team, we're taking this traditional growth lens, looking at it. And we're like, the market for designers is not that big. It's really small. And if you do the math of the market size of designers and what they charge, I don't think the price makes sense at $2 billion. It's too limiting. And our venture guys were losing their minds in this discussion. They're like, you guys are totally missing the point. The ratio of designers to engineers is basically double for the modern technology companies. So that's a leading indicator. That ratio is going to change. There's going to be double the designers in the world. More importantly, the whole engineering to design process is changing. And there's a melding that's happening of front end engineering and design. So thinking about this as the market for design is way too limiting. So you're just missing the point. So we were debating it and it was like speaking past each other. And finally, Ben called it off. He's like, okay, all right, we're not gonna solve this tonight. And ultimately it was a call on the growth fund side. And I slept on it and I woke up and I was like, look, this is an exceptional business model. And we're squinting to believe enough on the market size. Great founder, great business model. Is the market good enough? And I'm happy to take that risk. The risk I don't want to take is quality of business, quality of founder. But you really had to have a nuanced view of the market in order to get there, like with a traditional growth investing lens. And so fortunately, we got there and it worked out really well.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [37:38] Block Time For Learning And New Deals


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (35:40 - 37:38)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e2b0d534-9549-47a8-b494-e50252cd7396)


**Block Time For Learning And New Deals**

- Block regular think time to learn deeply about new markets and avoid constant reactive meetings. 
- Spend most time on new companies rather than just known portfolio names.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>A longtime mentor and friend of mine and an operating partner at our firm, gave me this really good advice that he and John Donahoe at the end of every year always went through an exercise Where they spent two hours looking at their calendar from the year. And then they had an objective of cutting 30% of stuff that was on their calendar. There was a way for them to make sure that they were giving responsibility down to the people on their teams, but also that they would get leverage. So he's given me that. And then he reminds me of it when he can tell I'm too busy with things that I shouldn't be. So I think I'm not very good at this, but I'll answer the question anyway. I try to make sure I'm spending adequate time meeting companies. So right now, our investment business looks something like two thirds relatively known companies and one third newer stuff. But I want to make sure my time is spent pretty differently than that. I want my time to be 20% on those known companies and spending time with people like Ali and the founders of Andrel, like whatever it may be, flock safety. But I want most of my time spent on the new stuff, because I need to be learning about those new markets. So constantly meeting with AI founders, talking to smart AI employees, and making sure that I'm deep and conversational and have an understanding of those markets. So I spend a lot of my day on that. I've started to move away from doing one-on And I'm like, you know what, I don't need to schedule one-on I talk to my team all the time. I'll call them after hours. I've started to very deliberately block off hours and days. So I block off two hours every Tuesday, two hours every Thursday. And then I also put an hour and a half block twice a week in afternoons. And that often gets consumed with things that are pressing and I need to make calls or whatever it may be. But I find that I learn a lot and develop a lot of my own thinking just by having think time. I'm the kind of person that has 20 things open in the browser and I want to read them all and then I don't get to them. So unless I block off a bunch of time, I actually just don't find that I'm spending the time learning as much as I should.</blockquote>
</details>

### [38:39] Start Meetings With A Five‑Minute Vision


[🎧 Play snip - 29sec️ (38:10 - 38:39)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/3a20ed21-7e0b-4fbc-8237-f81a9952b0ab)


**Start Meetings With A Five‑Minute Vision**

- Let founders lead the first minutes: ask them to explain vision in five minutes and then interrogate. 
- Use pointed questions to reveal depth instead of long intros.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>The introduction super brief. I like to jump in and say, hey, why don't you please spend five minutes explaining to me the strategy and your vision? Because I've read your website. I know a little bit about the company. I've talked to some customers maybe. But what is the bigger thing? You tell me. And then I just ask questions for 20 minutes. Okay, so what do you think about this? What do you think about that? This may be a stupid question, but can you tell me about this? And I find that to be a lot more effective. And the ultimate compliment that we get from a founder is, thanks, you've done your research. Or, hey, thanks for asking that question.</blockquote>
</details>

### [43:37] Single Trigger Investment Decisions


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (42:30 - 43:47)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/36bd812f-e7a6-4e20-aa08-886928088bd3)


**Single Trigger Investment Decisions**

- a16z growth fund uses a single trigger puller investment decision-making process like their venture process, rather than a traditional investment committee.
- This encourages exploring investment risks and rewards without political influence.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>So for example, one of the things that we set up at the outset was a bit of a different investment decision-making process than a traditional growth equity investment firm. So most growth equity investment firms have an investment committee. It's central. You go, you present, you battle to get the votes, and then they disappear, and then the smoke comes out, and here's the decision. And what we decided to do at the firm in the growth fund was do it totally differently. So we were going to actually make the decision process just like our venture process, which is single trigger puller. And the expectation I have set with our team and that Mark and Ben have conveyed, and I think we do a pretty good job of is you got to be intellectually honest. You've got to be transparent. And openly expect disagreement. But once you disagree, you disagree and then you commit. I think by doing it this way, you encourage people to fully explore the risks of investing and fully explore the rewards. You're never in this temptation to sell or to politic for a vote or try to influence someone's decision for the wrong reasons. You really like something and you really want to push. We don't have that dynamic. So I think it allows us to more openly explore the merits of an investment. And I think it's been a reasonably good process. And we're small. So we move very fast. We do this very iteratively. It's not like we need to have</blockquote>
</details>

### [46:18] Early Product Cycle Trumps Cheap Capital


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (44:58 - 46:18)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/a8ba9c11-ecc8-4390-96ab-1890ed9c5d03)


**Early Product Cycle Trumps Cheap Capital**

- The ideal growth environment pairs early product cycles with a weak capital cycle, but those rarely coincide. 
- Early product cycles matter most for generating outsized returns.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>If you could cook up in the kitchen, the perfect environment for you to be deploying dollars, what are the features of it?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Well, the optimal would be early product cycle, bad capital cycle, but those rarely happen and coincide with one another. If I had to pick, it's all early product cycle for the style of growth investing that we do.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>What does that mean, early product cycle?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>It means we're at the outset of a new technological change. Beginning of a market wave. Yeah, the new market wave. So maybe it's easiest to highlight in retrospect. It turns out that when you and I were starting our investing careers, we started at a really good time. You did. I was in public markets. Well, you were in public markets and so you had to deal with GFC and stuff. But notwithstanding that, it's capital cycle, that one. It's obvious in retrospect, but it's really hard to feel it in the moment. Maybe less so because AI is so well covered. And the question is, are we in an AI bubble now? Not is there a good product cycle ahead of us? But it turns out that at the same time, we had the mobile. We had cloud, SaaS, e-commerce all at the same time. And that was a great setup for us. If you look at all the mistakes that we've made as an industry, 2021 is very well covered. I always tell people the biggest mistake from 2021 is that we were actually late product cycle. And we just didn't realize it at the time. There was a bit of a head fake with COVID, but we didn't realize we were late product cycle. And what that means in practice is the ideas are just worse. The market opportunities are worse. It's just harder to go be successful.</blockquote>
</details>

### [49:06] Markets Misprice Persistent High Growth


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (47:02 - 49:06)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/a5225566-7464-429f-be9e-bd5737dc29a9)


**Markets Misprice Persistent High Growth**

- Public markets underprice persistent high growth because it's unnatural to model long multi‑year growth. 
- Investing in >30% growers at reasonable multiples can be less risky than low‑growth buyouts.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>So I'll have this debate. First of all, I recognize that revenue multiples are flawed and all that, especially for traditional investors. But what I tell our investors is, if I could invest for the rest of my career in 112% growing companies that are really, really great and good in markets at 21 times revenue, I would do it In a heartbeat. I would do it in a heartbeat. And oh, by the way, we used to have this debate at GA. I think that's way less risky than something where you're buying a 12% grower and PE for 15 times EBITDA. In a weird way, it's less risky because growth just takes care of so much for you and de-risks so much for you. I think above 30% growth, the market still doesn't fully value the growth rate. Why is that the case? I think it's just hard to model. My conclusion, I've studied all these companies that are, I call them the model busters, but I've studied all these companies and it is just so hard for any investor to build a five or 10 year model where high growth persists. It's just not natural. No one built a financial model for Google or Visa that had them growing 20 years into existence at 15 or 20 percent. It would just be totally unnatural to do so. If you look at the moment of the iPhone, and this goes back to the point about product cycles and how much you can get surprised. In 2009, if you looked at consensus estimates for Apple and then compared for 2013. So 2009 consensus estimate for the year 2013 and compared it to actual performance in 2013. Consensus estimates were off by 3x. That's a massive number. And that's the most covered company in the world. So I think you can be surprised on growth on these things. I get a big kick out of that. I try to learn a lot about it. But I think it's not natural to model anything that way. It's so natural to just say, hey, this company is growing 80%. Then they're going to grow 65, then 50, then 40, then 30, then a terminal growth rate and a terminal margin. And here's what our numbers are. It's very different than a company if it grows 80 and then the growth rate persists, 75, 65. It's like a 3X difference in your valuation. So you can just get it massively different. So that's why I love high growth. It's obvious. That's the math behind why I love it.</blockquote>
</details>

### [50:52] Prefer Pull Over Push Businesses


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (49:20 - 50:52)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b9068d58-575f-428c-bce2-ab2fe2e5a8b4)


**Prefer Pull Over Push Businesses**

- Test if the market is actively demanding more of your product; pull businesses scale far easier than push ones. 
- Favor products with organic adoption, high retention, and increasing usage over time.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>You find a pull business. So I have a post-it note on my computer in the office that says, is the market demanding more of your product? It's the most special thing when it happens. By the way, a lot of these AI companies, what's so magical about the way ChatGPT has grown? It's a billion users. It's organic. It's all brand. And the shocking thing about that one, by the way, is it doesn't have a network effect. That was one of the more surprising things for us. So is the market demanding more of your product is probably the most important question that we can answer. Because when it happens, especially in consumer, it tends to create the most special companies in the world. So we've seen it in companies like Roblox when it really works. And that one has two network effects. And so it's super special. We also see it in companies that aren't network effect or consumer. Like in the case of Andrel, turns out the market really, really, really is demanding more of their product. And there's many reasons for that. We've reached all at the same time, this confluence of AI capabilities, autonomy, know-how, and how to navigate governments, mostly from alums of companies like Palantir and SpaceX, At the same time that we have a desperate geopolitical need. The market is demanding more of their product, and that's really special. One of the things that I say about push businesses, which is you got to go sell it, sometimes those are really successful. And there's industries where this is the case, like cybersecurity and things like that. They don't tend to get easier over time. They tend to get harder. If you have to go sell or market your product, the bigger you get, often it gets harder. So that's not always the case. Sometimes you get increasing returns to scale from brand and things like that. But especially on the consumer side, it almost always gets harder if you're a push business.</blockquote>
</details>

### [53:15] AI Unit Economics Evolve With Usage


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (51:29 - 53:15)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/26ee6bc6-4965-48a5-8d69-aaf89bd98edb)


**AI Unit Economics Evolve With Usage**

- In AI, ease of acquisition, durable engagement, and falling inference costs determine long‑term winners. 
- Low gross margin today can be acceptable if usage and product value grow as costs decline.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>One is ease of customer acquisition. And we see this with the really, really special ones like cursor, which has been largely viral growth. So ease of customer acquisition, it happens even with things that need to be sold, like a bridge. You got to go sell to hospital systems. It turns out hospital systems are dying for this because the doctors love it. It's really good. It saves them a lot of time and it's really valuable. So ease of customer acquisition is something that is sort of a must for us in this AI wave. The second is customer behavior, customer retention, customer engagement. There are some head fakes that we've seen of things that grow really fast and then they fall off and they're experimental. So the things that have durable behavior, things like cursor, where the users really, really use it and ideally or increasingly use it over time. Harvey is an example of a company where as the models have gotten better, customer engagement and usage has actually really grown. It actually took a step change, which we've seen, which is interesting to see because it happened at the same time as the reasoning breakthroughs. We're like, oh, that makes sense. Actually, lawyers need to reason. And turns out models got really good at reasoning and people use the products a lot more. So ease of customer acquisition, the behavior that we observe on customer retention engagement. And then there's gross margins. And we give a little bit of a pass on gross margins right now. We're in this funny environment where late stage SaaS cloud, we would look at a company and it's like, oh man, if you're not 70% plus gross margin, you're not really a SaaS business or Cloud business, whatever. And that's going to be a knock and people will trade you differently. And that's when you get valued as revenue versus gross profit or whatever. Now it's like a badge of honor to have low gross margins because we're like, oh, at least people are using your AI products. We get these pitches and they're like, I'm an AI thing and I got 75% gross margins. I'm like, well, no one's using the AI stuff then. That doesn't really seem like an AI product to me. So we give a little bit of a pass on that. The expectation is the cost is going to continue to go down. Just the inference cost. Inference cost is going to go down over time.</blockquote>
</details>

**Cursor Showed Instant PMF And POCs**

- Cursor's product attracted rapid bottoms‑up adoption that immediately turned into enterprise POCs. 
- a16z observed repeatable enterprise interest after viral consumer usage, signaling product‑market fit.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>I'll use a recent one that the product is so good that people just naturally gravitated to it as cursor. And again, maybe in the fullness of time, that'll get harder. But GitHub is a great example of this. I'll tell a funny story about GitHub too. So GitHub was so special of a company that for a long period of time, they never actually talked to customers. So the first time I ever met GitHub, they were like, we got to tell you this. This is so awesome. We sold to Walmart and they're paying us 400,000 bucks and no one ever talked to them on the phone. We were like, wow, this is an incredibly magical product and incredibly magical market. Just imagine if you had talked to them on the phone. What would they have paid you if you just called them on the phone? Like they probably paid you $4 million. So unique product that leads to unique distribution with a founder that wants to optimize the situation. So the AI founders, I'm not the one involved with Cursor, but Michael is a very special founder and his team. They recognize what they have, and then they are aggressively pursuing the enterprise at the same time. So that's a really good combination where you have unique product, you have great product people love that leads to some uniqueness of distribution. And then you can build on that advantage by saying, Hey, we have all this bottoms up use, like we're going to go sell enterprises. And so a big part of what we do as a firm is we help to facilitate customer introductions, new business, we call our go to market function, they're referred to as EBCs sometimes. And we get notes after every one. And this is the most fun thing in the world of AI because we get these notes. And in the case of Cursor, every single time, it's like immediately to POC, immediately to POC, proof concept, whatever, immediately to POC. And oh, immediately to full sale deal. And you can see that. That's actually incremental data for us and making decisions, but you can see it. It is magic it happens. So Martine led the A of Cursor, one of my partners who leads our infrastructure fund. And after one of these emails, he chimed in. And it's a big list. It's like 100 people on the list or something. He wrote product fucking market fit. So now we're like, oh, you know, PMF is now PFMF. So when you see that, you know, that's unique product, that's unique distribution.</blockquote>

### [58:57] Decentralize To Scale Expertise


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (57:20 - 58:57)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/cadb3aca-cf1b-40c5-9b1b-ba9fe3e4166d)


**Decentralize To Scale Expertise**

- Decentralizing decision power into focused funds improves domain expertise and scalability. 
- Trade-off: loss of one big cross-team investment forum but gain faster, expert decisions.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Strategy for scaling is pretty well covered, but effectively we think scale allows us to bring more power to the entrepreneurs and give them a greater chance to be successful in the Market. That's the fundamental thesis behind the scaling for us. And with more resources, you can bring more resources to bear for the entrepreneur. So for us, when I joined, every single Monday and every single Friday, we used to sit in a room together, all of us. And we'd hear all the pitches, and then we'd have long meetings to talk about each of them, all as a group. So Dixon was leading our crypto fund and we'd have bio fund pitches and we'd all listen to all of them and then we'd all debate. And then we realized at a point that was not the optimal use of time. Dixon weighing in on a bio investment and vice versa probably doesn't make sense. And you could extrapolate that out to a bunch of our investment processes. Ben and Mark decided to decentralize the firm and put more power down into the investing teams that ran each investment fund. And the reasoning behind that is twofold. One, we thought it would allow us to have better expertise around the table. If you're only just fully deep in infrastructure or applications or American dynamism or crypto or bio, that's an advantage. It's both an advantage in making decisions, but also an advantage in go to market with the entrepreneurs. And then secondly, if we are going to scale, you can't scale an organization with 25 or 30 decision makers around the table. It's too hard. You can't make a trade off between should we put an incremental dollar into a bio fund investment or a crypto investment or how should we think about reserving this versus that? It's too hard. So we shrank the size of decision makers by doing this to a smaller group who's in charge of their own funds. And so far, that's working really well.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:00:51] Make Follow‑Ons Fresh Decisions


[🎧 Play snip - 56sec️ (59:54 - 01:00:51)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/6020e2e0-62ae-4d10-b10e-eefd6dbe0866)


**Make Follow‑Ons Fresh Decisions**

- Treat each follow‑on as a fresh investment decision rather than automatic reserving. 
- Reserve minimally for small participations but avoid large precommitted reserves that breed complacency.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>It's how has the founder done this?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>How do you do reserving in the growth fund? Is it materially different than elsewhere?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>When we first started the growth fund, I was like, Scott, zero reserves. Let's do it. Every single dollar is going to have to be scrutinized, literally every dollar. It turns out that's not really practical. You need to reserve a little bit. So we reserve a tiny amount. And this is for small follow-ons where our participation is important, but we're not a lead. We do zero reserving for large investment amounts that we think we're going to make in a company, because I think that would lead to lazy decision making. We'd say, oh, well, we reserved for it.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>Let's do it. So you just treat it as a new investment next time?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Every single thing is a new investment. So if you look at our largest investments in the growth fund and just run down the list, Databricks, SpaceX, Handroll, OpenAI, XAI, Phlox Safety, Figma, Stripe, Coinbase, most of them Are across multiple funds. And that's by design. We want to be flexible and say, hey, if we're super excited about a new investment, it's fine.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:05:47] How Startups Outrun Incumbents


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:03:08 - 01:05:47)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/7b4c646c-7abc-4dc2-be53-24006c213f0f)


**How Startups Outrun Incumbents**

- Startups beat incumbents with business‑model shifts, new UI/UX, and novel data sources. 
- Combined, these create defensible advantages incumbents struggle to replicate.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David George</b><br/><br/>Model shift is a super powerful thing that's very hard for incumbents to react to. That's part of what is so exciting about the customer support industry in Decagon. The odds are so stacked in their favor because the business model is going to be very hard for incumbents to react to. And it's, on the customer side, better, faster, cheaper. Fully better, faster, cheaper. By a lot. By an order of magnitude in each case. So business model shift is one. The two simple components that I'm looking for, which generally we're not really seeing yet, is completely reimagined UI and then completely new sources of data. So we're large investors in Databricks. We're very optimistic about the data layer. I think they'll have some success in enabling applications built on top. But the UI UX thing and the data thing paired with a business model shift, I think are what are going to give the startups the best chance against the incumbents. And the more dramatic the shift in those, the harder it's going to be for the incumbents. So take Salesforce.com. I use this as an example. It's a good company. I never thought it would be as big as it is. It's a good company. So maybe they'll be one of the incumbents that survives and reacts. What do people do in Salesforce.com? It's basically like a sophisticated form checker with some analysis and it's brutal. It's painful to use. The future with AI is not going to be anything like that. It's just going to be, to my point earlier about proactive versus reactive, it's going to be a proactive thing. You, a salesperson, you're going to log into your Salesforce and it's going to be like, hey, these are the five customers that you have business that you should be doing. Oh, by the way, I've been monitoring what they've been doing online. There's a shift in this group. You got to be aware of it. I've drafted a call script. This person actually likes to be talked to on the phone. This person wants just to engage via your AI email. I've drafted one for you. I've already taken a bunch of action on your behalf. Here's what you need to do. That's going to be the future, I think. And then the data that goes into informing that is no longer the database that makes Salesforce so powerful. It's all the unstructured data that's getting pulled from every interaction that everyone has everywhere. So my hope is that the fullness of the new product has that entirely reimagined UI UX. The fact that it's pulling all this new data from different places is an advantage to incumbent because Salesforce is so sticky because of the column or database that they have. And then if you, on top of it, have a new business model that's attached to it, I think that's a really good shot for a startup to be able to finally go rip Salesforce out. If you look at the SaaS and cloud wave, basically the whole story was a 7x-ing in the amount of revenue in the market. There's this question of like who wins the incumbents versus startups. It basically split 50-50. So 7x more revenue, incumbents grew a bunch. They took half of the new share, startups took half the new share. I think the more dramatic the shift, especially with the more dramatic the shift in potential business model, the more likely it favors the startups. That's the bets we make.</blockquote>
</details>

But speed generally, I think, is super important, you know. It comes to this first principle, things shouldn't take longer than they need to take, you know. You save time by being fast. So this sounds a bit strange, but if you answer, if I send you an email and you answer within one minute, how long does that email need to be? Two words, right? And I'm impressed because you answer really quickly. If you answer the next day, how long does it have to be? A paragraph? And you answer a week later. It needs to be a page, right? And so I asked them, hey, why are you so good? Well, you know, we have two reasons. One, we have a very great, we got a really good debrief system. And also we work with sports psychologists, you know, to work with a mental type of, you know, strength. And so what is important in sailing is that, you know, you can be number one and then the win direction changes and then suddenly you're the last one. Now, next race, you need to take the same type of risks. You know, you cannot let it impact you. And that's the same way it is with investing. You know, just because you lost, you need to get back on your horse and take the same amount of risk. And it's very difficult. And that's why you need some psychologists to help you with this. You know, I did a podcast with Magnus Carlsen, the chess player. And so I asked him, hey, what happens when you lost a game? Do you take more or less? That's fascinating.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Because I was thinking as you were saying that a little bit, that the worst people would take less or more risk. ### [33:34] Principles For A Stable Wealth Fund


[🎧 Play snip - 53sec️ (32:40 - 33:34)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/370d2d72-ba75-4fbd-acb9-c40181f12aa5)


**Principles For A Stable Wealth Fund**

- Build a sovereign wealth fund with broad political anchoring, full transparency, and a strict spending rule. 
- These elements help the fund remain stable across political cycles and serve long-term national interests.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Nicolai Tangen</b><br/><br/>I'm too humble to advise anybody in that field. What has worked well for Norway is to have a sovereign wealth fund, which has broad political anchoring. So the political parties generally agree on the big decisions, which means that when you have a change of government, you do not change the way you run the fund. That's important. Then transparency, we are the most transparent fund in the world, and there is actually a world championship in that, and we won it three times in a row. It's a world championship, which has been run by, actually a Canadian company called SEM in Toronto. And then the last thing is we have a spending rule, which means that the politicians spend 3% of the fund every year, maximum of 3%. So I think the broad political anchoring, the transparency, and the spending role.</blockquote>
</details>
