### [26:58] Models Are Commodity; Distribution Matters


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (24:15 - 26:58)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f985d7d5-4398-4973-bd32-5a1186535fc7)


**Models Are Commodity; Distribution Matters**

- Models today are commoditized and many LLM products look and behave similarly across providers.
- Distribution, brand, and product integration may matter more than raw model differences initially.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>I mean, we're asking, okay, well, there are some people who are saying all of our capture is going to be in the models. There's this kind of funny split between people who are just talking about the models getting better and everybody else who's saying, well, all the value is going to be in the application Layer. And, you know, what are the companies and let's fund Cursor and let's fund all of this stuff. And why isn't there a consumer breakout yet? And other people are saying, what do you mean there isn't a consumer breakout? Everyone's using chat GPT, which answers, well, not exactly, which is my data point. Some people are using chat GPT. Most people look at it and don't get it. Still, just fascinating there's this kind of core where's the value capture question then there's like a bunch of questions we could have asked two years ago where we don't have an answer Will the error rate ever be controllable or manageable will you ever get to a model that knows when it's wrong which to me seems just like given the statistical system seems like a contradiction In terms but maybe it was a bunch of kind of you could make a list of like a dozen questions we could have asked in early 23. We don't really have answers to any of those. I mean, there were some people who were asking, are these things commodities? Will China catch up to a true answer? Even then was obviously yes, of course, which is what happened, which Deepsea kind of demonstrated. But we don't have that many new questions since then. The thing that I puzzle about right now is, first of all, there's this whole nexus, as I said, of like, what is LL, what is SEO for LLMs? You know, we have infinite product, infinite retail, infinite media. How will you choose what to buy? What happens if I go to an LLM and say, what mattress should I buy? What life insurance should I get? How does that work? That poses dozens of questions we don't know yet. Then there's the question around like the differentiation in the LLMs as product. Like it seems to me right now you could do like a double blind test of the same prompt given to Grok, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, DeepSeq. Do a double blind test. I bet most people wouldn't be able to tell which is which. That question of like, is there product differentiation? Can there be product differentiation around the LLM as consumer product? Because right now the models are commodities, but ChatGPT is way, way, way, way, way more usage. So ChatGPT is at the top of the outstore rank. Gemini bubbles between like 50 and 100. None of the others were in the top 100. Same in Google Trends. Same in the usage numbers. Same in the revenue. There's revenue for corporate APIs. Corporate is a whole other story. But as a consumer thing, it's like ChatGPT is now like the brand. It's the default. It's the Google. You use it because you've heard of it. And none of the others have broken it. Is that where we are now? But then if you look at the products, not only the models are all the same, the underlying models are the same, the products are all the same. It's really hard to tell the difference except they've got different color schemes and different icons.</blockquote>
</details>

### [29:07] LLMs Lack Strong Network Effects Today


[🎧 Play snip - 59sec️ (28:07 - 29:07)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b5d67d74-77d9-4b34-aad7-5074d1c4ee7b)


**LLMs Lack Strong Network Effects Today**

- LLMs currently lack clear network effects where more users improve the base model.
- Memory and personalization create switching costs but not the classic app-store-style network effects.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>So the winner, there doesn't appear to be a sort of self-reinforcing cycle in which more people use it because more people use it. The product gets better because more people use it. So more people use it, which is what you have with operating systems because you have more apps, therefore more users, therefore more apps. So what you have with Google Search, that Google has all the feedback from how people use it that makes the search engine better. You have a network effect on social media that you're there because you're there, because your friends are there, because you're there. There's no apparent equivalent in LLMs right now. There's no reason why the LLMs get better because more people use them. Now, that may come. You have that, OpenAI and people have been doing memory, where it remembers what else you've asked, but that seems more like a switching cost than a network effect. And also, it might be easier for you to just ask it what it knows about you and then tell Claude, or vice versa. So it's not clear if that. But we are at that sort of stage where you're looking at the browser and saying, is there a way that you can create stickiness here, or that you can create a network effect on the browser? Or is it just that the browser itself is a commodity?</blockquote>
</details>

### [34:18] Adoption Is High Awareness But Low Habit


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (31:27 - 34:18)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/273cb77b-2c2a-4f93-a78e-f449c5b99ea1)


**Adoption Is High Awareness But Low Habit**

- Surveys show a small core of regular LLM/chatbot users and many people who try it once and drop off.
- Widespread awareness doesn't equal habitual, high-frequency usage across the population.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>Yeah, I think this is really important. There's a whole bunch of survey data on how people how many people are using this stuff you've got the numbers from so so so so chat open ai pretty say well we've got this many weekly active Users the funny thing about social is people when social happened people would talk about registered users you know in the early days of the internet people would talk about hits yeah And then we realized that if your web page has seven items in the menu bar that's seven gifs so that's seven hits so hits was meaningless and you had to switch to page impressions and then It's registered users and then it's monthly active users and on social people said well hang on if you're using instagram once a month you're not using it it's daily active users or nothing And weekly active users we don't like either now open ai is doing weekly active users and sam altman was a social media startup founder he knows this is a bullshit number you look at survey Data and i had to did this slide in the last presentation I did of like five different surveys from the US from late last year, earlier this year. And it's all roughly the same. It's like something around 10%, give or take three or 4% of people, depending on the survey, are using this, say they're using this every day. Sort of 15 to 20% of people say they're using it every week. And then there's, say you've got like, say 10% of people using it every day, say 15 or 20% of people using it every week. Another 20 or 30% of people who say I use it every month or two. And another 20 or 30% of people who said, yeah, I had a look, I didn't get it. And then you have this survey where people say 70 percent of people are using AI. And I'm like, wait, what do you mean? There's a whole other rabbit hole, which is, you know, people say, well, did you use Snapchat's face filters? Oh, so you're using AI. What do we mean by AI? So let's be specific. Let's talk about are you using a consumer-facing LLM chatbot? Like you're going to chat to UPT or Claude and asking questions. Like that's the number we want to look at. And to me, there's a bunch of you could matrix this. So some of this is it's early. There's a counterpoint here, which if people do the chart and they say, oh, my God, it's so fast. It's like it's faster than smartphones. Yes, because you didn't need to buy a thousand dollar smartphone. Right. It's faster than PCs. Yes, because you know what PCs cost in the 80s adjusted for inflation? It's like five grand. Yeah, it's free for a lot of them. It's free. It's a website. You just go there. Of course, it's got faster adoption. And there's way more people online as well. So even the absolute numbers are faster than they were for Facebook 20 years ago, 15 years ago, because there's way more people online now. Yes. So that's, again, an example of my unfair but relevant comparison. You're sort of standing on the shoulders of Jones. So, of course, you can get to way more people quicker. But you have to keep asking, well, yes, but. Why do so many more people look at this and not get it? Or even worse, the not getting it, I can kind of see. Because people look at everything and don't get it. Why is it that somebody looks at this and gets it and goes back every week, but only every week? Why is it they can only think of something to do with this once a week? I worry about those people.</blockquote>

### [37:17] Ship AI As Integrated Features, Not Blank Chatbots


[🎧 Play snip - 37sec️ (36:40 - 37:17)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e86e64b2-dfa0-4485-8632-ab1c4d0f2042)


**Ship AI As Integrated Features, Not Blank Chatbots**

- Embed generative features as explicit product buttons to drive mass adoption.
- Turn AI from a blank canvas into clear, task-oriented features people instantly use.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>Were using Salesforce and you had a button that said, draw off me an email to reply to this client, then that gets massive adoption. Well, that's a feature that we talked about earlier. That's a different thing. Yeah. Is it the chatbot as product where you get this blank screen and you kind of look at it and you scratch your head and you have to think, well, what is it that I would do with this? And then you have to form new habits around it. Or is it that it's wrapped in product and UI where somebody else has said, it would be really useful for this, wouldn't it? And then you look at it and go, oh yeah, I could do that.</blockquote>
</details>

Depends how big the circle you're measuring is um you know this is the line about pi that you know we can calculate it the light than however many digits we have is like enough to calculate You know the diameter of the gal of the universe or something but people still adding more numbers so you know there's a little bit of xeno's paradox in here you know you get infinitely Close at a certain point it doesn't matter and this is at a high level this is some of the agi argument that if the thing gets infinitely close to reasoning without ever actually reasoning Does it matter like a certain point the thing if the thing is always right if the thing is if the thing is only wrong once in a billion years does it matter that it's not that it's not always Right the problem today is it's not wrong once in a billion years it's wrong a dozen times a page you don't want to spit that out and give it to somebody. That's like generations ago. That's not relevant to the point I'm going to make. The point I'm going to make is, A, it was always the right kind of biography. It was the right kind of degree, the right kind of university, the right kind of experience, the right kind of jobs. It just wasn't actually the right things. But B, I could take that and fix it. ### [41:16] Use Writing To Test Originality


[🎧 Play snip - 32sec️ (40:43 - 41:16)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/c1f080e9-09da-48d2-8cc5-73cc0cc1d1b4)


**Use Writing To Test Originality**

- Use writing to check whether your ideas add value beyond what an LLM would produce.
- If your draft matches ChatGPT, don't publish it; push for questions others wouldn't ask.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>I, well, actually, my chatty pretty use case, which is a more mental model than a practical thing, is I write something and I think it's actually what I always would ask in the past is kind Of your point about pattern recognition is I look at something and say, am I adding value here? Am I saying something useful? Am I saying something different? Am I asking the key question? Am I pushing further? Am I pushing? Am I asking the next question rather than just answering the obvious questions? Now I can just say, is this what ChatGPT would have said? And if the answer is this is what ChatGPT would have said, then I don't publish it.</blockquote>
</details>

- He uses it to show history repeats as new distribution and merchandising models emerge with tech.

<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>And it's basically about a 19th century Jeff Bezos calling a department store into existence out of thin air through force of will. And like he invents, fixed prices so that you can have discounts, loss leaders and mail order and advertising. And, you know, he puts the slow moving expensive stuff at the top of the store and he puts food and makeup on the bottom of the store. There's nothing new under the sun. And meanwhile, the shopkeepers on the other side of the street are saying, like, have you seen what that maniac is doing now? He's selling hats and gloves in the same shop. He's got no morals. He'll be selling fish next. And of course, he's got this counter. The whole plot point is about loss leaders. So you can, again, you're going to step back and think, well, people have freaked out about industrialized, mass-produced product before. People have freaked out about there being too much content. There's a line that Erasmus was the last person to have read every book. There's too much AI content slop on the internet now.</blockquote>
</details>

### [54:42] Calibration Comes From Contrast


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (52:43 - 54:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/dc0f8db5-2614-45ed-a457-d789b292e152)


**Calibration Comes From Contrast**

- Seeing many startups or masterpieces calibrates judgment and reveals patterns of what truly works.
- Exposure to contrast trains better pattern recognition than isolated examples.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Benedict Evans</b><br/><br/>That's probably, I wouldn't want to dignify them as, like, theses or anything else. But there's a whole bunch of maxims and sayings in venture, which, you know, we could have a podcast talking about. There are better people to give you a podcast talking about the mechanics of venture. But like you're understanding what startups are and how they work and how the machine works. And startups are an industry and Silicon Valley is like a machine for creating startups. And still too many people kind of look and say, well, that was a dumb idea. And it's like, well, that's the wrong question. The question is, if you look at a startup and you think, could it work? And if it did work, what would it be? And could those people make it work? And then you understand more, you like the mechanics of, well, how does social media work? And how do people build companies? And what is it like to create a startup, which is a whole other conversation. I think something else that I learned was calibration. This is sort of, again, another metaphor I always think of, which is that if you go to like a really great art gallery, like you go to the MoMA or, you know, the Met or the Louvre or something, Everything there is a masterpiece. If you go to a smaller, weirder art gallery, like London's a gallery in London called the Wallace Collection, or I was in Rome a couple of weeks ago and I went to one of the sort of old aristocratic Palaces. And this palace is like, it's like 10 or 15 rooms of pictures. And they've got like a quite good Tintoretto and a maybe Titian and a Raphael. You see it glowing across the room. They're like, oh, that's why he's Raphael. And it's the same when you see lots and lots of startups. Like, oh, that's why he's Max Love Chin. Oh, no, that's why this is bollocks. Like, you get 10 minutes in and I've got another 45 minutes. I've got to pretend to be interested and polite so the founder has a good experience. It's seeing that contrast and texture and seeing what good looks like, seeing what worked, what didn't work, what people tend to say, how this tend to work.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:13:39] Remove Barriers First


[🎧 Play snip - 43sec️ (01:13:06 - 01:13:49)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/378fc93e-c8e7-4289-a4d6-1d76cf0687b6)


**Remove Barriers First**

- When you want to encourage something, focus on removing obstacles rather than just adding more incentives.
- Removing the obstacle is more powerful than adding force.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>I love the idea, and I think this works in almost all cases. When you want more of something or you want to encourage something, it's not necessarily what you can do. It's what you can remove. And if you think back to Daniel Kahneman, he had these two notions of behavior. And you could push the right behavior or you could remove the barriers to the right behavior. And those two things are different. So there's basically an equilibrium and you can add more force to it or you can take away force on the other side that's preventing the behavior in the first place. And one of the things that Danny said to me that I really appreciated was, you know, I would start with removing the obstacle before adding force. And it's much more powerful. And I think that there's a lot to be said there. In a world where, you know, politicians need</blockquote>
</details>

### [39:01] Find Mission In What Obsesses You


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (36:07 - 39:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/5c9cbe74-1aba-462a-939f-4dec3d942dac)


**Find Mission In What Obsesses You**

- Find your mission by noticing what you obsess over and where you pay attention to detail.
- Match passion to value other people reward within practical limits.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Oh, number 12. I'm a longtime listener of The Knowledge Project, and I've always been curious about the beautiful strings of music used in your podcast intro and outro. It really set the perfect tone for the show and complemented the thoughtful discussions beautifully. Oh, thank you. For the AMA, I'd love to know, where did you source this music? Did you work with a specific composer, use the library, or commission it? For the podcast, I'm interested in both as a fan of the show and someone curious about the creative process. Ah, we used a local artist in Ottawatawa and we commissioned it we own it we went back and forth a few times i wanted something really original and i really always liked yo-yo ma and so i Wanted something on the cello and just something really unique that set the tone. And I'm glad you appreciate it. I'd have to look up the name of the person because, God, this was like 10 years ago now that we did this. But, yeah, it was amazing. And, you know, I sort of went back and forth with her about things that I liked and didn't like. And we just ended up with that piece. And I think it really sets the tone too. So I'm glad you appreciate that. Number 13, I really want to understand how to find my mission. I'm currently reading Elon Musk's biography, and the one thing that stands out to me is his incredible and unrelenting ability to align his vector towards his goal of making life multi-planetary. I think the question to ask with this stuff is, like, what are you obsessed with? Where do you find yourself paying attention to detail and nuance? Where do you find yourself thinking about things in moments where you wouldn't normally have to think about those things? Like, what do you think you've been in the shower? And I think those are good leads to finding your mission. But I don't know I'm not some sort of guru in this domain I think you should just dive into you know what fills your heart and with within reason you also have to earn a living and make money And I'm not a follow your passion kind of person I'm a follow your passion and match it to like what are you passionate about that can that other people find difficult or um that society Rewards so can you be useful but everybody's situation is different so if you're independently wealthy then you know feel free to just follow your passion but, or your mission could Be to help other people. And I think, um, I don't know, I think we all just have to figure that out. And I think part of it is part of the journey is figuring that out for yourself. And I think it also changes, you know, your mission at 20 is very different from your mission at 40 and your mission at 40 is probably very different from, uh, your mission at 60 or 70. And I think it becomes a little bit less self-centered as you go forward.</blockquote>

### [43:07] Leverage Human Connection Against AI

**Leverage Human Connection Against AI**

- To stay relevant, add emotional connection and authenticity that AI struggles to reproduce.
- Use your face, voice, and lived experience to build an audience AI can't easily replace.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>My question was to ask for guidance from Shane on thinking strategies to best use AI with. I'm finding more and more of my work is being augmented by AI, taking notes in meetings, framing comments for LinkedIn posts, and summarizing longer documents. I'd be interested in hearing what guidance you can offer from the standpoint of applying mental models to this conundrum. Well, I mean, there's two approaches that I would think of with that. One is you need to find a way to add value because if you're not adding value, you're just going to be replaced by AI it's cheaper it works harder it never stops the second thing is I don't Know the particular business that you're in but one thing that AI hasn't been particularly good at and will take a long time probably to get good at is you know you mentioned LinkedIn Posts and summarizing you know I think it's building emotional connection with people and being real and being tangible so i think about this a lot from the context of the podcast right We've inserted up to 22 minutes of ai generated voice by shane so my words but an ai reading it sounding like me and you know we did this experiment I'm okay failing in public and pissing A few people off by experimenting and I think some of my learnings from this were were probably six months away from that being almost indistinguishable from me However, it's flat in A way that I can't explain. So it'll sound like me and it'll be virtually indistinguishable from me, but there's a feeling aspect to it. So pause right there for one second. I create a bit of a feeling with people. Now think about books, right? I think about this as well. Why would I write another book? I mean, are books even going to sell in three years? Why would people buy a book when they can just basically, you know, open up chat GPT and say, apply clear thinking to my life in a one page five page and ten page summary and it goes through And it literally takes my work and it specifically applies it to your life in the most dense detailed action-oriented way possible that i can never create that and what i do think where I think people have an advantage in my space particularly because I mean I can't apply I don't know enough about your space but the LinkedIn makes me think that you're sort of building An audience in some way shape or form and one of the big changes that you guys have probably all noticed is like my face is on things now I you know i'm recording videos i'm putting myself Out there i never wanted the podcast to be about me it's always about guests or the people we're talking about on the outlier series but i think the world that we're moving to it's more Important that i have a relationship with you and you have a relationship with me and i think that that will be very hard for computers to replicate and AI to replicate so AI might you know Two years from now be able to script outliers better than I can it might be able to distill the lessons better than I can it might be able to ask questions better than I can it might be able To read better than I but I won't be as good at making you feel connected to somebody who's in the struggle or in the arena or going through the same things that you might be going through. I think it'll be, you know, a long time before we crack that code. And maybe I'm wrong and maybe that gets cracked away but I think building a human connection is going to be a little bit harder I think audiences are definitely going to change too.</blockquote>
</details>

### [52:17] Internal Monologue

So I think we talk ourselves out of doing things all the time because it's ego in a way. Like the fear of failure is ego if you really boil it down to things.</blockquote>
</details>

### [54:51] The Power of Self-Narrative


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (52:43 - 54:54)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/9e973cc3-cd6c-497d-95aa-ed7108702fcf)


**The Power of Self-Narrative**

- Recognize that the most powerful story in the world is the one you tell yourself.
- As conscious humans, you can edit that story and focus on creating a positive environment.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>And that means that the most powerful story in the world is the one that you tell yourself. And so what is the story that you're telling yourself? And the difference is we're conscious humans, so we can edit that story. We can listen to the story, and then we can edit it. And I find the most helpful way to edit this, to be honest with you, is not to work on myself first. It's to work on my environment first. And what I mean by that is if I'm feeling particularly negative, I just start to look at who I'm spending time with. Who do I want to be around? Who's positive? Who's optimistic about the future? And it's really easy to hang around people who are negative about the future. And you eventually, even if you're a positive person, you eventually become negative. So I had the benefit i guess of working in my i don't know early 20s to mid 30s and and i spend most of my life now just basically around people who who don't think anything's impossible really It's just that we haven't figured it out uh you'll notice, like no podcast guest has been like uber negative on the world. And I think there's a strong correlation to that. That doesn't mean that, you know, I have worked rarely with people who are incredibly smart, who are both cynical and optimistic at the same time. But i think the the best people in the world by and large are have a good at least external self-narrative now that doesn't mean they're not insecure it doesn't mean they're not driven By dirty fuel as brent bishore would call it in our podcast um versus clean fuel clean fuel is more internal dirty fuel is more like chip on your shoulder, insecurity driven. But those things do end up driving society forward and moving humanity forward. I think the courage to take risks is super important. We talked about this in the last question</blockquote>
</details>

### [58:55] Learning To Fail In Public


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (57:30 - 58:55)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e40bc195-8cdf-4775-86c2-5300cbc26ee7)


**Learning To Fail In Public**

- Shane learned to tolerate public failure and collected critical feedback early on.
- Repeated exposure reduced sensitivity and helped him keep producing work.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Like oh god I can't quote that person again or I can't talk about that person or wow I didn't realize I was offending so many people and I think at the end of the day you have to be able to take That and how you talk to yourself about that matters and I remember I used to like collect these emails and reply to them and I would be chirpy and you know other people be chirpy and it just Never went anywhere and I think um your internal monologue at that point could could sort of really hold you back or it can move you forward and I'm lucky in a way that I've had a chip on my Shoulder most of my life and failure doesn't really scare me so much uh that's not my inner monologue fear but I do want to be liked by other people i do want uh i do want other people to think Highly of me i want to contribute to other people's lives there are things that i want um and other people have the ability to control manipulate or um otherwise nudge me in those areas And i think that knowing that in and of itself helps you change your inner monologue to the point of, I want to put out really good quality work and I hope it resonates with people. But it really resonates with me. And I really hope it helps people. And at the end of the day, if it doesn't, it helps me. And so how do I just be the best for me?</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:02:49] Create Few Specific Personal Rules


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (59:11 - 01:02:49)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/c87eeecf-c8a9-41cd-899f-da826119e39a)


**Create Few Specific Personal Rules**

- Choose a small set of simple personal rules and design them for your weak moments.
- Make rules specific, limited (about three), and tied to situations where you usually fail.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Is it possible to put this into the members forum to get insights from others? Yeah, you can post just in the learning community and ask everybody their rules. You know, I sort of asked everybody to email me their rules from the book. So I think we've got a thousand or so by now for sure. A lot of them are the same, though. Different words, obviously. But I think the key here is you can't have 100 rules for yourself you can have about plus or minus five i would say start with three though and most of the rules are they have to be specific To you and your life and your circumstances so like a rule of spend less than you make makes sense for somebody because it keeps them in check and it doesn't make sense for somebody else Who does that naturally working out every day or as I prefer to think of it now as like sweating every day that comes that that one thing has just changed my life it's still one of my rules You know I have a nightly routine I'm in but by 11 I sort of follow the same wind down procedure with or without kids and I think that that helps too. And so it's just what are you trying to work on right now? Where are you least likely to make the desired decision? And can you design a rule around it so you've pre-decided? So the ones that I see over and over again are social. They're often social rules or they internal rules so like no eating after five or six p.m so that's a rule and that prevents you from going into the snack drawer it prevents you from grabbing That bag of chips or eating that tube of ice cream and i see the no drinking no desserts thing often when people email me email me these rules they apply them because they're trying they Eat out a lot for work or they have a lot of friends and they're often in these social situations and so they really design a rule to allow them to have their desired behavior which is maybe They only want to drink on on friday and saturday so they're they have a rule that they only drink friday and saturday even if they're out with friends um and so the no dessert thing is the Same thing right people just say no desserts and their rule is no desserts but it's not that they probably don't eat dessert it's that in social situations is really hard to do the desired Thing because we want to be liked by other people and going back to what we just talked about a little bit in the last part people will i don't know they'll chirp at you they'll nudge you To to do the thing that they're all doing in part because they want to feel better about their choices and the minute you don't do that thing they have to kind of think about their own choices Well if you're not dessert because you're trying to watch your health well that means that if I eat dessert I'm not watching my health is what you're really saying and that's kind of what They're hearing in a way maybe not consciously or unconsciously but I so it can rub people the wrong way but I think it also gives you the freedom and the confidence to just act on the thing That you want to act on. So the other ones that I've heard a lot are sort of boiled down to keeping your side of the street clean. You know, don't get involved in gossip, work politics, stuff like that. Swim in your own lane, focus on yourself. Stop focusing on other people. Those are nice little reminders and they're almost like derivative mental models in a way, like keep your side of the street clean or swim in your own lane. You have an experience that you can attach those to. I think we all have experiences where we can attach those to.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:08:06] Hire An Operating Partner Before Stepping Back


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:04:34 - 01:08:06)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/1da2db88-723c-4928-bd90-cf43d83646f0)


**Hire An Operating Partner Before Stepping Back**

- When transitioning from operator to owner, clarify the role you want and hire an operating partner or COO.
- Decide whether you'll be hands-off or on the board, and structure authority to avoid constant interference.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Thank you. Okay, so transitioning. Man, that's really hard. You know, if you're on the board, effectively, the person's going to be less CEO and more COO, if that makes sense. And maybe that's the way to think about it, is just to hire an operating partner instead of a CEO. I don't know. It depends on how far away you're going to be from the business. I've seen a lot of CEO transitions in the very short time of then post-government. I think there's so many unique things that go into every situation that it's hard to provide generic advice that's useful. I think you have to think about what you want and what role you're going to play. Are you hands-off or are you going to be hands-on? I think is a key question and if you're on the board you haven't really left, right? The company will still defer to you. You're going to have to deal with a whole bunch of problems that that creates and obviously you're going to stay on the board if you still own the company. I think the model that might be more appropriate if you're going to stay on the board to explore would just be hiring a COO with the eventual idea of either transitioning the business To them over time or transitioning them to CEO. But you really, you are like a board member then. You're not an operating executive from the board. I think it'd be hard for most CEOs who've created a business to step away completely. And these are questions that you just really have to dive into, Will, and figure out, like, what are you trying to accomplish? What's the best way to accomplish that? How do I attract the best talent? And talent wants freedom and creativity often, or a path to taking over the freedom and creativity. And so what does that look like? The other thing is, you know, people who are successful in one environment are often not successful in another environment. I would think of when you bring somebody over i mean are the people that we've had the most trouble with for example and you know internally is people who've done what we're asking them To do but they've done it for like a big company i think because we don't know anything about the space that we're in, we don't operate with many assumptions about what is or is not possible Or what things should or should not look like. And we sort of do a lot of experiments internally with our very, very small team. But when people come in and they think they know the way, and I think that we get into trouble with people who think that there's only one approach to things and so maybe you know I've seen A lot of CEOs struggle in this particular thing too where they bring somebody in from the outside and they don't fit the company culture if your company culture is sort of young and scrappy And you bring in somebody from the outside and they're old and stodgy you might like, but it's not going to work. There's just so many nuances that go into this without knowing more particulars. It's hard for me to be of help.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:13:13] Trust By Default, But Draw Firm Lines


[🎧 Play snip - 4min️ (01:08:30 - 01:13:13)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f85e7c15-df66-4c48-8762-4ea53fcd34b9)


**Trust By Default, But Draw Firm Lines**

- Trust by default but set clear pre-decided lines and be willing to walk away.
- Use deal structure and partner framing to reveal character and avoid one-sided arrangements.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>You know, I think I trust by default to 80%, but then I'll walk away if the situation isn't win-win. I think people reveal a lot about themselves by the type of deal that they bring you, by how they frame things. It sounds really weird, but it's true. If somebody's framing things in a way where they win and I lose, I think I want to partner with that person and if they're not considerate about me as a partner at the beginning they're Definitely not going to be considerate about me as a partner at the end you know I've been involved in a couple of situations that have changed since I got involved in them and I usually Just exit those situations for better or worse and I think you know a good example I was involved in this public company I think it was like 2006 ish and I was involved until 2011 I even actually Wrote an activist letter to a board can you imagine that you can still find it online if you're really good at googling yeah but this situation changed right and how do you this is a complex Decision involving uncertainty and people and money on top of that and business partners in a way because if you buy shares in a public company you're partnering with the management Of that company doesn't mean the partnerships the same way that we think about you know working in the same office but the question remains like how you strip away noise balance your Intuition with logic and apply mental models and i think you have to draw lines in the sand about what's acceptable and what's not and with the situation that i was just referencing it Was just the slow boiling of the frog. Not a drastic change, but slowly just somebody I didn't want to partner with anymore. And so you just leave. I mean, we tried to change it because we owned a lot of stock. I say we because I own a lot personally, but I was also involved in an investing firm at the time. And it's just really hard, those situations. I think by and large, I operate with trust with people and it's worked out really well. And when people take advantage of me or the situation is just not going to change, then I just sort of exit. I don't make a big fuss about it. I don't, there's just, life is too short to deal with a lot of this. A lot of people don't trust by default and they trust by earned trust. I find that in business, those people have a much harder time, if that makes sense. Maybe I'm wrong, but I think that it's much easier to operate the way that I operate, which occasionally you sort of get hurt, I guess, if you will. Like you get stabbed in the back or, you know, something doesn't work out the way you anticipate or somebody takes advantage of you. But by and large, I think my way of doing things is more profitable. It's faster. It allows you to get to see who people really are. And with very few exceptions, those mistakes are not as costly as they otherwise could have been. We've had complete investments written off because I keep saying we, but I mean, I hate saying I, I guess. I've had to write off investments because, you know, people have taken advantage of the situation. And generally speaking, that was great knowledge, you know, for I lost some money, but I will never deal with that person again or and then you know I start to question people who trust Them or and and so it's really interesting in that sense but how people structure things is a clear sign of what type of partner they're going to be but I'm very much in the mindset of trust By default. And then have lines. I feel like when people go into negotiations with real estate, I always ask my friends, what's your max price? And then you have to be willing to walk away. And it's the same in business. You have to be willing to walk away, but you have to draw those lines before you get to the moment. I've seen these situations where you basically just justify anything. I've been on the board in these situations where you're watching people and you're trying to pull them back. If you don't draw the line in the sand, you just keep changing it over and over and over again. And so I think it's just important to think about those things.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:16:53] Where Decision-Makers Disagree


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:14:38 - 01:16:53)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e9042441-7286-436e-ba17-b37851c365ca)


**Where Decision-Makers Disagree**

- Decision debates center on speed vs deliberation, intuition vs analysis, and individual vs group choice.
- The right approach depends on cost of failure, pattern-matching expertise, and organizational needs.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>You know, I had my thoughts on this and then I asked you all as members have access to like the AI transcripts if you go to notes.fs So I asked them. So I took my list and then asked where people disagree. So the most common area is where people disagree. And I got two out of the three of these when I came up with it. So speed versus deliberation appears frequently as a point of contention. So like Reid Hoffman, for example, is like all speed. Patrick Collison, all speed. Some other people, it's a lot of slow down be deliberate i think you know i think about this in the sense of when the cost of failure is low move fast and when the cost of failure is high move Slow and i think cost of failure changes and it's unique to you and your situation 10 million might be a low cost failure to a company like Shopify. It might be a huge cost of failure to a small startup. So I think that that is a key way to look at these decisions. The role of intuition versus a systemic analysis is also up for great debate. You have, you know, a lot of people rely on gut decisions. I think when you break it down, it becomes pattern matching at some level. And I think they also verify. So even the people who talk about intuition, I think that I've yet to meet people who've had an intuition done big deals that could have killed the company without sort of the check and Balance on it. You know, it's not like going to the store and buying a Snickers bar on impulse. Individual versus sort of collective decision making frequently comes up. You know, Gary Klein, for example, expressed a lot of skepticism about consensus decisions. I'm a big believer against, you know, committee decisions. I think individuals should make decisions. Other people want input from everybody and they think that we should go together, right? I think Alan Mulally actually had a really good answer to this.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:19:22] Use AI As A Tough Sparring Partner


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:18:19 - 01:19:22)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0929077c-aa7e-4fdc-962c-afd124f8df08)


**Use AI As A Tough Sparring Partner**

- Use AI as a sparring partner by assigning personas and hard prompts to expose blind spots.
- Ask AI to critique harshly and play expert roles to widen your perspective before deciding.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>I you know I think AI is the best sparring partner in a way in a lot of ways right so if you think of the source of all problems with decisions as being blind spots AI enables you to reduce your Blind spots. And whether you're assigning roles to people, I often do this. I assign roles. I'm like, here's a prompt, for example, that I would use. You know, I'm walking into a room and the room is populated with Warren Buffett, Paul Graham, Jim Collins, Cat Cole, like all of these people. I have a decision. And, you know, here's the problem as I see it. I want you to talk to me as if you were these people and assume their persona. And obviously, like I have a five or six page prompt for this, but this gives you an example of sort of like how you can use it. And then, you know, Buffett will look at it through the Buffett lens and Jim Collins will look at it through the Jim Collins lens and Charlie Munger will look at it through a lens that's True to Charlie Munger. And so I think you can just sort of like have it as the sparring partner.</blockquote>
</details>

# #226 Garry Tan: Billion-Dollar Misfits — Inside Y Combinator’s Startup Formula


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #226 Garry Tan: Billion-Dollar Misfits — Inside Y Combinator’s Startup Formula
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/e5edaea5-88d9-4fe0-ade0-25735002aec2)
- Episode publish date: 2025-04-24
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Most accelerators fund ideas. Y Combinator funds founders—and transforms them. With a 1% acceptance rate and alumni behind 60% of the past decade’s unicorns, YC knows what separates the founders who break through from those who burn out. It's not the flashiest résumé or the boldest pitch but something President Garry Tan says is far rarer: earnestness. In this conversation, Garry reveals why this is the key to success and how it can make or break a startup. We also dive into how AI is reshaping the whole landscape of venture capital and what the future might look like when everyone has intelligence on tap.   If you care about innovation, agency, or the future of work, don’t miss this episode. ### [10:20] YC's Interview Focus


[🎧 Play snip - 29sec️ (09:50 - 10:20)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/340d6320-6f3a-40ce-9bef-627f714392b0)


**YC's Interview Focus**

- Y Combinator's interviews focus on quickly assessing founders and their market understanding. Look for crisp communication and the founders' deep-rooted motivations for their idea.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Garry Tan</b><br/><br/>Surprising thing that has worked over and over again, ultimately, is in those 10 minutes, either you learn a lot about both the founders and the market or you don't. So we're looking for incredibly crisp communication. So I want to know, you know, what is it? And, you know, often the first thing I ask is not just what is it, but why are you working on it? Like, I want to sort of understand where did this come from? Did you just read about it on the internet?</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [13:56] Interview Signal


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (12:54 - 13:56)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/92393ce0-b7d0-41f7-bcfb-b108d903f7c1)


**Interview Signal**

- Garry Tan learned from Paul Graham that if you cannot understand a candidate in 10 minutes, the candidate doesn't understand what's going on either.
- Resumes may impress, but clear communication during an interview signals genuine understanding.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Garry Tan</b><br/><br/>One of the things I feel like I learned from Paul Graham interviewing alongside him so many years was that sometimes I'd go through and this person would come in, they had an incredible Resume, you know, they're like had a PhD or they studied under this famous person or, you know, they worked at Google or Facebook or all these really famous places. They had an impressive resume or they had the credentials of someone who I felt like, you know, should be able to do it. But then they had a mess of an interview. Like we didn't get any signal from it. We didn't understand or like it just it seemed garbled or, you know, at the end of it, sometimes they're asking like, oh, we just, you know, 10 minutes is too short. We need more time. And one of the things I feel like I learned from Paul was that if in 10 minutes cannot actually understand what's going on, it means the person on the other end doesn't actually understand What's going on and there isn't anything to understand, which is surprising.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>That's a really good point. I bet you that holds true. Do you look</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [35:38] YC's Impact on Unicorn Founders


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (34:30 - 35:40)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/eb5bb2ea-6d6f-4b86-9921-f0c4b9d4038c)


**YC's Impact on Unicorn Founders**

- A VC named Ali Tamasab found that 40% of the unicorns in the last 10 years were started by multi-time founders. 
- Of those multi-time founders, 60% are YC alumni, which highlights YC's significant impact.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Garry Tan</b><br/><br/>I have one other really crazy stat that like I'm thinking about all the time right now. There's a founder, there's a VC actually. His name is Ali Tamasab. He works at Data Collective. He wrote a book called Super Founders. And I get this email from him out of the blue. He says, did you know that about 40% of the unicorns from the last 10 years in the world were started by multi-time serial founders? Like multi-time founders are, you know, they know a lot more, they have networks, they have access to capital. Like that's not a surprising stat. You know, if anything, it's a little surprising that it's only 40%. Like you would have guessed maybe that was 80. But the thing he said after that really shocked me. He said, did you know that 40, you know, of those 40%, 60% of those people, the people who created unicorns the last 10 years, are YC alumni. Oh, wow. So I'm like, that's crazy. I'm really glad that YC exists now because even if YC today is basically a thing that is for first timers. We do have second timers</blockquote>
</details>

### [45:07] AI Enables Lean, Fast Growth


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (42:05 - 45:07)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b665246a-9e7c-4317-b746-f3f5a9f21878)


**AI Enables Lean, Fast Growth**

- AI startups achieve massive revenue faster with fewer people than past blitzscaling models.
- Intelligence on tap enables high growth with small teams.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Garry Tan</b><br/><br/>I think the biggest thing that is increasingly true, and we're seeing a lot of examples of it in the last year, is blitzscaling for AI might not be a thing. What's blitzscaling? So I think Reid Hoffman wrote a whole book about it. It was definitely true in the time of Uber. So that was sort of a moment when interest rates were descending. And then these sort of increasingly international marketplaces, these sort of offline to online marketplaces like Uber in cars or delivery, or you could say Instacart, DoorDash, You could throw in Lyft. There was sort of this whole wave of sort of the top startups were marketplace startups, but also in software too, this idea that scale could be used as a bludgeon, that the network effects Grow sort of exponentially. Then because you could have access to more and more capital, whoever raised more money would have won. And I feel like that was extremely true in that era, sort of the 2010s. And then in the 2020s, especially by, you know, we're in the mid 2020s now, I think that we are seeing incredible revenue growth with way fewer people. And that's very remarkable. We have companies basically going from zero to $6 million in revenue in six months. We have companies going from zero to $12 million a year in revenue in 12 months, right? And with under a dozen people, like usually five or six people. And so that's brand new. Like this is the result of large language models and intelligence on tap. And so that's a big change. Like, you know, I think we are seeing companies that in the next year or two will get $250, $100 million a year in revenue, really with under, you know, maybe 10 people, maybe 15 people Tops. And so that was relatively rare. And my prediction would be this becomes quite common. And my hope is that's actually a really good thing. Like this is sort of the silver lining to, um, you know, what has been really a decade of big tech, right? Like it's more and more centralized power. Um, you know, what might happen here is that, you know, and what we're actively trying to do at YC is we hope that there, you know, are thousands of companies that each can make hundreds Of millions to billions of dollars and give consumers an incredible amount of choice. And we hope that that will be very different than sort of this. The opposite, I think, was increasingly true. We have fewer and fewer choices in operating systems, in web browsers, the board, just more and more concentration of power in tech.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:45:46] Decompose Prompts for Accuracy


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:41:51 - 01:45:46)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/42f6a131-0fae-4bb2-a0c0-e33ca6cb8e33)


**Decompose Prompts for Accuracy**

- Break complex prompts into smaller steps to reduce AI hallucinations.
- Design workflows mimicking human tasks improves AI accuracy and reliability.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Garry Tan</b><br/><br/>I mean, I'm mainly a user. You know, I spend a lot of time with people who spend a lot of time in prompts. Probably the person I would most point people to is Jake Heller. So he's the founder of Case Text. He was one of the first people to get access to GPT-4. And we think of him at YC as the first man on the moon, in that he was the first to successfully commercialize GPT-4 in the legal space. So what he said was that they had access to GPT 3.5 and it basically hallucinated too much to be used for actual like legal work. Like lawyers would see one wrong thing and say like, oh, I can't trust this. GPT 4, he found actually, you know, with good evals would actually, know, give, they could program the system in a way that it would actually work. And what he says, he figured out was if GPT-4 started hallucinating for them, they realized that they were doing too much work in one prompt. They needed to take that thing that they asked GPT-4 to do, and then break it up into smaller steps. And then they found that they could get deterministic output for GPT-4 like a human if they broke it down into steps. Oh, interesting. And what he needed to do, I mean, I sort of, it's sort of equivalent to Taylor time and motion studies in factories. It feels like that's what he did for what a lawyer does. You know, let's say you have to put together a chronology of what happened in a case and what a real, he's a real life lawyer, so which is like sort of unusually perfect to figure out this Prompting step. Like he realized that he needed to look at what a real lawyer would do and literally replicate that like tailored time and motion style in the process and prompts and workflow. So for instance, doing this type of he would have to go through and read all the materials. And then this is why apparently lawyers have sort of their many, many different colored little flags and highlighters and things like that. They just get very good at doing a read through paragraph by paragraph, sentence by sentence, and pulling out the things that are relevant and then sort of synthesizing it. And so, you know, early versions of case text and a lot of it today, I think is still just doing that. It's like, what is a specific thing that a human does? Break it down into the very specific steps that a real human would do. And then actually, basically, if it breaks, you're just asking in that step to do too many things. So like break it down into even smaller steps. And somehow that worked. And like, basically, this is the blueprint that I think a lot of YCE companies and AI vertical SaaS startups are doing across the whole industry right now. They literally are taking, you know, model out what a human would do in knowledge work and then break it down into steps and then have evaluations for each of those prompts. And then as the models get better, because you have, you know, what we call the golden evals, basically you just run the golden evals against, you know, the newest model. Like, 4.0 comes out, Cloud 3.5 comes out, DeepSeek comes out. You have evals, which is basically a test set of prompt context window data and output. And you can actually, what's funny is like, it's even fuzzy that way. You can even use LLMs in the evals themselves to score them and figure out, does it make sense?</blockquote>
</details>

It's, you know, sometimes days, like sometimes weeks, like he'll just, you know, iterate and iterate and send it out to people for comment. So, um, you know, you would think that that's, oh yeah, that's like something, you know, startup 101, like, uh, you're helping people with their pitch. That sounds so basic. Like you believe something that nobody else believes and you need to be able to breathe that belief into other people and you need to do it in as few words as possible. Like, so if you, the joke is like, oh yeah, like what's your elevator pitch? But like you might run into someone who could be your CTO, who could introduce you to your lead investor, who could be your very best customer. And you will literally only have that time. You know, you will only have time to get two sentences in. And so, and even then, I mean, I guess it's kind of fractal. Like that's what I love about a really great interview. Like, you know, someone comes in and I'm like, oh yes, I get it. Like I know what it is and I know why that's important. I know why I should spend more time with you. That's what a great two sentence pitch is. And, you know, knowing what it is, is very hard. ### [02:27:36] Advocate Minimal AI Regulation

**Advocate Minimal AI Regulation**

- Global competition makes restrictive AI rules risky and hard to enforce.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Or eight now. And the results are phenomenal. This is AI teaching you from first principles math. So if you get it wrong, it actually explains it to you. It teaches. And it's specific to you and the mistakes that you're making. So every kid doesn't get the same lesson. And the results so far in El Salvador seem to be off the charts from what I'm gathering. And anywhere this has been tested, this is going to rapidly spread throughout the world, I think, in a way that empowers parents and kids and gets us back on that. We seem to have gotten off track on some of the math stuff, especially in the schools. And I do want to just talk briefly about the regulatory stuff. I'm a proponent of sort of loose regulation on AI. I'm not at the point where I'm scared or worried about AI taking over our lives. Do I think that that point will exist? Yes. Do I think we have to be cautious? Yes. But we're also competing in a world where regulations aren't going to be clear everywhere else. There's no worldwide agreement on this. I wouldn't want somebody to be able to opt out of this sort of prisoner's dilemma and get an advantage. And we sort of see this with climate change, right? Regardless of whether you think climate change is or is not a thing, I don't want to have that debate. But Canada, for example, is investing billions, hundreds of billions of dollars in climate change. And all these other countries are still polluting. And so that is a tax on Canadian citizens and a tax on the economy that makes it hard to compete in a worldwide arena. And you can see that on productivity. You can see it in all these different ways. And I don't know how to think about that. You can't have this worldwide body that people always defect. And so I'm more of the opinion of sort of quasi-free market, free market economy, getting back closer to that, not regulating AI as much, and letting people loose, letting innovation Loose, and then sort of being fast to react if we see it going down a dangerous path. And, you know, maybe I'm naive on that. I don't know. There needs to be some regulation, just to be clear. I'm not advocating for no regulation at all. I do think there needs to be some, but I think it needs to be the minimum possible to be safest.</blockquote>
</details>



---



# #229 [Outliers] Andy Grove – Only The Paranoid Survive


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #229 [Outliers] Andy Grove – Only The Paranoid Survive
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/5a5e4104-a0d7-4b55-86c5-aa5cc68f4dfd)
- Episode publish date: 2025-05-15
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Most people protect their identity. Andy Grove would rewrite his, again and again. He started as a refugee, became a chemist, turned himself into an engineer, then a manager, and finally the CEO who built Intel into a global powerhouse. He didn’t cling to credentials or titles. When a challenge came up, he didn’t delegate, he learned. This episode explores the radical adaptability that made Grove different. While his peers obsessed over innovation, he focused on something far more enduring: the systems, structures, and people needed to  scale  that innovation. Grove understood that as complexity rises, technical brilliance fades and coordination becomes king.   You’ll learn how he redefined leadership, why he saw management as a creative act, and what most founders still get wrong about building great companies. If you’re serious about getting better—at work, at thinking, at leading—this is the episode you’ll be glad you didn’t miss.   This episode is for informational purposes only, and most of the research came from The Life and Times of an American by Richard S. Tedlow, Only the Paranoid Survive by Andy Grove, and Tom Wolfe’s profile of Robert Noyce    available here   .   Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business.  Follow Shane on X at:  x.com/ShaneAParrish  Access your transcript today:  https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/outliers-andy-grove-229/ <br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

## Snips


### [24:28] Grove Joins Intel Volunteer Role


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (22:21 - 24:28)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b4b27065-934c-43a8-8d08-cc18b96d2875)


**Grove Joins Intel Volunteer Role**

- Grove joined Intel as the operational expert complementing founders Moore and Noyce.
- His management and execution skills turned brilliant ideas into scalable realities.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>The company skyrocketed from founding to industry dominance, then began unraveling due to management missteps. Its technical achievements were revolutionary, but the business itself was poorly run. Two things stand out to me here. First, Andy's willingness to follow the data over dogma, even at personal risk. And second was his recognition that technical brilliance alone doesn't build lasting companies. While his peers focused exclusively on innovation, Grove was already developing the organizational mindset that would later distinguish Intel. By 1967, Andy Grove had reached a crossroads. Fairchild's semiconductor, where he'd cut his professional teeth, was hemorrhaging talent. The exodus had begun with the traitorous eight engineers who had abandoned Shockley's lab to form Fairchild. And now Fairchild itself was spawning a second generation of Silicon Valley startups. The valley wasn't just growing, it was subdividing. When Gordon Moore and Robert Noyce announced they were leaving Fairchild in 1968 to launch their own semiconductor venture, Grove didn't hesitate and didn't wait for an invitation. The moment Moore mentioned their plans, Grove immediately declared, I'm going with you. And just like that, he became employee number three at Intel, short for Integrated Electronics. Intel's founding trio constituted semiconductor royalty. Gordon Moore was the visionary physicist who would soon formulate Moore's law predicting the doubling of transistor density every two years, a prediction that would drive the industry's Ambitions for decades. Robert Noyce, the co-inventor of the integrated circuit, brought charismatic leadership and industry credibility that opened doors and investor checkbooks. And then there was Andy Grove. What Grove brought to this threesome was something altogether different but equally crucial. A ferocious commitment to operational excellence. As one industry observer noted, in the semiconductor industry, management talent has been harder to find than engineering talent. By becoming a brilliant manager, Grove differentiated himself and the company. Here's the irony. Until Intel's founding, Grove had shown virtually no interest in business management.</blockquote>
</details>

### [35:45] Leadership as Orchestration


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (34:25 - 35:45)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f0377410-c6fd-4c7f-bbc0-12329ba30756)


**Leadership as Orchestration**

- Grove redefined leadership as orchestration, not supreme technical expertise.
- As complexity rises, coordination and collecting talent become more valuable than individual control.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>While obvious now, in the early 70s, this was revolutionary. Grove was inventing manufacturing analytics decades before the term even existed. The lesson here, the most valuable approaches often seem like common sense in retrospect, but require seeing what others don't in the moment. Looking back at the 1103 achievement, Grove wrote with uncharacteristic immodesty, making the 1103 concept work at the technology level, at the device level, and at the systems level, And successfully introducing it into high-volume manufacturing required, if I may flirt with immodesty for a moment of fair measure of orchestrated brilliance. Everybody from technologists to designers to reliability experts had to work to the same schedule toward a different aspect of the same goal, interfacing simultaneously at all levels. Four years earlier, in July 1969, Grove had cut out a description of a film director's job from Time magazine. Above it, he wrote, My Job Description. The clipping read, quote, Vision to Aspire. Any director master formidable complexity. He must be adept at sound and camera work, a soother of egos, a cajeweler of artistic talent. A great director has something more, the vision and force to make all these elements fuse into an aspired whole, end quote.</blockquote>
</details>

### [57:32] Firing Themselves to See Truth


[🎧 Play snip - 35sec️ (56:57 - 57:32)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/72563926-95a6-43a8-92d6-e33ac9208ae7)


**Firing Themselves to See Truth**

- Grove and Moore mentally ‘fired’ themselves to see Intel’s problems objectively.
- This helped them decide to abandon Intel's core memory business before it collapsed.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Business circles. It's the one I mentioned in the introduction. Looking at the terrible memory chip numbers for the latest quarter, Andy said to Moore, if we got kicked out and the board brought in a new CEO, what do you think he would do? Moore answered without hesitation. He would get us out of memories. Andy then posed a pivotal question. Why shouldn't you and I just walk out the door, come back in and do it ourselves? This mental exercise of viewing the company as an outsider would become the cornerstone of what Andy would later term a strategic inflection point, a moment when the fundamentals Of the business are about to change.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:03:42] Middle Managers as Early Warners


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:01:36 - 01:03:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/8a1eed26-b925-4693-8027-927294d73759)


**Middle Managers as Early Warners**

- Middle managers, dubbed "Cassandras," often spot early signs of strategic inflection points.
- Their frontline position gives them better insight than insulated senior executives.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>He wrote, the Cassandras in your organization are a consistently helpful element in recognizing strategic inflection points. As you might remember, Cassandra was the priestess who foretold the fall of Troy. Likewise, there are people who are quick to recognize impending change and cry out an early warning. Although they can come from anywhere in the company, Cassandras are usually in the middle management. Often they work in the sales organization. They usually know more about the upcoming change than the senior management because they spend so much time outdoors where the winds of the real world blow in their faces. In other words, their genes have not been selected to achieve perfection in an old way. Because they are on the front lines of the company, Cassandres often feel more vulnerable to danger than do senior managers in their more or less bolstered corporate headquarters. Bad news has much more of an immediate impact on them personally. Lost sales effect is salesperson's commission. Technology that never makes it into the marketplace disrupts an engineer's career. Therefore, they take the warning signs more seriously. If you're a senior manager in a company, Andy explained, strategic inflection points arrive in disguised form. Top executives are often the last to recognize the fundamental shifts because they're insulated from market realities and emotionally invested in the status quo. Middle managers, by contrast, operate at the intersection of the company and the outside world. They usually have a better sense than the senior management of what's happening with both sides. Andy noted, their position gives them an unfiltered view of the customer shifts, competitive threats, and technological changes. Andy illustrated this with a powerful analogy, comparing strategic inflection points to fire drills in a theater. When the alarm sounds, audience members in the middle of the theater have the clearest picture of what's happening. They can see both the stage where the fire may have started and the exits. Audience members in the very front, like senior executives, may too close to the stage to see the big picture, while those in the back, frontline employees, may be too far from the action. At Intel, Grove created forums where middle managers' voices could be heard and respected regardless of hierarchy.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:13:37] Bounce, Don’t Break

- When you can’t change circumstances, control how you respond.
- Transform adversity into strengths by focusing on how you react to challenges.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>So lesson number one, bounce but don't break. Grove faced devastating childhood circumstances. A father sent to labor camp hiding his Jewish identity and permanently losing his hearing from scarlet fever. Yet he transformed this difficulty into advantage, developing extraordinary attention to subtle signals and the ability to make decisions with incomplete information.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:14:46] Face Reality Before It Faces You


[🎧 Play snip - 26sec️ (01:14:20 - 01:14:46)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e8ae49ac-0c0f-4bf5-b432-8e600cabf612)


**Face Reality Before It Faces You**

- Face brutal facts early to avoid disaster.
- Use mental exercises to detach emotionally and see blind spots in your business.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Three, face reality before it faces you. Grove's willingness to confront brutal facts became his defining leadership trait. When faced with Japanese memory manufacturers overtaking Intel, he asked Moore the pivotal question. If we got kicked out and the board brought in new CEO, what would he do? This thought experiment created distance from his own decisions and allowed him to abandon the very business that built Intel. He was effectively enabled to see his blind spots.</blockquote>
</details>
