### [20:11] Weaken Restraining Forces To Change Behavior


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (17:34 - 20:11)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/020e7e2b-98cb-4a07-97c0-487cf3b82d00)


**Weaken Restraining Forces To Change Behavior**

- Change behavior by weakening restraining forces rather than merely adding pressure.
- Ask ‘Why aren't they doing B already?’ to reveal barriers and remove them.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>I think that's the main insight. You know, when you want to influence somebody's behavior, that's a very big insight. I've always thought that this is the best psychological idea ever, so far as I'm concerned. But it's that when you want somebody to move from A to B in terms of their behavior, you can think of it that there are two ways of doing it. You can push them. Or you can ask the question, why aren't they doing B already? Which is an unusual question. So then when you ask why not, why aren't they doing B as they think they ought to, then you get a list of what's got to win. That's the psychologist who, my guru on this, my hero, and many people's hero. He spoke of restraining forces. I mean, so there are reasons why they're not where you want them to be. So he spoke of behavior as an equilibrium. There are forces that are pushing you one way, forces that are pushing you the other way. So how loud you speak, how fast you drive. It's easy to think of it as an equilibrium. And what we tend to do when we want to move people from A to B is we push them. We add to the driving forces. And Kurt Lewin's insight was that this is not what you should do. You should actually work on the restraining forces and try to make them weaker. And that's a beautiful point. And he showed, he had that image that I've had since I was an undergraduate. And I'm not sure actually whether it was his image or something that I drew from reading him. But it's like you have a plank and it's being held by two sets of springs. You know, you want it to move one direction. And so you could add another spring that would push it that way, or it could remove one of the springs that are holding it back. And the interesting thing, and that's the striking outcome, is when it moves, if it moves because of the driving force, you've added to the driving force, then at equilibrium, it will Be in a higher state of tension than it was originally. That is because you've compressed one spring and such pushing back harder. But if you remove a restraining force at equilibrium, there'll be less tension on the system. I must have been 20 years old. I thought that's just so beautiful.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [21:27] Behavior Is More Situational Than Dispositional


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (20:20 - 21:27)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/4bf21672-3dcb-4de4-9b9c-38ad1bcf3d66)


**Behavior Is More Situational Than Dispositional**

- Behavior often reflects situational pressures more than personality traits.
- Looking at the situation increases empathy and reduces judgmental conclusions.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Class two, which is a development from class one, you know, it's the same idea extended. Class two is that behaviors don't necessarily reflect the personality, but behaviors have a lot to do with the situation. And so if people behave in strange ways, look at the situation they're in and what are the pressures in the situation that make them act as well. So there is a bias that the social psychologists, well-known social psychologists, call the fundamental attribution error. And that means that when you see people acting in some way, you think that it's because of their personality that they do it. That may not be the case. It's quite likely that the situation is making them do it. I'd like people to know that motivation is complex and that people do good things for a mixture of good and bad reasons and they do bad things for a mixture of good and bad reasons. And I think that there is a point to educating people in psychology is to make them less judgmental.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [22:14] Motivation Complexity


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (21:08 - 22:21)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/3187c3a1-a727-4b5f-9b25-566f215b0b3c)


**Motivation Complexity**

- Daniel Kahneman suggests people do good and bad things for a mix of reasons. 
- Educate people in psychology to make them less judgmental, cultivate empathy, and practice patience.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>I'd like people to know that motivation is complex and that people do good things for a mixture of good and bad reasons and they do bad things for a mixture of good and bad reasons. And I think that there is a point to educating people in psychology is to make them less judgmental. Just have more empathy and more patience, and being judgmental doesn't get you anywhere.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>When you talk about situational, one of the things that comes to mind is it's so easy for us to give our friends advice. But if we were in that situation, we might not necessarily see it. Why is that the case? Why is it so much easier to give other people advice?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>I mean, feelings get in the way of clear thinking. There is a phenomenon that we call the endowment effect, which is that when I'd ask for more money to sell you my sandwich than I'd pay to get it. I mean, that's essentially the endowment effect. And our explanation of it, there are many explanations, but a story I like to tell about it is that it's more painful to give something up than to get something. But there is an interesting result that if you have an agent making decisions on</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [26:03] Beliefs Come From People, Not Pure Reason


[🎧 Play snip - 33sec️ (25:30 - 26:03)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e4049a71-6988-4e02-8d2a-821aac0aac4e)


**Beliefs Come From People, Not Pure Reason**

- Independent clear thinking is rare because beliefs form from trusted people more than from reason.
- Emotions and preexisting commitments obstruct impartial belief formation.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>You know, what gets in the way of clear thinking is that we have intuitive views of almost everything. So as soon as you present a problem to me, I have, you know, I have some ready-made answer. And what gets in the way of clear thinking are those ready-made answers, and we can't help but have them. So that's one thing that gets in the way. Emotions get in the way. And I would say that independent clear thinking is, to first approximation, impossible.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [30:35] Prefer Algorithms Over Intuition


[🎧 Play snip - 37sec️ (29:57 - 30:35)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/489b82b5-cbf3-4263-9a77-3d697dd634e7)


**Prefer Algorithms Over Intuition**

- Use algorithms to replace human judgment wherever possible because they reduce error and variability.
- When algorithms aren't feasible, slow down and apply structured procedures to judgments.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>I would love to.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>If you really want to improve the quality of decision-making, use algorithms. I mean, wherever you can, if you can replace judgments by rules and algorithms, they'll do better. There's big social costs to trusting, allowing algorithms to make decisions, but the decisions were likely to be better. So that's one thing. If you can't use algorithms, then you slow yourself down. And then there are things that you can do for certain types of problems, and there are different types of problems.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [34:24] Israeli Army Interview System That Worked


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (31:24 - 34:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/d7faf320-9111-4048-bde2-d4afd6bb9330)


**Israeli Army Interview System That Worked**

- Kahneman described creating a six-trait structured interview in the Israeli army that improved prediction of soldier performance.
- Interviewers rated traits independently, then closed their eyes and gave a final intuitive score that added useful information.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>So that's a long time ago, like 63 years ago. I was a psychologist in the Israeli army, and I was assigned the job of setting up an interviewing system for the army. That's ridiculous, but, you know, this was the beginning of the state of Israel, so people were improvising all over the place. So I had a BA, and I was, I think I was the best trained psychologist in the army. My boss was a chemist. Brilliant. But anyway, and the existing system was one where people would interview and try to form an intuitive global image of how well that recruit would do as a combat soldier, which was the Object of the interview. And because I had read a book about Paul Neal, I took a different talk. And the different talk was I identified six traits that I sort of made up, and I had them ask questions and evaluate each of these traits independently and score it and write down the score, Then go on to the next trait. And they had to do it for all six traits. That's all I asked them to do. And the interviewers, who are about one year younger than I, all recruits, but very, very smart, selected for being good at it. They were furious with me. And they were furious with me because they wanted to exercise their intuition. And I still remember that one of them said, you're turning us into robots. So I compromised with them. And I said, okay, you do it my way. And I told them, you try to be reliable, not valid. You know, I'm in charge of validity. You be reliable, which was pretty arrogant, but that's how I presented it. But then when you're done, close your eyes and just put down a number of how good a soldier is that guy going to be. And when we validated the results of the interview, it was a big improvement on what had gone on before. But the other surprise was that the final intuitive judgment added it was good. It was as good as the average of the six straights and not the same. It added in formation. So actually, we ended up with a score that was half, was determined by the specific ratings, and the intuition got half the way. And that, by the way, stayed in the Israeli army for well over 50 years. I don't know whether it's, I think it probably, some version of it was still being forced, but around 15 years ago, I visited my old base and the commanding officer of the research unit Was telling me how they ran the interview. And then she said, and then we tell them, close your eyes. So that had stayed for 50 years.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [39:57] Judgment as Measurement


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (38:23 - 39:58)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/691fe932-27d8-4f05-ab29-12786f39f3e8)


**Judgment as Measurement**

- Judgment integrates information informally into a score, akin to measurement using your mind as the instrument.
- Because it's informal, expect variability and differences in people's judgment.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>A bit to separate. And judgment is what you do when you integrate a lot of information informally into a score of some kind. We speak, we being my co-authors and I in the book we're writing. We speak of judgment as measurement, but it's measurement where the measuring instrument is your mind. But you do it informally, and because you do it informally, people are not necessarily going to agree. So wherever we say it's a matter for judgment, we're allowing for differences, for variability. Now, judgment can be more or less slow, more or less systematic. So at one end, you have pure intuition where you allow the judgment to go very quickly and so on. And at the other end, you try to delay intuition. But ultimately, if you're making it by judgment, you're going to have a judgment and it's going to be like an intuition and you're going to go with it. So the more or less deliberate judgment, intuition is always involved at one point or another.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>You're either sort of like listening to it or fending it off.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Yeah.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>And our recommendation is fend it off. Are there ways to judge the quality of somebody's judgment? Oh, sure. I mean, some of them would be unique to the actual scenario, but what are the sort of other ways that we could?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Well, I mean, you may require people to explain their</blockquote>
</details>

### [40:37] Judge Quality By Explaining Judgments


[🎧 Play snip - 42sec️ (39:54 - 40:37)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/2e729bd7-c155-4bfb-ba4a-94c9bdc889c2)


**Judge Quality By Explaining Judgments**

- Require people to explain judgments and evaluate explanations for logic and evidence use.
- Assess whether conclusions were formed before evidence or influenced by wishes.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>I mean, you may require people to explain their judgments and evaluating the quality of the explanation is, you know, whether it's logical, whether it uses the evidence, whether it Uses all the evidence, whether it is strongly influenced by wishes, whether the conclusion was reached before the judgments supposedly is made. You know, there are lots of ways for judgment to fail that can be recognized. So it's harder to recognize very good judgment, but it's really easy to see, you know, what goes wrong. And there are quite a few ways for judgment to go wrong.</blockquote>
</details>

### [44:02] Reject Anchors, Reset Negotiation Frames


[🎧 Play snip - 59sec️ (43:03 - 44:02)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/55e41c53-c341-408f-9449-8273ac850028)


**Reject Anchors, Reset Negotiation Frames**

- Recognize anchoring in negotiations and refuse to accept absurd opening numbers.
- Respond forcefully to reject unreasonable anchors and reset the negotiation frame.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>So you're going to negotiate a price with somebody. They start very high, and that has an effect. So you know, or you should know, that the person who moves first in a negotiation has an advantage. Because the first number changes everybody's view of what is considered plausible. So it moves things in that direction. That's a phenomenon. People can learn that, and they can learn to resist it. So when I was teaching negotiations, I would say somebody does that to you, comes up with a number that's absurd. I would say, lose your temper, make a scene. Say, I will not start the conversation from that number. It's an absurd number. I don't want to erase that number. So that's something that, you know, you can improve if you recognize it.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:02:59] Noise: Useless Variability In Judgment


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:00:20 - 01:02:59)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/ac9e2330-ac05-4269-a9d4-e044396a6de0)


**Noise: Useless Variability In Judgment**

- Noise is the useless variability across judgments that organizations underestimate.
- Wherever judgment exists, there is often far more noise than executives expect.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>I can really explain it by saying what, you know, was the beginning of it, and which was a consulting assignment in an insurance company, where we had the idea of running a test to see whether People in a given role, who are supposed to be interchangeable, with each other. So, you know, when you come to an insurance company and an underwriter gives you a premium, the underwriter speaks for the company. And so you expect that any underwriter, that it doesn't matter which underwriter you get to afford a premium. And the company has that expectation. It shouldn't make much difference. So we tested that and they constructed some cases and then we had some like 50 underwriters assess a premium for the case. With the same information? Yeah, with a really very realistic, we didn't construct it. They constructed the case, and they conducted the experiment. But now, the interesting question is, how much variation do you expect there to be? So, we asked the executives the following question. Suppose you take two underwriters of random. By what percentage do they differ? I mean, you look at the difference between the premium, divide that by the average premium, what number do you get? And people expect 10%. By the way, it's not only the executives in that company, for some reason, people expect 10%. And it was roughly 50%, 5-0. So that's, you know, that's what made me curious about noise. That and the fact that the company was completely unaware that it had noise. It took them completely by surprise. So now we're writing a book because there's a lot of noise. So wherever a rule is that wherever there is judgment, there is noise and more of it than you think. So that's the pattern.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Are there procedures to reduce noise? And conversely, it strikes me that the variation would be good, but maybe only in an evolutionary concept.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Well, we call that noise is useless variability. I mean, variability can be very useful if you have a selection mechanism and some feedback. So evolution is built a variability, but of course it's useful. But noise among underwriters is useless. There's nothing. Nothing gets learned. There's no feedback. It's just noise. And it's costly.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:04:34] Train Scales To Cut Noise


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:03:23 - 01:04:34)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/05c26356-6e5b-40f3-9bf8-048d3df6aed4)


**Train Scales To Cut Noise**

- Reduce noise by training people to share reference frames and use calibrated scales.
- Use frame-of-reference training and probability scales to align judgments.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>So that's the best that we can do. And there is one very important aspect that I haven't mentioned, and this is training people in what the scale is. So there is one piece of advice that you'd have for underwriters, that they should always compare the case to other cases. And if possible, if you can have them share the same frame of reference with other underwriters, you're going to cut down on the noise. Oh, that's a clever idea, yeah. So controlling the scale. And that exists in human resources, where performance evaluation, which is one of the scandals of modern commerce, how difficult it is, but performance evaluation, they have the Thing that's called frame of reference training, which is teaching people, you know, how to use the scale. There's a lot of variability in the scale. And a part of what the super forecasters do, they make judgments in probability units, and they teach them to use the probability scale.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:06:05] Why Psychologists Overclaim Effects


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:04:46 - 01:06:05)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/a120ce94-4505-4062-a622-6ec7ee48780f)


**Why Psychologists Overclaim Effects**

- Replication failures forced Kahneman to be cautious about spectacular social-psychology findings.
- Psychologists overestimate effects because within-subject intuitions don't map to between-subject reality.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Oh, a lot.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Anything big?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Daniel Kahneman</b><br/><br/>Yeah. There's been a replication crisis in psychology, and some of the stuff that I really believed in when I wrote Thinking Fast and Slow, some of the evidence has been discredited. So I've had to change my mind. What are the, what's the biggest? Some of the sexiest stuff, priming and unconscious priming, and so just hasn't held up in replication. And I believed it, and I wrote it as if it were true, because, you know, the evidence suggested it. And in fact, I thought that you had to accept it because that was published evidence. And I should have, I blame myself for having been a big gullible. That is, I should have known that you can publish things even if they're not true. But I just didn't think that through. So I changed my mind. I'm now much more cautious about spectacular findings. I mean, very recently, I've come, I think I have a theory about why psychologists are prone or social scientists generally are prone to exaggerate, to be over about their hypotheses.</blockquote>
</details>



---



# Small Town Billionaire: John Bragg's 3 Empires [Outliers]

<summary>Show notes</summary>
> One man controls half the world's wild blueberries, built North America's largest private telecom, and did it all without ever leaving his hometown of 1,100 people. In this episode, we decode the counterintuitive playbook of patient capital, rural advantage, and why Bragg's refusal to sell a single share made him unstoppable. My  interview with John (#204)  was the class. This is the homework.   This episode is for informational purposes only. ### [15:37] Bragg's R&D Sharing


[🎧 Play snip - 47sec️ (14:55 - 15:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/3e2dfb8c-eb92-42b5-9043-58d58409c3dc)


**Bragg's R&D Sharing**

- John Bragg shares his blueberry R&D findings with competitors because he believes that what's good for the industry is good for everyone. 
- By 1970, he faced a math problem as Oxford needed 8,000 workers for the harvest, highlighting the need for innovation.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>But here's the kicker that'll surprise you. They share all their findings with everyone, including competitors. Why? Well, what's good for the industry is good for everyone. Us and them included, Bragg explained. We all need to grow the industry. And the best way to do that is by helping one another. Like Saul Price in episode 241, sharing his warehouse club innovations with competitors, John Bragg understood the fundamental truth. It's better to grow the pie than fight over the slices. By sharing his R&D with everyone, even competitors, he helped the entire wild blueberry industry expand. When the whole pie grows, everyone's slice gets bigger. By 1970, John Bragg had a math problem. A good hand raker could harvest maybe 500 or 700 pounds of blueberries a day.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [26:08] From Blueberries To Cable In A Night


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (23:27 - 26:08)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/43a1ae91-a5dc-4136-b7b4-deb0083e2584)


**From Blueberries To Cable In A Night**

- While sleeping on his factory floor in 1969, Bragg applied for a cable TV license for Amherst.
- Early cable meant recording shows on tapes and bussing them in, yet he persisted despite heavy monthly losses.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>While he was sleeping on the floor of his empty blueberry factory trying to figure out how to pay the bills, John Bragg quietly applied for a cable television license in Amherst, Nova Scotia. Why would a blueberry farmer want to get into cable TV? The answer to that question would eventually make him a billionaire. Okay, everyone in the Maritimes know how John Bragg got his first cable license. It was 1969. The liberal government is in charge, and they've just swept to power in the country. The Braggs were big-time liberals, so John's father had run for them. John had worked for their campaign, so when a cable license came up, of course John got it. Political connections. That's how business works, right? Here's the embarrassing truth. He was the only one who applied. No one else wanted it. Think about it for a second. The government was literally giving away licenses to print money. At least that's what cable television would eventually become. And nobody wanted them. Not in Amherst anyway. It had a population of 9,000 people. It was in the middle of nowhere. Who's going to make money selling cable television to 9,000 people? But here's John Bragg. His blueberry factory is sitting empty from the June frost. He's sleeping on the factory floor trying to figure out how to pay bills. He's making onion rings for the McCains because he needs the cash. And somehow in the middle of all this chaos, he decides to get into cable television. Why? Because everyone else saw Money Pit, but John saw the future. Now to understand how insane early cable television was, you need to know exactly what John had to do to deliver programming. First, he had to set up antennas near the New Brunswick border. And from there, they'd capture ABC, NBC, and CBS signals from the air. Then, and this is the part that kills me, they record these signals on tapes, physical tapes, and they put the tapes on a bus. And the bus would drive them to Amherst through whatever maritime weather was happening that day and deliver the tapes. And then John's team would play those tapes through their cable system as the programming. So when the people of Amherst watched the Boston Bruins, they were watching a game from like two weeks ago. Sitcoms, two weeks old. News, ancient history. And this was the business model at the time. This is what John Bragg thought would compete with just putting up an antenna on your roof for free. But it gets a little worse. By 1971, Bragg Communications was losing $11,000 every single month. That's about $90,000 in today's money.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [29:29] Pay More To Buy Reputation And Options


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (27:58 - 29:29)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b78ad4d3-e1fd-426d-a9f3-497f21b127ce)


**Pay More To Buy Reputation And Options**

- Bragg paid fair premium prices for acquisitions to build trust and gain future deal flow.
- Reputation accelerates access to scarce assets and reduces friction in consolidation.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Starting in the late 70s, he began swallowing up cable companies across the Maritimes. Year after year, another system joined the growing empire. While everyone else was squeezing sellers for every last dollar, John Bragg did something different. He decided to pay more intentionally. The one thing I never wanted to do is nickel and dime the owners, Bragg explained. I knew we were paying a bit more than the others wanted to pay, but I also knew that we could make it work in the end. This sounds crazy, right? In business, you're supposed to buy low. But Bragg understood something deeper. Every acquisition is really two acquisitions, the company you're buying today and the companies that might sell tomorrow. But there was another reason, too. When I asked John why he paid more for some of these assets, sometimes as high as two times what they were worth, he said something that has stuck with me ever since. It's only available once. It's not always available. Word spread fast in the tight knit community. If you wanted to sell your cable system, call John Bragg. He'll give you a fair price, close quickly, and he won't jerk you around. And one story captures this perfectly. A seller was in serious financial trouble, which became obvious during negotiations. And Bragg could have crushed him. He could have used his advantage and renegotiated. He could have taken advantage of this guy. Instead, he stuck to the original deal.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [29:47] John Bragg's Acquisition Strategy


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (28:15 - 29:51)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b494f1b8-f0b4-4603-be7e-cb31b3b30652)


**John Bragg's Acquisition Strategy**

- John Bragg intentionally paid more for cable companies, understanding that every acquisition is also about future opportunities.
- By being fair and quick, his reputation ensured he got the first offer on cable systems coming to market.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>He decided to pay more intentionally. The one thing I never wanted to do is nickel and dime the owners, Bragg explained. I knew we were paying a bit more than the others wanted to pay, but I also knew that we could make it work in the end. This sounds crazy, right? In business, you're supposed to buy low. But Bragg understood something deeper. Every acquisition is really two acquisitions, the company you're buying today and the companies that might sell tomorrow. But there was another reason, too. When I asked John why he paid more for some of these assets, sometimes as high as two times what they were worth, he said something that has stuck with me ever since. It's only available once. It's not always available. Word spread fast in the tight knit community. If you wanted to sell your cable system, call John Bragg. He'll give you a fair price, close quickly, and he won't jerk you around. And one story captures this perfectly. A seller was in serious financial trouble, which became obvious during negotiations. And Bragg could have crushed him. He could have used his advantage and renegotiated. He could have taken advantage of this guy. Instead, he stuck to the original deal. That seller became his biggest evangelist, telling everyone the same thing. There's no better person to deal with than John Bragg. This wasn't charity, though. It was strategy. While competitors fought over the last dollar on one deal, Bragg had already moved on to the next. His reputation meant that he got first crack at every cable system coming to market.</blockquote>
</details>

### [32:58] Go Alone When Partnerships Stall


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (31:53 - 32:58)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/cd226674-618d-46c8-8167-9dae6094fefe)


**Go Alone When Partnerships Stall**

- If partners block your strategy, raise the capital and build independently when possible.
- Stay nimble and act faster than bureaucratic incumbents to capture technological shifts.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Shaw Communications was selling all of their Nova Scotia assets. The price tag, $265 million. This wasn't another acquisition. This would give Bragg 80,000 new subscribers overnight. It would make Eastlink the fifth largest cable company in Canada, and it would give him virtually all of Nova Scotia. But $265 million for a private family company in 2001 that refused to sell equity to anyone else? It took 20 years to make the deal work. 20. The banks begged him to go public, sell some equity, share the risk, he refused. He sold everything he could to finance this. He sold his BC blueberry farms. He sold his apartment buildings. He borrowed until, as he put it, we were stretched, we were levered to the hilt. This was the bet of a lifetime, a quarter billion dollars on a cable television in Atlantic Canada. But here's the thing. Bell's rejection turned out to be the best thing that ever happened to Eastlink.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [34:45] Privacy Enables Speedy Innovation


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (33:24 - 34:45)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/499e0f09-8c73-4fbc-aa84-f0939f293fe2)


**Privacy Enables Speedy Innovation**

- Staying private let Eastlink act like a startup and deploy new tech quickly.
- Speed beat scale when implementing telephone-over-cable and bundled services.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>When Eastlink engineers visited Atlanta to see telephone over cable technology, they came back with one recommendation. We should implement it. At the big telecommunications companies, those recommendations would have triggered months of studies, committees, reports. At Eastlink, Bragg gave them $10 million and 30 people and said go. This was the advantage of staying private, staying independent. While Bell was still debating, Eastlink had already taken action. So Bell said no to the partnership. John Bragg borrows $265 million and built a big competitor instead. Eastlink became the first cable company in Canada to offer telephone service over cable, first in North America to bundle internet, phone, and TV into one package, the first to let Customers add or drop channels in real time. And this pattern has never changed. Bragg was spotter technology shift coming, his team would find the best implementation anywhere in the world, and they'd move fast while larger companies were still scheduling their First meeting. The telephone over cable breakthrough shows exactly how this worked. Big Canadian telecoms had already burned millions trying to make it work. They gave up. Eastlink succeeded by doing something simple. They adapted existing telephone technology instead of trying to reinvent something new.</blockquote>
</details>

### [40:26] Use Patient Capital, Reinvest Everything


[🎧 Play snip - 33sec️ (39:52 - 40:26)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/07fd0d3d-9157-4483-9ab6-f9dce7f06edf)


**Use Patient Capital, Reinvest Everything**

- Reinvest earnings and avoid dividends to build patient, long-term advantage.
- Use debt strategically to buy assets that pay off over decades, not quarters.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>It's just patient money. John Bragg poured cash into fiber optic cable when nobody understood why. He built infrastructure between small towns when everyone else focused on cities. He lost money for years on bets that wouldn't pay off for decades. He could do this because he never took a dividend. He never cashed out. He never went public. For 50 years, every dollar of profit went back into growth. So while the bigger companies like Bell and Rogers paid billions of dividends to keep shareholders happy, John Bragg kept building.</blockquote>
</details>

That's when John Bragg called. Where others saw dirty work, Bragg saw his favorite setup. It's a fragmented industry. There's high regulatory barriers. There's recurring revenue. And owners who don't think big enough. Owners who aren't looking at the horizon about where things are going. He bought Inland and immediately went shopping, acquiring two other companies right away. Each acquisition followed the Bragg playbook, pay fair, integrate fast, reinvest everything. Today, Inland serves 50 airports across five countries. They've recycled over a billion liters of de-icing fluid. The company has grown tenfold under Bragg's ownership. But you have to ask yourself, why would an 80-year billionaire care so much about airplane de-icing fluid? Well, it's for the same reason he cared about cable television in 1969. He saw where the world was going, stricter environmental rules, more air travel, airports desperate for compliance solutions. Everyone else saw dirty work, but John Bragg saw recurring revenue. In 2012, when Nova Scotia needed wind farms to hit renewable energy targets, a strange partnership emerged.</blockquote>

- Use investing exercises to sharpen capital allocation and competitive analysis skills.

He didn't just invest though, he turned it into a teaching tool. He created six teams from Oxford and Eastlink executives and gave them $10 million of company money and told them to pick stocks and manage a real portfolio. There's no bonuses for good performance and no penalties for losses. This wasn't about making money. It was about educating them. I wanted them to see how strong companies operate, Bragg explained, and more importantly, how weak companies fail. When you have real money at stake, you pay attention differently. You read the annual reports, you study the management decisions, you learn what works and what doesn't. One team nearly tripled their stake while others learned harder lessons. Bragg himself became a student and started attending Berkshire Hathaway meetings, often bringing his maritime friends along.</blockquote>

- Bragg leads by suggestion and presence, traveling to teams instead of commanding remotely.
- Letting people solve problems creates leaders and sustainable change.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>So every month at 6 a.m., John leaves Oxford and drives to Halifax. And, you know, I've made the drive. It's not far, but there's not a lot to look at either. And by 8 a.m., Sharp, he's sitting with his Eastlink executives. After lunch, he's back to Oxford for company meetings and then to Tro for Inland. He goes to all of his companies, not the other way around. The executives know the drill. There's seven updates. Sales, operations, quality complaints, shipments, financials. But Bragg rarely tells anyone what to do. John will suggest something we should consider and leave it at that, says East Link CEO Deborah Schaffner. He wants us to lead. This drives some people crazy. David Hoffman tells one story of Burleigh Crane, who ran John Bragg's main blueberry operations with methods from the 1970s. He was still harvesting by hand when machines were available and outperforming them. So every visit, John would suggest modernizing and Crane would nod, agree, and then go right back to his old ways. The head office begged John to force the change and John just simply said, he'll get there. And Crane did, eventually on his own schedule, of course. The main operations became one of their most efficient.</blockquote>
</details>

### [48:39] Bragg's Leadership Style


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (47:26 - 48:39)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/5d2a2ac2-6938-431b-bc92-e240dc705a98)


**Bragg's Leadership Style**

- Understand that forcing change may create compliance, but giving employees space to find their own way creates leaders. 
- Bragg builds leaders who build companies by suggesting, not commanding, resulting in well-managed, billion-dollar companies.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>This is part of John Bragg's genius. He understood that forcing change might create compliance, but letting people find their own way creates leaders. But don't mistake patience for softness. When beekeeper Jack Hamilton insisted that 2,500 hives was the maximum that one person could manage, John pushed back. I have full confidence you can look after more. Think about how it could be done. Hamilton went away, redesigned the entire process, and came back with a plan. And today, he manages 12,000 hives, five times what he thought was possible. Difference, Bragg didn't tell him how to manage more hives. He just made it clear that the old limit wasn't acceptable. This is what makes all of our outliers amazing. They don't accept the limitations that society puts on things, that people have these preconceived notions about what can and can't be done. And if you look at all the outliers, one of the commonalities so far between all of these people is that they don't accept these limitations. When you tell managers what to do and how to do it, Bragg says, you make them weak leaders. Think about this for a second. John Bragg has three companies on Canada's best managed list. He owns North America's largest telecommunications company.</blockquote>
</details>

### [52:08] Character Multiplies Strategy Over Decades


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (50:50 - 52:08)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/dd591947-c105-43bb-8bc9-7990f084331a)


**Character Multiplies Strategy Over Decades**

- Bragg's thrift and humility underpin scalable success and long-term focus.
- Who you are when nobody's watching shapes enduring company culture and legacy.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>He kept management to a minimum. For years, he was the CEO, he was the head of marketing, he was the CFO, and the HR director all rolled into one. He worked 18-hour days. There was no such thing as work-life balance. He never stopped watching the spending. He never stopped living in the business. 40 years after Bragg started, business researchers documented four principles of enduring success. It turns out that Bragg has been following them all along. First, exploit existing assets before exploring new ones. He did this with wild blueberries on abandoned farmland. Second, diversify your portfolio. East Link, Inland, Wind Farms. Third, remember your mistakes. That June Frost taught him never to depend on one product. Fourth, manage change carefully. He knows exactly when to move fast and when to wait. But here's what the researchers miss. It's not just about following these principles. It's about who you are when nobody's watching. It's about using scuffed golf balls when you could buy the pro shop. It's about sleeping on the factory floor when you could be home in bed. It's about treating the seller who's going bankrupt with the same respect as the one who's thriving. It's about building companies that will outlast you in communities that others have long abandoned, using money you borrowed when everyone said you were crazy.</blockquote>
</details>

We're big, big believers in looking at the horizon, he says. Only those who look at the horizon find the right road. If you look at your feet, you will stumble. How profound is that quote? That license that nobody wanted was the first brick in what would become North America's largest private telecommunications empire. Number four, grow the pie. Bragg's brother invented a blueberry harvester that did the work of 30 handpickers. Instead of keeping it secret, they sold them to competitors. What's good for the industry is good for everyone, Bragg said. He wasn't competing against other blueberry growers. He was competing against every other fruit. This drives people crazy, even today. ### [01:04:25] The Five-Step Production Algorithm


[🎧 Play snip - 8min️ (55:29 - 01:04:25)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/505a9dd6-b219-4f9a-8b73-c87ded657627)


**The Five-Step Production Algorithm**

- Run Elon’s five-step algorithm: question requirements, delete, simplify, speed up cycles, automate last.
- Always validate deletions and avoid automating before requirements are nailed down.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David Senra</b><br/><br/>I'm going to read you my note first. Find the bottleneck. Keep asking why until you get to the root cause. Then delete and simplify. You don't have to be a genius to do this. You just have to constantly apply these ideas over a long period of time. They go against human nature, even for smart people. Our nature is to overcomplicate and go along with the flow. What do I mean by that? Elon noticed the assembly line was being slowed at a station where strips of fiberglass were glued to the battery packs by an expensive but slow robot. I realized that the first error was trying to automate the process, which was my fault because I pushed for a lot of automation, he says. Elon asked a basic question. What the hell are these strips for? The engineering team told him that it had been specified by the noise reduction team to cut down on vibrations. So Elon called the noise reduction team, which told him that the specification came from the engineering team to reduce the risk of fire. It was like being in a Dilbert cartoon, Elon says. So he ordered them to record the sound inside the car without the fiberglass and then with the fiberglass. See if you can tell the difference, he told them. They couldn't. Step one should be to question the requirements, Elon said. Make them less wrong and dumb because all requirements are somewhat wrong and dumb. And then delete, delete, delete. The same approach worked even on the smallest details. For example, when the battery packs were completed in Nevada, little plastic caps were put on the prongs that would plug it into the car. When the battery got to the Fremont factory, the plastic caps were removed and discarded. Sometimes they would run out of caps in Nevada and have to hold up shipment for the batteries. When Elon asked why the caps existed, he was told that they had been specified to make sure the pins did not get bent. Who specified that requirement, he said. The factory team scrambled to find out, but they weren't able to come up with a name. So delete them, Elon said. They did. And it turned out that they never had a problem with bent pins. So I think those two stories are a great example of why he's just saying you have to constantly question requirements. And you're going to be surprised at how much you can delete and simplify. And so as you can imagine, as he's going through production hell, he's also firing a lot of people. And so he repeats himself here. By trying to be nice to people, you're actually not being nice to the dozens of other people who are doing their jobs well and will get hurt if I don't fix the problem spots. So if you remember earlier when we were talking about the fact that the most successful people also have the most failures, and the great thing is you see all these examples of Elon making A decision that he'll come to regret later. So he's constantly learning and updating. So he was obsessed with automation. This is when he starts updating the Elon algorithm and he goes all in on de-automation. And there's probably 300 pages left in the book from where we are. And he's just relentless about this. This is excessively, excessively important to him. One of the most important ideas that he learned, and it came 25 years into his career. So he said, we had this enormously automated production line that used tons of robots. There was one problem. It didn't work. Elon flipped from being an apostle of automation to a new mission that he pursued with similar zeal. Find any part of the line where there was a holdup and see if de-automation would make it go faster. We began sawing robots out of the production line and throwing them into the parking lot. We had to put a hole in the side of the building just to remove all the equipment, Elon said. The experience became a lesson that would become part of Elon's production algorithm. Always wait until the end of designing a process. After you have questioned all the requirements and deleted unnecessary parts before you introduce automation. And so we see another one of his great ideas. You need to have an easy way to see data that allows you to identify a problem. And then once you do, you need to get your ass straight to that problem. So he calls this walk to the red. There was a monitor that showed each station on the assembly line with a green or red light indicating whether it was flowing properly. So Elon was able to walk the floor and hone in on the trouble spots. He called this walking to the red. He would head straight to any red light he saw. What's the problem? A part was missing. Well, who's in charge of the part? Get him over here. A sensor keeps tripping. Who calibrated it? Find someone who could open the console. Can we adjust the settings? Why do we even need that fucking sensor? Elon would then resume his procession through the factory looking for the red lights. And so he's just constantly questioning things and trying to find the limit. Why do we have to have four bolts there? Who set that specification? Can we do it with two? They would say, no, we'll try it. See if it fails. And then on and on and on. He just moves down the line. So this is what I mentioned earlier. You need to be decisive or you're going to be dead. Elon calculated that he made 100 command decisions a day as he walked the floor. At least 20% are going to be wrong, he said. And we're going to alter them later. But if I don't make decisions, we die. He then turns it into a game. Elon started walking the conveyor line, wielding a can of orange spray paint. Go or stay, he'd ask his vice president of engineering. If the answer was go, the robot would be marked with an orange X and workers would tear it off the line. Elon took responsibility. This is another thing he does over and over again. He accepts full responsibility and blame. Elon took responsibility for the over automation. He even announced it publicly. Excessive automation at Tesla was a mistake. To be precise, my mistake. Humans are underrated. And so they're still in production hell. They still have not hit the goal that would keep Tesla alive, which is we have to make 5,000 Model 3s a week. Now, one of the most fascinating things that I've already said once to you, but I think will keep being more and more obvious as we go through this, is that Elon takes lessons from everything Around him. How humans use eyes to drive, how gophers dig holes, how toys are manufactured. He will get countless ideas from science fiction books, from science fiction games and movies. He will also take examples from history. He loves military history and he will apply that to car production. Elon likes military history. He recounted a story about World War II. When the government needed to rush the making of bombers, it set up production lines in the parking lots of the aerospace companies in California. Start building a huge tent in the parking lot, he said. Just three weeks after Elon came up with the idea, the new assembly line was rolling Model 3 sedans out of a makeshift tent in Tesla's parking lot. And then he says one of the best lines in the book describing this, if conventional thinking makes your mission impossible, then unconventional thinking is necessary. A few weeks later, Tesla starts manufacturing 5,000 cars a week. And that brings us to what I think is the most important part of this entire book, Elon's algorithm. At any given production meeting, there is a non-trivial chance that Elon will intone, like a mantra, what</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:00:59] Automate Only As A Last Step

- Avoid automating processes until after questioning requirements, deleting unnecessary steps, and stabilizing the line.
- Be willing to remove robots and reintroduce humans if automation causes holdups.

### [01:05:46] Hands-On Managers And Attitude First


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:04:34 - 01:05:46)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b1df626e-9367-43e3-b0c8-cb9a6c74f697)


**Hands-On Managers And Attitude First**

- Require technical managers to remain hands-on and spend a portion of time doing the work.
- Hire for attitude and hunger; skills are teachable but attitude is not.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David Senra</b><br/><br/>Number one, all technical managers must have hands-on experience. For example, managers of software teams must spend at least 20% of their time coding. Solar roof managers must spend time on the roofs doing installations. Otherwise, they're like a cavalry leader who can't ride a horse or a general who can't use a sword. Number two, camaraderie is dangerous. It makes it hard for people to challenge each other's work. There is a tendency to not want to throw a colleague under the bus. That needs to be avoided. Number three, it's okay to be wrong, just don't be confident and wrong. Number four, never ask your troops to do something you're not willing to do. Number five, whenever there are problems to solve, don't just meet with your managers. Do a skip level where you meet with the level right below your managers. You'll see many times Elon just goes to the people that are actually doing the actual work. Number six, when hiring, look for people with the right attitude. Skills can be taught. Attitude change requires a brain transplant. Seven, a maniacal sense of urgency is our operating principle. Eight, the only rules are the ones dictated by the laws of physics. Everything else is a recommendation.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:12:16] Precision Comes From Caring

Like, no fucking way these should take so long, he said. He decreed that they would start having meetings on Starship every night, seven days a week. We are going to go through first principles algorithm every night, requirements and deleting. That's what we did to unfuck the bullshit that was Raptor. How soon, he asked, would it take to get the booster on the launch pad to test the engines? 10 days, he was told. That's too long. This is critical for all human destiny. It's hard to change destiny. You can't do it from nine to five. And so this idea that he's going to move into where the problem is, he's going to be one doing the meetings every 24 hours. He talks about the fact that being a frontline general is key to being effective. And this is what he says. And I think that's one of the things I most admire about him.</blockquote>

### [01:32:32] Enforce Engineering-Led Product Changes


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:30:16 - 01:32:32)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/3dd2bf75-5faa-4a36-ab4b-f26ff1003e17)


**Enforce Engineering-Led Product Changes**

- When acquiring a company, impose engineering-led product changes and expect quick decisions from leaders.
- Require product managers to understand code and keep product design linked to engineering.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David Senra</b><br/><br/>He takes all these engineers, he kind of like just drops them in from his other companies, and he starts trying to remold the culture to how Elon likes his companies to run. If you could figure out, I think if you're like, hey, can I design a culture that's directly opposite of an Elon company? What would that be? It sounds like it was Twitter. I'm just going to read a couple of this. I wrote, Elon finds this complete opposite company culture. Twitter prided itself on being a friendly place where coddling was considered a Everyone needs to feel safe here, says Leslie Berland, or Berland, who was chief marketing and people Officer. She was fired by Elon. Twitter has instituted, had instituted a permanent work from home option and allowed a mental day of rest each month. One of the commonly used buzzwords at the company was psychological safety. Care was taken not to discomfort. Elon laughed when he heard the phrase psychological safety. It made him recoil. He considered it to be the enemy of urgency and progress. His preferred word was hardcore. Discomfort, he believed, was a good thing. It was a weapon against complacency. Vacations, flower smelling, work-life balance, and days of mental rest were not his thing. And so he starts making changes to the product immediately. He wants the Explore page changed. And so they send a message to the engineer. Of course, the engineer is not in the office. So they send a message to the engineer that was in charge of the Explore page. And he sent back a message saying, I will make that fix when I get to the office on Monday. Do it right now is what he was told. And what was fascinating is this went on for a little bit, but the engineer actually liked this decisiveness. He says, we had worked on many possible new features for years, but no one ever made decisions about them. And suddenly we had this guy making rapid decisions. And so then Elon immediately starts implementing that core belief that you cannot separate engineering from product design, that product design should be driven by the engineers. And he wants his product managers to understand code. He says product managers who don't know anything about coding keep ordering up features they don't know how to create. This is like Calvary generals who don't know how to ride a horse. You must stay as close to the actual work as possible. Do not separate yourself from the pain of your decisions.</blockquote>
</details>

### [06:52] Berkshire's Earnings and Cash Hoard

**Berkshire's Earnings and Cash Hoard**

- Berkshire Hathaway's operating earnings dropped significantly due to natural disasters and foreign exchange losses.
- Despite this, the company maintains a massive cash hoard, signaling readiness for opportunities or market flexibility.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Mike Santoli</b><br/><br/>Closing an all time high yesterday. Get you up to speed on those numbers coming into the meeting. We we were looking for a handful of things. The stock, of course, sitting at an all time high. The B shares up nearly 20 percent this year. The earnings this morning are showing a bigger drop in operating results than perhaps expected. There was a pretty significant drop in insurance underwriting earnings year over year. And the surge in the value of the Japanese yen led to foreign exchange losses. That's kind of a mark-to thing. Also, the equity portfolio did take a $5 billion, also accounting loss. It's not really relevant because it just says stocks were down as of March 31st on a year-to basis. Now, overall, the headline is that earnings did come in well below expectations. I would say kind of steady as she goes in the operating businesses, along with that slight decline in insurance underwriting. In the release, Berkshire said, our periodic operating results may be affected by the impact of ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical events. The release also said the pace of changes in these events, including international trade policies and tariffs, has accelerated in 2020.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Becky Quick</b><br/><br/>And I have to say that is the question that I received most from shareholders who have been writing in, hoping to get questions in front of Warren Buffett and G. Jane and Greg Abel for this, that tariffs was front and center. More questions on that than anything else.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Mike Santoli</b><br/><br/>Yeah. Yeah. It's certainly the biggest swing factor that's shown up in the last several weeks that could impact them. Also notable, Berkshire did not list the value of its five largest holdings as of March 31st. Of course, they listed the stocks. They are the same top five. American Express, Apple, Bank of America, etc. However, it would appear there's no material change in the amounts in terms of buying or selling.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Becky Quick</b><br/><br/>One thing that is notable that we've been watching is just that cash board. Did he put any of that money to work before March 31st? And the answer is no, that cash board actually grew. If you're looking at the numbers at the end of 2024, it was $334.2 billion. Now at the end of the first quarter, it's $347.7 billion. And that's substantial. I mean, it's a small buildup from where we were at the end of the fourth quarter, but it is a</blockquote>
</details>

#### 📚 Transcript

If, in fact, overseas, you know, a lot of what you see in terms of maybe backing away from American brands, even bears on Apple at all?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Steve Kovach</b><br/><br/>Yeah, two things. Well, first, let's talk about American demand real quick, because this was really interesting. Are we going to see people rushing out buying iPhones ahead of time? Tim Cook told me he saw no evidence of that, at least in the March quarter, that people were kind of anticipating and buying ahead of time and therefore implying the growth we saw in iPhone Was organic demand. This was, you know, forget tariffs. This was people were just excited to buy the new phones. They had a new model that launched. But you're totally right, Mike, to point out the anti-American sentiment. We've been seeing this for a number of years, even before President Trump initiated these tariffs, that Huawei came back online and started making phones again a couple years ago. And just quarter over quarter, year over year, Huawei continues to eat into the market share in China. China's sales were down to 16 billion. Now, the growth rate is not as bad as it was before. It was still negative 2 percent in China year over year, not as bad as that negative 11 that we saw in the December quarter. But still, China is way down from its peak. And part of that, at least, is that negative sentiment or this nationalistic view that we have to buy these homegrown brands like Oppo and Huawei. So that's just a trend that we're going to continue to see. And then the question becomes, what exciting thing can Apple do over in China to get those customers excited? Some people are saying that might mean a new kind of phone design, those foldable phones that those customers typically like. And then, of course, there's artificial intelligence. We know that big mess that they had in this past quarter, delaying that big Siri AI update. That caused a lot of analysts to reduce their expectations for iPhone sales. And in China, they love specs. They love AI. They love cool technical things like that. And when Apple can't offer it, but one of those Chinese brands can, that's why we see so many people gravitate to that, Mike.</blockquote>

- Sei authentisch und arbeite an deinen Stärken, nicht an fremden Vorstellungen.

<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Rob Fraser talks about the mindset required to excel in both elite sports and business. He shares how a personal mission fueled his resilience through injuries and setbacks in cycling and how the same perseverance has driven him in entrepreneurship. We dive into the lessons he's learned from failure, the importance of long-term thinking, and how competitive sports taught Rob skills like preparation, endurance, and performing under pressure–skills he now uses everyday to build his business.  Rob Fraser is the Founder and CEO of OUTWAY socks and Custom Lab. He is a five time Team Canada Cyclist. ### [46:15] Work-Life Balance for Entrepreneurs


[🎧 Play snip - 22sec️ (45:55 - 46:17)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/553076e5-4360-47c5-89bf-82fd76125be8)


**Work-Life Balance for Entrepreneurs**

- Rob Fraser argues that "work-life balance" isn't relevant for entrepreneurs.
- Instead, health and relationships are part of the "work" needed for peak performance.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Rob Fraser</b><br/><br/>Right. And that's by definition, no balance. However, I think what gets missed here is that part of the work for an entrepreneur, like an athlete is like your health and your personal relationships. If those aren't in a good spot, you're not operating at 100% efficiency. And so that's part of the work, right? What's not part of the work is maybe partying or doing things that you shouldn't be doing that are going to set you further back.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:32:58] Savor the Journey


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:31:27 - 01:32:55)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0450e97a-b723-4f5a-8eb8-71f787ed2c1f)


**Savor the Journey**

- Rob Fraser didn't appreciate his experiences traveling the world as a cyclist because he was too focused on results.
-  He now realizes he'd be more fulfilled if he had 'smelt the roses' and enjoyed the journey.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Rob Fraser</b><br/><br/>And a good example of this is when I was cycling, I got to travel the world and see all these incredible places. I was so intense and so focused that I would not enjoy anywhere. I'd be in Spain or Italy, and I would be so tunnel visioned that I didn't even give myself time to appreciate where I was. And I always told myself, I'll be back. I'll appreciate it later. I haven't been back. It just ended. And I look back, I'm like, I think I would be a lot more fulfilled if I actually just smelt the roses. Because the results are one thing, but the experiences and the opportunities to be there. And we always look back and we don't talk about the results. We talk about the experiences and the journey. And unfortunately I just like, didn't take time to appreciate the journey. And so I'm really trying to do that now. And you know, it's just way more fulfilling to be like, we're in it. One of my favorite quotes is one of my favorite shows, the office. And it's like the final episode and Andy says, uh, he's kind of reminiscing. He's like, um, wish someone told us we were in the good times, like in the good old days, right? He's like, they're looking back and they're like, I wish they would tell you before it ended. And I'm kind of getting goosebumps talking about it. It's like, I'm in the good old days right now, right? Like I know when this business is over as tough as it is or whatever we're going through, even like raising young children. I try to remind myself, like, these are the good old days. Like another thing I read, it was like, you know, like it can be hard to take your dog. I have a dog, hard to take them out in the rain, but when they're gone, how much would you pay to take that dog for a walk right now?</blockquote>
</details>

# Todd Herman: Unleashing Your Secret Identity


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fmegaphone.imgix.net%2Fpodcasts%2F9c0d00ea-df1d-11ef-9477-2b8336fcd8e9%2Fimage%2F113a058265322df92ae562c548d1dc8f.jpg%3Fixlib%3Drails-4.3.1%26max-w%3D3000%26max-h%3D3000%26fit%3Dcrop%26auto%3Dformat%2Ccompress&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: Todd Herman: Unleashing Your Secret Identity
- Show: The Knowledge Project
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/a2fef7e1-d0df-46ab-908e-71c2af3c7aeb)
- Episode publish date: 2023-11-28
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> We all inhabit different identities throughout our day. Perhaps we’re entrepreneurs or employees, mothers or fathers, athletes or CEOs. But how can you harness the strengths of these different identities to get the best out of yourself? And can these different identities be used to get through tough times? Todd Herman calls on more than two decades of experience working with top performers on performance, strategy, mindset, and execution to discuss his thoughts on peak performance, the value of patience, the fear that prevents us from performing our best, imposter syndrome, and how he worked with Kobe Bryant to build the legendary alter-ego of The Black Mamba.   Herman has worked with elite athletes, peak performers, and entrepreneurial leaders for over 22 years. He helps them achieve their most ambitious goals by becoming more resilient, creative, confident, and courageous. He is also the author of the bestselling book  The Alter Ego Effect .   -- <br/>>    Want even more?  Members get early access, hand-edited transcripts, member-only episodes, and so much more. Learn more here:  https://fs.blog/membership/ <br/>>   Every Sunday our Brain Food newsletter shares timeless insights and ideas that you can use at work and home. Add it to your inbox:  https://fs.blog/newsletter/ <br/>>   Follow Shane on Twitter at:  https://twitter.com/ShaneAParrish <br/>>    Our Sponsors: <br/>>    MetaLab : Helping the world’s top companies design, build, and ship amazing products and services.  https://www.metalab.com <br/>>    Aeropress:  Press your perfect cup, every time.  https://aeropress.com <br/>>    Vanta:  Helping you get compliance-ready, fast.  https://www.vanta.com/ <br/>> <br/>>   <br/>>  Learn more about your ad choices. Visit  megaphone.fm/adchoices
</details>

## Episode AI notes


1. The true nature of hope has a dual nature - it can be beneficial as a safety net, but relying solely on hope without taking action prevents progress and growth.
2. Success for knowledge workers and athletes lies in simplifying life and focusing on what one is good at to increase value and achieve success.
3. Embracing different roles and identities allows individuals to develop wholeness by uncovering latent traits and qualities, leading to a more holistic human experience.
4. Successful individuals leverage rituals and creative imagination for performance enhancement, including the use of imagery and visualization.
5. Maintaining a balance between celebrating successes and setting new goals is crucial to avoid complacency and continue progressing.

### [11:31] Importance of Patience and Long-Term View in Success


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (09:56 - 11:34)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/ff698350-aa44-4247-b625-61b0136ab535)




### ✨ Summary
Success is more about avoiding stupid mistakes than making brilliant choices, as exemplified by the impact of a subtle mistake like drunk driving. Patience acts as a powerful force, compelling action and driving a long-term perspective. Viewing entrepreneurship as a career emphasizes the need for skill-building and a long-term approach, in contrast to the common short-sighted view.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Todd Herman</b><br/><br/>Who's a hero to both of us, Charlie Munger and Buffett both would talk about how success has a lot more to do with not making stupid mistakes than it ever does in making phenomenally right Choices. Yeah. You know, and the easiest example is if I'm out drinking with you, Shane, me getting into the car and driving is a, could be just a subtle, stupid mistake. Nothing ends up happening, or it could be an irrevocably bad decision that ruins or, you know, causes a lot of people harm. So yeah, I think patience is, um, it can be a great forcing function because it drives immediate need right now and can force someone to finally get up off their ass and do something. Or this is actually something that might resonate with you too. Shane, like I stand on stage in front of like a lot of entrepreneurs. And when I talk about my business life in building businesses and whatnot, I talk about my career, which isn't something that entrepreneurs typically talk about. They don't talk about their career. And so I've had people ask me, like when they come to the mic stand afterwards, I'm like, why'd you use career? Like entrepreneurs, that's just a, I never heard an entrepreneur say that. And I say, because I look at entrepreneurship as a career. And when you look at something as a career, then you look at it as, well, what are the skills that I need to build? And career isn't something that's to your point, an impatient two week timeframe. It's a very long view of things. So I take a look at that world a lot differently than I think many other entrepreneurs do and</blockquote>
</details>

### [19:02] The True Nature of Hope


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (17:48 - 19:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/5dc13a18-7d2c-4414-8cf8-be5d478c626c)




### ✨ Summary
Many people do not confront their true desires as pursuing them would mean losing the comfort of hope. Hope, like everything in nature, has a dual nature - it can be both beneficial and detrimental. It serves as a safety net, providing comfort when dreams remain unactioned. However, relying solely on hope without taking action prevents progress and growth.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Todd Herman</b><br/><br/>They're and they know And I think most people really don't sit down and be really honest with themselves about what it is that they truly want to be doing. And one of the reasons that they don't is because if you, Shane, were to actually pursue the thing you want and start taking action on it, you're losing the excuse of hope. I think hope is a double-edged sword, like everything in nature. Something can't only be good because nature tells us that that doesn't work. Hyperhidrosis and hypohidrosis. Water isn't always good. When you and I are floating in the middle of the ocean, the last thing I want is more water unless it's rainwater to help me fuel my body. And hope is the same way. Hope can't only be good because if I'm hoping that, you know, maybe someday I'm going to be an actor in Hollywood and that's always just a dream in my mind, but I never take action on it. It's actually a place of safety and security because the moment I take action on it, I no longer have that warm blanket of hope that someday, maybe I'll be able to go and do it. And</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [35:21] Success in Prioritization and Simplification


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (34:03 - 35:23)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/be3367e3-1b40-4be4-9ea5-d334f9b24555)




### ✨ Summary
Success for knowledge workers and athletes lies in removing distractions and subtracting non-essential commitments. By simplifying one's life and focusing on what one is good at, individuals can increase their value and usefulness. The process involves eliminating priorities, reducing surface area of responsibilities, projects, and commitments to enhance performance and achieve success.


---

Projects you want to take on, of commitments that you have, of people reaching, the more successful you are, the more people reach out to you. How much of developing performance is getting reps in what you're good at, which means subtracting all of these other things. So getting rid of priorities, simplifying your life and not letting the surface area continuously expand. Well, it's undeniable that that's a major part of that process of being more valuable.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Todd Herman</b><br/><br/>Because we were talking before about success, and I don't like that term. I think in the terms of being useful and being valuable, the useful you are, the more valuable you are, then whatever success is going to happen is going to happen, I think. But when I'm working with people, we subtract, we remove, and we delete so</blockquote>

### [01:36:35] Fending Off Complacency

The challenge lies in how to avoid complacency after achieving success. Often, individuals reach their goals and feel satisfied with the accomplishment, leading to a lack of motivation and a sense of having 'made it.' This contentment can hinder further progress or growth, as individuals may become distracted by enjoying their achievements instead of striving for more. It is crucial to maintain a balance between celebrating successes and setting new goals to continue progressing and avoid becoming stagnant.

# #215 David Heacock: Managing and Marketing a $250M Business


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #215 David Heacock: Managing and Marketing a $250M Business
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/5a9bb131-6af6-403b-8f62-dc399775e4de)
- Episode publish date: 2025-02-13
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> David Heacock felt the entrepreneurial pull from day one. At 29, he left his million-dollar Wall Street salary to bet on air filters. That bet transformed into Filterbuy, now a $250 million direct-to-consumer manufacturer serving more than 7 million customers through a ruthlessly efficient operation. Today we talk about what actually matters when building a business, balancing obsession with family life, selling on Amazon, what he’d do differently if starting over, and the freight decision he calls his biggest mistake. Whether you’re starting a business, scaling one, or simply looking for insights on hiring, managing, or making bold decisions, David shares the lessons that helped him build his empire.  David Heacock is the founder and CEO of Filterbuy. Before revolutionizing the air filter industry, he traded options at Goldman Sachs from 2005-2012.  Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business. Sign up here at  fs.blog/newsletter    Follow Shane on twitter at:  twitter.com/ShaneAParrish   Access your transcript today:  https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/david-heacock-215/   <br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24

- It's crucial to build the necessary systems yourself, especially when small, as hiring someone to do it is nearly impossible.

And at least that has been my experience. And I think that it sounds great to say, hey, I'm just going to go and hire somebody to do all of these things. But the reality is that if you really want</blockquote>
</details>
