### [49:10] Brand Building Focus


[🎧 Play snip - 5min️ (43:42 - 49:10)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/c475f7d8-6c02-4e12-a339-2003665ef35d)


**Brand Building Focus**

- Heacock prioritizes brand building over short-term marketing gains. 
-  He focuses on creating a strong brand identity and customer experience, believing this leads to long-term success.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>So let's talk about marketing. What does that look like? Is it Amazon, Google? I mean, I would assume people have intent when they're buying a filter. How do you think about marketing and branding? Yeah.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>So branding and marketing are two very different things to me. And we can go as deep as you'd like on it. But I actually would like to start with the brand a little bit in that I, from the very beginning, was focused on building a brand. To me, if you look at any big company that stands the test of time or has real value, it's really the brand. You know, nobody is searching for – there are people that are searching for shoes, and there are people that are searching for Nike. You want to own the company that is Nike, because then people, you have no competition if somebody is searching for Nike. They're only looking for you. If there's somebody searching for shoes, and you have to compete with all those people that are selling shoes, that's a much harder game not likely to be to stand the test of time. And so I recognized that early on, and it was important to me to build a brand. But you don't build a brand overnight. It's much like a reputation. It takes a lifetime to build and it takes a second to ruin, you know, and that's just true. But from the beginning, you know, we really focused on, I really focused on branding. And, you know, there's lots of nuances that go into that. But simple things like how we package all of our products, you know, and like a lot of our competitors, especially early on, didn't do any packaging at all. We're just putting, you know, generic filters into a box. And we've really, you know, gotten more and more much better over the years at really differentiating within our brand. And, like, we've actually rolled out some new media with a partner with Kimberly Clark to do it. And, like, we really kind of innovated and worked to make sure that we have a best-in product that people see, they resonate with it, and ultimately led us to launching in Walmart two And a half months ago, which retail I think is something you may want to double-click on later. Anyways, branding was extremely important to me from the beginning. And marketing to me is anything to raise awareness about that brand. But marketing without a good product or without a good experience is completely wasteful because especially if you're in a high customer acquisition, you know, type business that You need that recurring revenue or that repeat revenue in order to be able to make into a business, you have to have a good product and a good customer experience. So a brand all starts with that, just like a reputation. It's like building that over a long period of time and all the elements that go into that. But then from a marketing perspective, the business originally was built almost exclusively on what I would call intent-based marketing. And so, you know, basically if you search for air filter or any iteration of an air filter, like 20 by 20 by one air filter or 20 by 25 by five air filter or furnace filter or AC filter, HVAC Filter on Google or Amazon or Bing, then, you know, you're going to see us. And that's been the case for about a decade. Early on, my strategy was I wanted to suck all the oxygen out of the market in that I knew we were vertically integrated. And that means that our cost of delivering a product to a customer is as cheap or cheaper than anybody else as possibly could be. That's part of the nuance of our business model because, you know, we are completely vertically integrated. And, you know, we're really in the logistics business, not the air filter business. We manufacture an air filter for about $2 and sell it on average for $12. The, you know, unfortunately, that's not all just profit between the two. There's a lot of logistics involved in getting that product to the end customer. But because we've optimized all of that, you know, I know that there's basically nobody that can compete with us on price if that was a deciding factor that I wanted to go out and compete On. We don't compete on price. It's just not something you don't build a brand that way. But I knew that because of that, I had the kind of staying power from a marketing perspective and an intent-based marketing perspective to suck all the oxygen out of the rest of the market. And I did that even when it was hard early on, but I knew that if you were searching for an air filter or something related, that we needed to be the obvious choice based off of the, you know, Taking over all the ad spots that, you know, would be related to that. And so that was, you know, effectively the marketing strategy that got us to, you know, to where we are today, more or less, right? I mean, that combined with great product, quick delivery, great branding, great quality, you know, all of those things combined with, you know, the sucking all the oxygen out of that Intent-based marketing world is how we built the brand we have today. I mean, you fast forward now, you know, on Amazon, we know because it's, you know, we have access to the data, it's pretty public. Between one in four and one in three filters purchased in the furnace filter category on Amazon is one of ours. So we've really taken a ton of market share</blockquote>
</details>

- Focus on building a brand, not just selling a product.
- People search for brands (like Nike), giving you a competitive edge.

<blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>You know, nobody is searching for – there are people that are searching for shoes, and there are people that are searching for Nike. You want to own the company that is Nike, because then people, you have no competition if somebody is searching for Nike. They're only looking for you. If there's somebody searching for shoes, and you have to compete with all those people that are selling shoes, that's a much harder game not likely to be to stand the test of time. And so I recognized</blockquote>

### [53:24] Be Where Customers Are


[🎧 Play snip - 4min️ (49:10 - 53:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/fed1cc21-7afc-4edd-b639-9d98a9640ba3)


**Be Where Customers Are**

-  Be present where your customers are, even on platforms like Amazon. 
- Heacock argues against ignoring major sales channels, focusing on brand building and customer experience regardless of the platform.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>In that specific category just off of that long-term marketing strategy. How do you think about turning an Amazon customer where you don't get the data yourself or do you? Are you shipping? Are you doing all the logistics? We fulfill 100% of our orders on Amazon. So you get all the customer data.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>I mean, we get their address. I mean, we have to ship it to them. Do you get their email address instead? No, you don't get their email address. But I know the question you're asking, and I think it's the wrong question, and I'll tell you why. If you are a brand, if you're a CPG brand, your goal should be to sell and be wherever your customers are. And I shouldn't care where you buy my product. I just want you to be buying my product. And so like 60% of product searches start on Amazon. That's just a fact. So like if you, you know, are selling a product that you, you know, for whatever reason say, oh, I don't want to sell on Amazon, then you are basically ignoring 60% of your potential buyers. And so like from the beginning and a big motivation for me starting this business and the way that I did is I wanted to build a brand and a product that I could work with companies like Amazon, Rather than trying to compete with them. And when you own a brand and you own all of your manufacturing, then we're in a unique position to be able to do that. And it goes back to that earlier experience I had. I told you in 2009, a long time ago, we sold six and a half or I sold six and a half million dollars of office supplies on primarily on Amazon. Like that was where I saw all these people were buying stuff. And so to me, the opportunity was, you know, I want to find a product that I can sustainably sell on Amazon as a channel because I know that that's where the customers are. And so I think anybody that kids themselves and says, oh, I'm too good to sell on Amazon or I want to ignore it is just incredibly naive.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>I think the question that I was going to go after was more about a lot of people spend a lot of time going, yes, I want to be on the Amazon channel, but then I want to take that person that buys On Amazon and convert them into a direct relationship because with Amazon, I don't own the relationship with the customer. Amazon is the middle person. And for better or worse, and I don't have an opinion on this because I don't sell on Amazon at all. They want to take that and have a direct relationship with that consumer. How do you think about that?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>Early on, I would spend some time thinking about that. And honestly, I realized, at least in our case, and maybe everybody's different, it's a fool's errand. I mean, it's like, there's a reason why customers choose to buy on Amazon. I mean, there's a funny story. She'll maybe get mad at me for telling it, but I will. Our house and my wife actually ordered our air filters on Amazon. And it really irritated me at the time because I said, how in the world, why in the world would you go on Amazon and buy our air filters? But for her, she was just doing a chore and she was looking for the easiest way to, she wanted to solve that problem and we buy a lot of stuff on Amazon. And so she just went and clicked order on Amazon. And so that's my wife that made the decision to go and buy our product on Amazon. So I quite frankly gave up on spending any energy and worrying about it. I'm focused on building our brand and making the best products we can and giving the best customer experience we can. And if people choose to buy on Amazon, then that's great. I don't care where people buy us. Early on, I thought I would never get into retail, to be honest with you. And here I am, we just launched in 505 Walmarts and really have a big retail strategy going forward. 84% of retail transactions still happen in store. So I can sit here and say, oh, I'm just going to sell online because I want to be a pure direct to consumer company. And that's fine. But to me, that is very short-sighted and you're losing a lot of potential customers in that process. I mean, that's the decision that you're making, but you have to at least know that you are making that decision. So that's really how I think about that is I really stop thinking about it. I love all the</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [55:20] Internal Marketing Management


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (53:24 - 55:20)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/35c64029-3bd9-4518-8faa-1b64af895077)


**Internal Marketing Management**

-  Manage most marketing internally, even when using agencies for support. 
- Focus on building top-of-funnel brand awareness through various initiatives like giveaways and public appearances.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Detail. Thank you for this. Keep going with this. Do you outsource the marketing to somebody else or do you have it all internally?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>And is that important and why? It's mostly internal. We do have an agency that we work with that I hired a few years ago that does manage some of our ad accounts that are more on autopilot, but it's just keeping best practices and keeping things Together. I built out all of our intent-based ad campaigns. Our budget next year is $42 million for our marketing in general. So it's a $42 million line item for us, at least going to next year. I think it was $38 million this year. So it's a big line item for us, and we manage most of it internally. But what's so interesting still to me, my marketing team may not like to hear me say it, but it's like most of that money is still built on ad campaigns that I effectively launched 12 years Ago and honed over that period or 10 years ago. In Amazon's case, they rolled out their advertising a bit later, but it's not like those things have shifted all that much. One of my big goals personally, I really am going all in on brand and on building the roles leading indoor air quality company, and we're getting into the service business and doing some Other things. And so for me, the next kind of step of marketing is building top of the funnel brand awareness. And that's part of the reason why I'm going so public now. And then we're going to start alongside me. I'm going to have people doing the same thing for the core filter by brand that may appeal to different demographics than I would appeal to. And we're really kind of going all in on that top of funnel brand awareness. We gave a Jeep away last year. We're doing big giveaways every month. We're doing things that are not related to air filters, but that get us into a customer's mind. That's kind of where our marketing is going. And almost all of that's done in-house.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:17:12] Obsession vs. Willpower


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:15:59 - 01:17:11)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/36b49763-49a6-468c-af14-1fa529af635c)


**Obsession vs. Willpower**

- Obsession fuels success because it eliminates the need for willpower.
- Willpower is not a sustainable strategy; it eventually breaks, while obsession propels you forward.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Eventually, everybody loses the battle with willpower.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>Willpower is not a strategy. That's why going back to your obsession point, the people that are really successful are obsessed because when you're obsessed with something, you don't need willpower because it's All-consuming for you and that's what you do. And, you know, I think that, you know, that's really why people need to lean into things that are more natural for them or interesting for them. It's not that, oh, follow your dreams and everything's going to be great. I think that's awful advice, but you do need to lean into the things that you enjoy and are genuinely curious about. It's not like I enjoy air filters in and of themselves, but I love business and I love the puzzle of that. And there are lots of pieces of that that I can easily become obsessed with that are wrapped up in it. And so I think that figuring out where your natural curiosity is, is super important because that obsession wins, whereas willpower will break inevitably time and time again. I like that. Obsession wins, willpower breaks.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Describe the hardest moment that you went through.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:21:21] The Glue Crisis


[🎧 Play snip - 4min️ (01:17:08 - 01:21:21)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0ac298c9-d9fc-4496-8706-3c74e4fbf667)


**The Glue Crisis**

- A humidity-related glue problem nearly halted Filterbuy's production. 
- Heacock found a last-minute solution by driving overnight to Indiana to purchase a hot glue laminator, demonstrating his resourcefulness.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Describe the hardest moment that you went through. Was there a moment when you wanted to quit and give up? Yeah. I told this story a few times and I'll give you the short version because it's a bit tricky to articulate.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>But I had been working on manufacturing air filters for about three years, maybe two and a half years. And there's a part of the manufacturing process, which is the beginning where you basically laminate or put glue onto like this chicken wire that goes onto the media to give the media The rigidity and the air filter. So it's called the laminator. And when I bought the initial machines, I bought what's called a cold glue laminator, which means like Elmer's glue type consistency that goes on and then it dries. And it's supposed to dry before it gets rolled up into this roll that then goes into the next part of the manufacturing process. Well, there was a May in Alabama when it was super humid and it was super humid in our manufacturing facility. And the glue was not drying. It was not drying in the way it was supposed to because there was so much water in the air. And so we were making all of these components or all of this raw material that's supposed to feed our whole manufacturing process. And the glue was, the rolls were ultimately getting stuck to each other because it hadn't dried properly and it became unusable. And so we were wasting about half of our raw material any given day, which was extraordinarily expensive and also was keeping us from being able to manufacture the product that we needed For our customers. And I was panicking because I didn't know how to solve this problem. What am I supposed to do differently? And I started calling around like desperately and found a company in Indiana that had what's called a hot glue version of this laminator where you use hot glue rather than cold glue to Do it, which I did not even know existed. And by some act of God, they had one on the floor that I've never had that happen since, where I'm able to find a machine that was built to order. But for some reason, they had one on the floor. And I spent like $55,000, probably close to the last 55 grand I had to buy that hot glue laminator. I drove up on a, I rented a 20 foot Penske truck in Anniston, Alabama. I drove to New Albany, Indiana, got there about two o'clock in the morning, woke up, got trained on that laminator all day that Friday, then drove through the night back to Alabama and Early Saturday morning trained the team on that hot glue laminator and that solved all of our laminating problems. And if they had not had that piece of machinery in stock, I do not know that I would have been able to continue manufacturing air filters because that was my breaking point. And I really didn't know how to, how to solve it. But I ultimately found that solution and, and went and figured it out. And actually, you know, you won't be able to see it here, but I actually have a scar on my finger where I almost cut off my finger on that machine, you know, when I was training them as I made A mistake. And I literally almost cut off my finger that Saturday morning when I got back. So I have the scar to remind myself of that experience. But that was really the turning point for us because after that, things started really coming together. But that was two and a half years into my entrepreneurial journey. And I would not have had the financial or, quite frankly, mental staying power to continue to do it if I had not been able to find that solution. But it's also very formative for me in that ever since then, I've been hit with a lot of problems. Some of them seem seemingly bigger than that. I mean, and that's just life. And that's certainly life in business. But I realized as I've gotten older that I've built the muscle of dealing with those problems and, you know, actually almost thriving in those situations because I get hit with something That it's always annoying, but you say, but I have the confidence that I'm going to go and figure out a solution to it.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:29:40] The Freight Business Mistake


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:26:00 - 01:29:40)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/6a3ac248-3ec4-47f4-a3a8-48677b155966)


**The Freight Business Mistake**

- Heacock's foray into the freight business during COVID-19, motivated by tax incentives and demand, resulted in a significant loss. 
- The bigger cost, however, was the distraction from Filterbuy's core business, highlighting the importance of focus.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Decision. What went into that? How did you realize it wasn't working? What did you do when you realized it wasn't working? Oh, you want to rub some salt in my wounds.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>You know, I could actually give you a couple of other examples too, but freight, long story short, in COVID, so again, 2020, freight became very hard to source and it was very expensive Because there was just so much demand to move this stuff. And again, a bit hubristic, I would argue, if I was looking back at it. I thought, well, you know, I'm so good at this business thing, right? How hard could running a freight business be? And I have a lot of demand for freight, so I can just do my own freight and then sell the excess to other people. And also in the U.S. Tax code, as a bit of an aside, there is a section that basically allows for accelerated depreciation. And what that means is that if you bought a million dollars of equipment like trucks, then you could write off that million dollars against your taxes in that year. Oh, wow. And it actually still exists currently this year. You can only do 60% with Trump back in office. My guess is it'll go back up to 100%. But it's something that you don't see talked about a lot. But if I bought a million dollars of trucks, I could effectively borrow a million dollars from a bank because they love to finance against trucks. And you could also, if you had a million dollars of income, you could then take that income effectively to zero and pay zero income tax. That's how accelerated depreciation works. So that's another part of the reason I got sucked into this whole thing. But I thought, oh, if I can do this and operate a freight business, then I found a cheat code here. So that was kind of the origin for me getting into it. And between 2020 and 2021, I bought 50 tractor trailers. I bought 50 trucks and actually probably 60 trailers to go along with it. I don't do things in a small way or I don't make small mistakes. If I make them, I make them big. It's probably $6 or $7 million worth of equipment, more or less, in that neighborhood. We operated them. Never once did we operate them profitably. Even in the beginning, it was an example where the freight business is very cyclical. And I knew that going in. It wasn't like I knew it. I just thought I was smart enough to be able to mitigate it, which is wrong. But it's very cyclical. And, you know, you saw a freight race start to come down pretty quickly in 2022, at the end of 2022, and really into 2023. But even when they were higher, the managing of that business was both a distraction, because, you know, that's a lot of people to manage. We did not have the right infrastructure to manage it and the right teams to manage it. I really bit off more than we as a company could chew while still, we're still growing our business at a really rapid rate, right? And I'm trying to do this as a side. And so ultimately, I liquidated all of those trucks at the end of 2023. All in that probably was a $3 or $4 million mistake on my side. But the money is one thing. The biggest mistake was the lack of focus or the energy that it took in distraction away from the core business, both for me and for people around me that were helping me to manage it. So it cost you</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:44:23] The Importance of Mission

-  Heacock believes purpose, not just material wealth, is key to happiness and a meaningful life.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>I consider myself-private equity. Why do you think we're attracted to strong missions and visions?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>I think that we as human beings need to have a purpose in life and have a reason to wake up every day. And I think that it's the X factor that gets people excited to come to work and to go the extra mile any given day. But I just think it's how we're wired. ### [01:56:29] Combating Lifestyle Creep


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:53:10 - 01:56:29)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/b7cb098b-c422-4a33-b85d-b0c04499e72a)


**Combating Lifestyle Creep**

- Lifestyle creep can lead to a scarcity mindset, hindering risk-taking and future success. 
-  Heacock suggests focusing on adding value and increasing earning potential rather than simply cutting expenses.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Shane Parrish</b><br/><br/>Without mentioning names or anything, is there an example that stands out to you that other people might be able to learn from who are listening to this? A lot of incredibly successful people listen to this.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>David Heacock</b><br/><br/>Yeah, well, the examples that I've seen come back to something we spoke about earlier in the conversation. But when you're relying on other people or a paycheck or one income stream to pay your bills, and then it may be like if you're working in finance and you're making a few million dollars A year, then your lifestyle creeps up to that. And then you wake up one day and maybe, you know, you're not as marketable as you were before and you don't have any other skills and you don't have the muscle of building those new skills. And then you're in trouble. And I know a number of people that have lived that because it's easy to think, oh, this is going to go on forever. And then it inevitably doesn't. And even if you have a lot of money, it goes back to that scarcity mindset. Because you're so afraid of losing it, you can't spend it, you can't invest it, you can't take the risk that you need in order to be able to go out and build that lifestyle again, so to speak. And so most of the people that I've seen that have been impacted have come from like the finance world that, you know, that is now declining as a share of GDP. And so, you know, it's just contracting. There's not as much money as there was before. And then a lot of those people get to a point where then they're not employable at the same level, but they still have that same lifestyle. And even if they have, you know, a decent amount of assets, they're then ironically taking less risk with them because they're afraid of losing it, which creates this whole negative Kind of spiral with that. So that's what I have seen. I think it's easy to have lifestyle creep. I mean, like I look at my, like some, I always talk to my wife, it's like, you know, I, when I travel or go out, it's mostly work related. So like when I look at our personal expenses, like my wife is not a big spender and I'm very thankful for that. But I look at it and these things add up so much. And you're like, how do regular people do this? You know, it's easy for that to happen. And the more successful you are, ironically, the more people take advantage of you. And it's in subtle ways, but it's definitely one of those things that happens. So I understand how, you know, things creep up. But I come at all of these things from a place of abundance. And, you know, I have the confidence to go out that I know I can go out and build what I've got to build. And like, you know, I earn more than I spend. And, you know, and, you know, I really do that through the business and compounding it. So I'm certainly fortunate. But, you know, I think the way that you ultimately combat it is to build the skills to be undeniable. And, you know, then you have the confidence that you can go out and earn what you want. I mean, I just am such, I'm so adverse to the thinking of, oh, I need to, you know, cut my expenses and live like, to me, the answer isn't living a lower lifestyle in those cases. The answer is going out and figuring out a way to add value to be able to deserve that lifestyle. And that's not great advice for everybody. I mean, everybody's different, but that's just how I'm built and how I have to frame it and how I have to think about it.</blockquote>
</details>

### [49:27] Highlight Loss to Inspire Change


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (47:59 - 49:32)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/e2c398e0-cd99-416c-acd0-a5ac85c9360a)




### ✨ Summary
In the context of risk and decision-making, when faced with loss, individuals may prefer to take greater risks for a chance of avoiding defeat altogether. This perspective of loss impacts not only sporting outcomes but also extends to negotiation and behavior change. To motivate someone to abandon the status quo and embrace new actions, it is essential to emphasize potential losses rather than gains. While gains help maintain current behavior, spotlighting losses is a strategic approach to encourage action and risk-taking, revealing a broader psychological principle that can apply across various scenarios.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>But in that situation, can I ask you this? In that situation, there's an interesting dynamic which is, if you lose by a goal or you lose by 10 goals, the loss is equivalent in that situation. And so the incentive is to risk losing by much more for the slim chance of not losing at all. And so that makes sense to me. But what makes less would make less sense to me is if losing by three goals disadvantaged you three times as badly down the track as losing it. And so how would you think about that in the sense of prospect theory? Right.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>And I think that that would not be as clear cut because the loss is a little bit different there. But what I think is interesting is if you think about this not just in the world of sport, which I know you think a lot about the world of sport, but I think it's really interesting to consider That if I go from the soccer pitch to the negotiation table, that if I want to get you to do something you're currently not doing, if I want you to move off the status quo and do something New or do something different, what I'm asking you to do is take a risk, right? Doing what we're already doing is risk aversion, risk free. Doing something new is risky. So if I want to get you to do something, I have to highlight loss, not gain. And I think that's the very counterintuitive idea that comes out of it, which is you highlight gain to maintain the status quo. You highlight loss to move off, gain to maintain, loss to move off. And so I think that that is pervasive across all situations, but I can't tell you what would happen if they change the rules in soccer.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:12:09] Anchoring in Negotiation


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:10:36 - 01:12:09)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/db1686bc-ba0e-4d14-84bf-f0bda95c8c13)


**Anchoring in Negotiation**

- Anchoring, a psychological bias, influences estimates and negotiations by setting an initial value. 
- Make the first offer to gain an advantage and frame the conversation.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Sure. So anchoring is a psychological bias. People get anchored on numbers and they insufficiently adjust off of those estimates. And so anchoring happens every day. If I asked you to estimate the number of what you would call mackers, what I would call McDonald's restaurants in Australia, if I asked you to estimate the number of them, and I had a big Urn in front of me filled with balls with numbers, and I pull out one ball in one room and it says 5,000, and in the other room I pull out a ball that says 50,000, I'll get higher estimates From the people who see the 50,000 than the five, even though you know there's no relationship at all between that ball that I just pulled out and how many McDonald's there are. But the reality is that works in any estimating situation. So in a negotiation, if I make that first offer and I anchor it, I'm going to get a huge starting point advantage because I'm not just throwing out a ball. I'm giving you an offer with a rationale and a whole story. I'm going to get a huge anchoring effect. And what is true is it's not that people are just blindly attached to that initial point. They adjust. It's that they insufficiently adjust off of the initial starting point. And that's why you should always lead in negotiation. Remember that, Adam. Make the first offer. When you make the first offer, you get the anchoring advantage. When you make the first offer, you get to set the table with the right issues. When you make the first offer, you get to frame the conversation and you grab the relationship enhancing position. There's a lot of advantages to leading and anchoring is one of them.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:21:13] Car Negotiation Story

- Her approach was so effective that her husband refused to accompany her to the dealership.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>

### [01:27:42] Bronze vs. Silver Medalist Happiness


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:25:23 - 01:27:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f9279b3e-1c29-42e1-ba70-1c3361a5d3ae)


**Bronze vs. Silver Medalist Happiness**

- Research by Vicki Medvec and Tom Gilovich shows bronze medalists are often happier than silver medalists. 
- This is due to downward counterfactual thinking, focusing on what could have been worse.

### [01:30:24] Counterfactual Cutoff Points


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:28:16 - 01:30:24)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/8add35d3-dbc0-4668-93c0-7f6900b99e85)


**Counterfactual Cutoff Points**

- Counterfactual cutoff points create dissatisfaction despite objective performance differences. 
- Eliminate cutoffs and use continuous performance metrics for increased satisfaction.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>So let me get more into that because I want to give you the application back to your companies. I know you are a chairman for many companies. I know Adam is running luxury escapes. The reality is, this has implications inside your company. The implication inside your company is, anytime you create what we call a counterfactual cutoff point. There's a cutoff point between winners and medalists, and there's a cutoff point between medalists and fourth. There's cutoff.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>I really love where this is going.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Whenever you create counterfactual cutoff points, you create a disconnect between satisfaction and performance, where people who perform objectively better will be more dissatisfied Than people who they outperform. And that affects you when you're doing bonuses, for example. So if you create a system where you categorize people, like these are stellar, and these are excellent and these are average, you will create counterfactual cutoff points where the People that are in the stellar group who are just short of the next category will be far, far more disappointed and more likely to depart than people who they outperformed who just made It into that category over average. The same is true with grades. Whenever students are complaining about their grades to me, I always make certain to highlight that there were a lot of people between them and the next grade. I never want to say, you just missed it, because that makes them very dissatisfied. So you want to think about all the times in life where we create cutoffs. And I would actually say you should eliminate them as much as you can. Because what you really want is a more continuous performance metric. So imagine at luxury escapes, if you had something where you evaluated people and they got a score of 89, which meant they got 89% of their eligible bonus. And then next time they got 94, and they would get 94 their eligible bonus. You've just lost all the cutoffs of those counterfactual cutoff points, and you now have people who are performing better being more satisfied. So it actually has a lot of implication for management, but it was more fun to study it in the Olympics.</blockquote>
</details>

### [44:36] KI und Steuerberatung


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (42:32 - 44:36)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/375c7414-0212-4f8f-9a7d-2fac281c52eb)


**KI und Steuerberatung**

- KI kann Steuerberater derzeit nicht ersetzen, da sie große Datensätze und Haftungsübernahme benötigt.
- Delta Equity sieht KI als Chance zur Kapazitätserweiterung.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Dann haben wir künstliche Intelligenz, glaube ich, das haben wir in der Vergangenheit viele mal angesprochen. Was passiert hier? Ist es nicht so, dass künftig gewisse Softwarelösungen die Steuerberatungsdienstleistungen ersetzen. Es ist heute schon so, dass es eine ganze Reihe von Softwarelösungen gibt, die die Arbeit des Steuerberaters vereinfachen, wo man heute schon annehmen könnte, dass das Aufgabenspektrum Des Steuerberaters vielleicht auch kleiner wird. KI hat zwei wesentliche oder vielleicht drei wesentliche Probleme. Das erste Problem ist, dass sie einen entsprechenden Datensatz brauchen, von dem die K.I. Lernen kann und muss. So ein Datensatz ist heute nur schwer verfügbar bis gar nicht verfügbar. Dann haben wir auch einen Expert-Call gehabt mit einem Professor für angewandte künstliche Intelligenz. Er sagt, mit das größte Problem ist auch, wenn es heute im Steuerrecht zu Gesetzesänderungen kommt. Und selbst in dem unwahrscheinlichen Fall, dass Sie einen Datensatz haben, müssten Sie jetzt verstehen, welche dieser Daten basiert noch auf der alten Gesetzgebung. Die müssten Sie manuell rausfinden, damit die KI jetzt nicht hier eine falsche Empfehlung gibt oder die Steuererklärung falsch ausfüllt. Das ist eigentlich nicht möglich. Und das dritte große Thema ist Haftung. Sie werden keinen Softwarehersteller finden für KI, der entsprechend hier die Haftung übernimmt, während aus Berufswegen schon die Steuerberater heute auch ein Haftungsrisiko Natürlich haben und dafür auch entsprechende Versicherungen, Polisen abschließen müssen. Das ist im Grunde auch eine Anerkennungsvoraussetzung. Und das werden Sie hier im Bereich KI nicht haben. Vereinfachung des Steuerrechts, wir hatten ganz kurz darüber gesprochen. Da sehen wir jetzt weniger das Thema Fachkräftemangel ist hier sicherlich ein Thema. Das ist was, was uns jetzt in den Gesprächen mit möglichen Zielunternehmen immer wieder begegnet. Wir glauben aber, dass wir als Gruppe dort diesen Herausforderungen des Fachkräftemangels viel besser begegnen können, wie es heute kleinere Steuerberatungsgesellschaften, Die nur lokal verwurzelt sind, heute bereits können.</blockquote>
</details>

### [48:11] Fachkräftemangel


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (45:33 - 48:11)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/6137514f-5fe9-4a88-84d8-fafdb632bb03)


**Fachkräftemangel**

- Delta Equity hat in vorherigen Branchenkonzepten erfolgreich Fachkräftemangel und steigende Lohnkosten gemanagt.
-  Professionelle Recruiting-Strategien und Employer Branding sind entscheidend.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>

Oder verantwortlich involviert war, immer das Thema des Fachkräftemangels und der steigenden Lohnkosten hatten. Das ist ja auch in der Tat über Angebot und Nachfrage an sich ein bisschen verbunden und genauso das Thema KI. Ich habe eingangs erwähnt, IT und Personal sind für uns bei Delta sehr wichtige Bereiche, wo wir auch schon relativ früh dann Holding-Ressourcen schaffen und hier auch voneinander Lernen. Aus meiner Erfahrung bei der Radiologie Holding, wo wir einen sehr drückenden Mangel im Bereich medizinisches Personal hatten, kann man schon sagen, dass man durch eine geschickte, Professionelle, strukturierte Recruiting-Strategie auch mit entsprechenden Mitarbeitern, die das eben auf Gruppenebene dann auch machen und ihr Know-how dort einbringen, Dass man hier dann doch mindestens mal das Quäntchen besser sein kann als der Wettbewerb und hier einen Vorteil sich erarbeiten kann. Ich glaube, bei Steuerberatern wahrscheinlich nicht so drängend wie in den verschiedenen Gesundheitswesensberufen, in denen wir vorher tätig waren, aber auch ein modernes Arbeitsumfeld, Ein moderner, werfschätzender Umgang mit Mitarbeitern und eben dieses Employer Branding auch nach außen, ist für uns schon immer sehr, sehr wichtig. Lohnkosten, aber auch eben die potenziellen Risiken, die sich aus sich ergebenden IT-Thematiken zukünftig ergebend von sehen. Wir aber, dass auch ganz bewusst dieses Risiko eingehen möchten oder dieses in Märkte hineingehen, die diese zwei Aspekte dann auch deutlich ausgeprägt haben, weil wir eben dass Man über die Bildung einer Gruppe Skaleffekte erzielen kann, die man eben insbesondere in den Bereichen dann als Wettbewerbsvorteil auch umsetzen kann. Und da haben wir ganz viele Beispiele, wo das erfolgreich geglückt ist. Deswegen sind wir da ziemlich komfortabel, das nicht nur handeln zu können, sondern vielleicht auch in einem Wettbewerbsvorteil umsetzen zu können. Das</blockquote>
</details>

# #202 Matthew Dicks: Good Stories vs. Great Stories


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #202 Matthew Dicks: Good Stories vs. Great Stories
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/5ad338c6-c226-4db5-bc24-16295d0598af)
- Episode publish date: 2024-08-29
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Shane Parrish sits down with Matthew Dicks, a renowned storyteller, author, and teacher, to explore the nuanced art of storytelling. They go deep into the techniques that turn mediocre stories into masterful ones and the essential elements that make narratives unforgettable.  You’ll learn what makes a story truly resonate with an audience, how to identify and highlight the pivotal moments that create emotional impact, the architecture of compelling stories, how to structure narratives for maximum engagement and how to use techniques like suspense, stakes, and humor to keep audiences on the edge of their seats. This conversation covers broad frameworks, like how to structure a great story—and the granular details, like when you should talk quietly to refocus the audience. Matthew Dicks is novelist, storyteller, columnist, playwright, blogger, and teacher. He’s published fiction and non-fiction books, the latter of which include:  Storyworthy: Engage, Teach, Persuade, and Change Your Life Through the Power of Storytelling  and  Someday Is Today: 22 Simple, Actionable Ways to Propel Your Creative Life.   Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business. Sign up here: https://fs.blog/newsletter/ Follow Shane on twitter at: https://twitter.com/ShaneAParrish Access your transcript today: https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/matthew-dicks-202/<br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Episode AI notes


1. Matthew Dicks emphasizes the importance of embracing mistakes as a natural part of learning, particularly in building resilience in children.
2. Dicks discusses the value of individual support systems, like comforting objects, to help alleviate anxiety in stressful situations, fostering a sense of community among peers.
3. The podcast highlights that simple acts of care can create strong classroom connections, reminding children they are not alone in their struggles.
4. Success is portrayed as a continuous evolution; avoiding stagnation is crucial to discovering unexpected opportunities.
5. The essence of success lies in being open to change and pursuing new challenges, making the journey dynamic and adaptable.


## Snips


### [11:40] A Spoon of Comfort: Embrace Mistakes and Support Each Other


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (10:22 - 11:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0de8f793-7ff2-41e2-89cd-5fd111bfa6a5)




### ✨ Summary
Building resilience in children involves reassuring them that mistakes are a natural part of learning. Individual support, like placing a comforting object around a child's neck, can help alleviate anxiety during stressful situations, such as tests. This practice not only calms children but also fosters a sense of community, where peers support one another during tough times. Over a school year, this simple act becomes a symbol of care, reminding children that they are not alone in their struggles. Ultimately, meaningful connections and the ability to face challenges together create a strong classroom family.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>So all week he tortures me with this. And then Thursday comes, it's time for our weekly math test. It's time for Mackenzie to lose her mind because someday Mackenzie might get a problem wrong and that will be the end of the world for Mackenzie. So every Thursday, I have to build her up. Mistakes are valuable. It's okay, Mackenzie. You might get one wrong. And she's sort of falling apart as she does. And then Jamie's there, and he takes the spoon off, and he says, maybe this will help. And he puts it around Mackenzie's neck. And it's the best math tests Mackenzie has ever taken in her life. Somehow this spoon on her neck calms her down. Three days later, David's grandfather passes away. When David comes back to school, Jamie's by the door waiting for David. When he walks in, puts the spoon on David's neck and says, I think you need this today. And he did. For the rest of the year, every single time a kid is in trouble in any way whatsoever, that spoon finds their way on their neck. They forget their homework. They have to walk over to me. Face the music, we call it. They walk over with that damn spoon on their neck. They get in trouble with the principal. They got to make the long walk down the linoleum hallway. They make the long walk with the spoon. They get bullied on the bus on the way to school. When they go home that day, they go home with the spoon. Every single time it makes the kids' days better. So the last day of school, I gather all my kids on the floor in front of me. It's the last time we're going to be together as a family. And they really are a family. We get to know each other in really meaningful ways. And</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:55:01] Evolving Success: Embrace Change


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:53:59 - 01:55:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f75dc2c8-d9b9-48db-927c-214bca73be63)




### ✨ Summary
Success is defined by continuous evolution and the exploration of new possibilities. Stagnation is viewed as a form of failure, where planning too far into the future limits the opportunity to discover unexpected opportunities. The essence of success lies in being open to change and finding new pursuits that bring joy or challenge, emphasizing that the journey itself should be dynamic and adaptable.

I just had this conversation with someone.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>For me, success is always that at some very future tomorrow i'll be doing something different than i'm doing today i i think stasis is death i have a company you know i have a company that Produces storytelling content and i've got a business partner he always wants to plan out six months or a year and i know why he wants to do it actually my production manager wants the Same thing and i resist it all time. Because I feel like if you've planned out six months, we don't have the opportunity for me to stumble upon the new thing that I want to stumble upon. So for me, success is in six months, will I be doing something I never could have imagined doing today that I'm now trying and maybe loving or maybe hating, but at least finding a thing That I want to start doing that is different. As long as my life is constantly evolving and I am</blockquote>
</details>

# 10: Josh Wolfe - Illuminating Tomorrow


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fmegaphone.imgix.net%2Fpodcasts%2F45feeece-fb01-11f0-8ddb-670e0cbddc5a%2Fimage%2F57fa6e36d38beb29a095efdef3dec04a.png%3Fixlib%3Drails-4.3.1%26max-w%3D3000%26max-h%3D3000%26fit%3Dcrop%26auto%3Dformat%2Ccompress&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: 10: Josh Wolfe - Illuminating Tomorrow
- Show: Dialectic
- Owner / Host: Jackson Dahl
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/5a505d64-8131-46bc-9b41-a4db1f170c08)
- Episode publish date: 2025-02-25
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Josh Wolfe ( Website ,  X ) is co-founder and Managing Partner of  Lux Capital , a venture firm focused on emerging science and technology at the outermost edges of what is possible.<br/>>  Josh is a masterful storyteller who moves seamlessly between science, culture, and markets. As an investor, he seeks the counter-narrative—what others aren't talking about—and has backed countless breakthrough companies in AI, space, biotech, robotics, defense, and beyond. Beyond investing, Josh founded  Coney Island Prep  charter school and is a trustee at the  Santa Fe Institute .<br/>>  Our conversation explores the interplay between science and storytelling, the power of belief in both doubters and advocates, patterns in creative rebels, and what makes someone both "arrogant" enough to assert a new reality while remaining grounded enough to see reality clearly. We discuss America's scientific competitiveness, the value of competition in institutions, Josh's voracious appetite for the new, and his personal journey with control, trust, and family.<br/>>  Josh is one of my favorite examples of someone who is radically unhedged on himself: he leans into his genius—and thus sometimes, disfunction—in ways that make him authentically effective. Throughout the episode, he demonstrates his rare combination of wisdom and childlike curiosity, competitive drive, and deep care for the things that matter to him. His ideas on storytelling, science, and human nature offer a guide for thinking about bringing new things into the world.<br/>>   Episode transcript .<br/>>   Timestamps <br/>>    (2:12): Science & Stories   (6:30): Are technology outcomes and timelines determined or variable based on cultural movements, societal reaction, and stories?    (11:14): Who is Imagining the Future of Tomorrow?    (16:34): Originality, AI, and Everything is a Remix    (20:52): Improving as a Storyteller    (23:55): Secrets & Magic    (31:35): Josh's Biggest Believers    (34:20): Belief Beyond Capital, Motivation, and Fuel    (37:53): Patterns in Creative Rebels    (40:53): "Arrogance of the Highest Order" in Entrepreneurs, Asserting Reality, Great Men of History, and Elon    (47:02): US Exceptionalism and Celebrating Science & Technology    (55:23): Institutions, Academia, Government, and Competition    (1:00:56): Josh: Accomplished Yet Childlike - Finding the Edges    (1:04:07): Breadth and Depth: a Heat-Seeking Ability to Go Deep    (1:12:03): Managing Dowsides and Lessons that Could Have Been Learned Sooner    (1:15:58): Control, Past, and Future    (1:19:57): Learnings from Mom    (1:20:57): Trust and learning from Optimistic Partners    (1:22:54): Legacy <br/>>    Links & References <br/>>        Kevin G's Josh Wolfe Compilation        Kevin's much shorter twitter thread summary of Josh's spikeyness        Lux Q3 2024 LP Letter - Four Parables of Four Greek Titans
</details>

## Snips


### [05:16] Stories and Human Nature


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (02:48 - 05:16)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/9a0c2e31-c1ee-440c-8bd0-f17fdc0782ed)


**Stories and Human Nature**

- Stories shape us, and we shape our stories in an endless cycle.
- Technologies, markets, and governments change, but human nature remains constant.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>That's a great question. Yes. I, first of all, believe that everybody should be watching copious amounts of TV and reading tons of graphic novels and comic books. And all of those things arguably are like the condensed compression algorithm of either one or thousands of people that are basically scripting something. So you think about all of the effort, energy, money, creativity that goes into like a single Game of Thrones episode. And so that's on one end of the spectrum. And then at the other end of the spectrum, you have like a single comedian who has no budget and is just riffing on the realities of life. But I find that people that are conjuring these fictions are shaping us. So our stories shape us. We shape our stories in sort of this endless cycle. You can make the argument that everything is a remix, that we're constantly taking stuff from the past, we're recombining it. And we are narrative creatures. We are storytelling animals. And it is the way that we process things. I think it was Einstein that said that the reason that time exists is so that everything doesn't happen all at once. But A happens, then B happens, and it causes C and D, and then it goes back. And sometimes there's a fork in the road, and then there's a conflict, and then there's the hero's journey. And so all of those things are sort of timeless, and they are all stories. And I always say that technologies can change, and markets can change, and governments can change, and leaders can change, but human nature is a constant, literally going back to like The place to see in our evolutionary past. And it's the same thing. In fact, there was a startup that we funded. It was recently on deathbed, like in its final days. And miraculously, the CEO and the founder ended up, and we had committed financing, but I said, you need to find an external lead and we'll be all in. And he ended up doing it and exceeded his fundraising target. And it was like darkest before the dawn and he did it. And we're all like, amazing, pull the rabbit out of the hat. This was incredible. And then what happens next? Arguably, the predictable Shakespearean drama that there's some internal coup. Now they've got capital, they've got resources. And two people are like, you got to go. And he's like, but I was focused all on external financing. And they're like, yes, but you weren't focused on the company. And so I had to calm everybody. We say like, this is par for the course. This is what happens. This is human nature. This is conflict. So I deeply believe that the more stories you consume, whether they are through lived experience or through fiction, I actually think it doesn't matter. If you've seen a shit ton of movies and you've read a bunch of fiction and you've read lots of graphic novels, you have scenarios that I think can guide you. And there's extraordinary wisdom in all of these things from Shakespeare to Westworld.</blockquote>
</details>

### [11:01] Progress and Narrative


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (07:20 - 11:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/27210366-1e68-4b74-84c6-26cb71261663)


**Progress and Narrative**

- There's a directional arrow of progress in technology, but cultural narratives influence the timeline.
-  Nuclear power's decline illustrates how stories and cultural movements can impact technological adoption.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>Think it's both in that I believe deeply in the first part that there are directional arrows of progress. There are certain inevitabilities of technology and you don't know necessarily who the entrepreneur is going to be or who the company is going to be or when it's going to happen, if it's Going to be in two weeks or two months or two years or two decades, but there is an inevitability. So you take nuclear as an example. There is an inevitability of mankind's march through energy density. You went from carbohydrates, which were diffuse and disperse, not a lot of energy density, large fields of fuel that people would effectively burn, wood. Then you went to hydrocarbons. We discover oil and natural gas. And you crack those bonds and an exothermic reaction releases heat. You can boil water. You spin turbines. You produce electricity. And then you go to uranium, which is arguably today the densest form of energy. And that march from carbohydrates to hydrocarbons to uranium, from burning wood to burning fossil fuels to releasing the power of the atom is a directional arrow of progress. Now, people have to believe because we broke the atom in 1903, 1904, discovered the atom rather, thanks to a variety of bad immigration policies. We ended up with Einstein here, you know, bad immigration policies from Germany, to put it politely. And we ended up developing the bomb here and splitting the atom. And that made us a global superpower and arguably gave us 104 nuclear reactors and 103 nuclear subs that are going around the world. And that has declined in part because the story has declined. And the story declined in part culturally. 1979, just to spend a few moments on nuclear, you had Three Mile Island, which was an accident. Nobody died. There was no real radiation release, but people were freaked out. At the same time that you had a movie, The China Syndrome, so that's in the cultural zeitgeist, and people are primed and they're like, oh my God, this is horrible. This is a nuclear disaster. And at the same time, you have the conflation of nuclear power and nuclear war. And nuclear power is a good virtuous thing, particularly if you're on the left side and you're an environmentalist and you want zero carbon and large baseload power and energy to be Able to solve human problems and let people live and break out of poverty. Nuclear war is a terrible thing. And so you have my mother's generation of musicians like Neil Young and Joni Mitchell and James Taylor that literally have a concert in 1979, no nukes. And everybody was just like, no nukes, not just for war, but like for power.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Jackson Dahl</b><br/><br/>Right.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>And so I actually think that that set it back, but that was a cultural zeitgeist. That was artists and musicians leading a charge, movie and Hollywood leading a charge that resulted in what you could argue is a rising cost of capital. People wouldn't fund this stuff. People didn't want to work on it. It was socially taboo. And now that's starting to change. In the past five years, you have beautiful models that are promoting nuclear, like Isabel. And I was like, where did she come from? Is she paid by like the Nuclear Energy Institute? Were they smart enough to actually hire like an attractive influencer? And that is just changing the zeitgeist of younger people. So I, not her alone, but just like this, the zeitgeist is changing. And I think that's a positive. So those things take time, but they definitely are like these cultural memes, fads that change what people value. And that comes from storytelling of each other, echo chambers and amplification and people that you heroicize or villainize. If Elon came out, which he hasn't, in part because he has interest in solar, but if he were to say we should be developing and forget about me calling it elemental energy, if he called it Elemental energy.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Jackson Dahl</b><br/><br/>Yeah, yeah, yeah.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>Like the world would be funding nuclear power plants tomorrow because of his influence left and right, which would be incredible. So there's a long-winded answer to the first part is that there are these directional arrows of progress and they are also contingent upon people acting and you have to believe to act.</blockquote>
</details>

### [09:37] No Nukes Movement

**No Nukes Movement**

- The "no nukes" movement, fueled by events like Three Mile Island and the film "The China Syndrome," negatively impacted nuclear power.
- This demonstrates the power of cultural narratives to shape technological trajectories.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>And the story declined in part culturally. 1979, just to spend a few moments on nuclear, you had Three Mile Island, which was an accident. Nobody died. There was no real radiation release, but people were freaked out. At the same time that you had a movie, The China Syndrome, so that's in the cultural zeitgeist, and people are primed and they're like, oh my God, this is horrible. This is a nuclear disaster. And at the same time, you have the conflation of nuclear power and nuclear war. And nuclear power is a good virtuous thing, particularly if you're on the left side and you're an environmentalist and you want zero carbon and large baseload power and energy to be Able to solve human problems and let people live and break out of poverty. Nuclear war is a terrible thing. And so you have my mother's generation of musicians like Neil Young and Joni Mitchell and James Taylor that literally have a concert in 1979, no nukes. And everybody was just like, no nukes, not just for war, but like for power.</blockquote>
</details>

### [16:15] Sci-fi's Future


[🎧 Play snip - 4min️ (11:53 - 16:15)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/9d2c7bcc-3818-4317-8fae-cbf5fe9c4f8d)


**Sci-fi's Future**

-  Much of current sci-fi pulls from timeless themes, but new inspiration is emerging from China.
-  AI tools can help expand our imaginative capacity and inspire new sci-fi narratives.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>Remember, on the one hand, the same source material are the same stories told through different stages and different characters, right? So, you know, Joseph Campbell's Hero of the Journey, Shakespeare, the stages change, the costumes change the characters change but it's the same stories so there's these timeless Aspects of that um of conflict of jealousy of envy of love of betrayal of camaraderie of alliances formed against the powerful like those are constants um i think that the latest sci-fi That is inspiring is coming from China. Think about Three Body Problems. You have a movement away from the dystopian environmental sci-fi, which I find utterly boring, uninteresting, predictable. You have people that mix a little bit of history with a little bit of speculative sci-fi. Neil Gaiman, unfortunately, has been like now canceled but i think he's a brilliant mind neil stevenson uh his latest i think beginning of a trifecta polistan talks about the origin Of the nuclear bomb and um and weaves in geopolitics in u.s and russia and um somebody actually was just telling me that there was this one character dick and um he's a really interesting Character he's like this Jewish nerdy scientist and physicist. And somebody's like, you know that's Richard Feynman. I was like, what? Oh, yeah, Dick. Oh, my God. You know? Anyway, so I think that there are people that take history and combine it with speculative futures, and that is super valuable because, you know, history doesn't repeat. It rhymes as cliched as that is, and it's true. And then I think you have wildly imaginative people. And I actually think AI is helping break through what is imaginable and possible because you can just literally tell mid-journey, imagine this crazy thing that I just imagined. And then suddenly it becomes real and you're able to express yourself. And so I do think that there are just creative geniuses out there that can combine ideas and genres and inspire people. But if you think about it, most sci-fi is like, okay, we're going to go populate or colonize outer space. And all of those space operas are still wrought with those same Shakespearean conflicts. Abandoned. They're marauded. It's an individual from like the Martian to aliens. We encounter some alien force in some cases like, what was it, District 9. They're metaphors for- Hard time or whatever, yeah. Yeah. And in other cases, it's just H.R. Geiger, a representation of an alien that literally we're going to encounter in outer space. Then you've got the people that I think do the near-term prognostications like, who is it, Charles Brooker from Black Mirror, who I think is just brilliant because those have presaged Social implications of the technologies that we're inventing. And that to me is always interesting that Kevin Kelly, you know, the founder of Wired, who I think is one of the great philosophers of technology, was like, the key question is not to ask What happens when one technology exists. You can sort of predict how somebody might use it. The key question is what happens when everybody has that thing. Of course. And so I think that people that are able to sort of imagine those speculative things are really interesting. There was a recent short film that I actually thought was really good. It wasn't sci-fi so much, but I don't even know where or how I discovered it. It might have even been me querying Chagipiti or Anthropic, and it came up with a recommendation, but it was a 20, 25-minute film about a writer's room. I think they called it Writer's Doom or something like that. The premise was that they had to imagine an AI future. You had all these five different personalities that really did a thoughtful treatise on how AI was likely to develop and going towards super intelligence and it was done in a scripted Way. I could have read one of these long essays, Leopold or whatever, but this was like a beautiful 20-minute human drama in dialogue, almost Aaron Sorkin, West Wing-like, that just made It hit. So I'm optimistic of our creative class, ever more accessible. And by the way, globally, because now their ideas can be translated as Three Body Problem was. And I think the people that write sci-fi, if we saw a genre of Chinese sci-fi, I'd actually be worried because they're going to be defining the future. So we want to attract talent here to build our companies, but we also want to attract the sci-fi authors here to write the future that we want to live in.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:13:51] Validating Emotions


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:12:21 - 01:13:54)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/f51699f0-46a4-48c5-877f-38dd892e30e8)


**Validating Emotions**

- When someone is upset, validate their emotion sincerely.
- Don't debate the validity of their feelings, as this adds fuel to the fire.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>This is sort of in that mindfulness meditation thing for the individual. I am highly emotional, highly vengeful and vindictive. It is not a great virtue of a personality that I would want my kids to model in many aspects. And thankfully, I have counterparts in both my wife, Lauren, and my co-founder, Peter, that have the antithesis kind of personality. So it's a balancing thing. If I was alone, I'd probably be more self-destructive in that way. So I think having a partner that is a yin and yang to balance out the positivity and negativity is one thing. The single most important thing that I wish I would have learned, which I only learned less than a decade ago through family therapy is the idea of CBT or DBT, cognitive behavioral therapy Or dialectic behavioral therapy, which I can condense down all the money and the time that we spent into a few things. When somebody is upset, whether it's my daughter, her son, my wife, a partner, a CEO, the worst thing you can do is debate them on why what they're feeling is ridiculous to feel. My daughter's upset because she can't get her shoe on when she was three or something. I'm like, what are you doing? Just like, get your damn shoe on. We got to go, you know, yelling at her. My wife would say, I'd be like, what do you mean? You know, these things that lead to fights and conflicts. A CEO upset or a CTO upset about their CEO and invalidating an emotion, all it does is add fuel to the fire and them feeling like they don't understand. Yeah. Calm down. You're freaking out. Worst thing you could say. So I learned if my daughter's upset, I can say, I completely understand and can see you're really upset in a sincere way.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:15:53] Managing Emotions


[🎧 Play snip - 3min️ (01:12:21 - 01:15:53)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/1cac59d4-b2de-49a2-9be8-0151e7a4c380)


**Managing Emotions**

- Validate others' emotions, even if you disagree with their actions, to reduce conflict.
- Avoid using the word "but," which negates preceding statements and hinders receptiveness.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Josh Wolfe</b><br/><br/>This is sort of in that mindfulness meditation thing for the individual. I am highly emotional, highly vengeful and vindictive. It is not a great virtue of a personality that I would want my kids to model in many aspects. And thankfully, I have counterparts in both my wife, Lauren, and my co-founder, Peter, that have the antithesis kind of personality. So it's a balancing thing. If I was alone, I'd probably be more self-destructive in that way. So I think having a partner that is a yin and yang to balance out the positivity and negativity is one thing. The single most important thing that I wish I would have learned, which I only learned less than a decade ago through family therapy is the idea of CBT or DBT, cognitive behavioral therapy Or dialectic behavioral therapy, which I can condense down all the money and the time that we spent into a few things. When somebody is upset, whether it's my daughter, her son, my wife, a partner, a CEO, the worst thing you can do is debate them on why what they're feeling is ridiculous to feel. My daughter's upset because she can't get her shoe on when she was three or something. I'm like, what are you doing? Just like, get your damn shoe on. We got to go, you know, yelling at her. My wife would say, I'd be like, what do you mean? You know, these things that lead to fights and conflicts. A CEO upset or a CTO upset about their CEO and invalidating an emotion, all it does is add fuel to the fire and them feeling like they don't understand. Yeah. Calm down. You're freaking out. Worst thing you could say. So I learned if my daughter's upset, I can say, I completely understand and can see you're really upset in a sincere way. Not I can see you're really upset, right? Which is totally different. But like, I can see you're really upset. All of a sudden you can see this feeling of exhalation and validation. So validate the emotion. It doesn't mean you have to agree with the person. So I can say, I can see you're really upset and you can't punch your sister in the face. The second thing is never say, but people are in this from improv to inspire creativity. The second you say, but you invalidate everything that came before. Jackson, I love you, but, you know, and people in relationships say that, you know, Josh, I love you, but the second you say, but you invalidate and you're basically presaging that you're About to be critical. And so if you just change that to an end of, I love you and what you did made me feel like it's just, people are more receptive to it. Because in both cases, you're otherwise provoking them to just be defensive and become more emotional and you don't get to that. So that would be something that I would say I'm very grateful. My wife, one of her best friends is this woman, Molly Carmel, who's a psychologist and therapist and focuses on eating disorders and has helped extraordinary people and the scions Of billionaires and fascinating. And she really helped us with this as a family and has become like an integral part of like reducing the dysfunction of dysregulated people that are highly emotional. My wife and I are both alpha people. My kids are all alpha people. And so that has made us just a way tighter, more loving, more understanding family. We're imperfect at times, but it's just like that is something I wish everybody would learn. I wish that we would teach it in schools. It doesn't take any of the ambition or intensity out of your pursuit of wanting to be competitive, but it makes you more effective. And I learned that my daughter doesn't walk into a room and a CEO doesn't walk into a room and one of my partners doesn't walk into a room thinking, you know what? I really want to say something really stupid and piss everybody off right now with horrible consequences. No, you just, you suddenly become empathetic to say they don't have the skills to walk into the situation and regulate their emotions and be effective. So that is the biggest thing I wish that I would have learned earlier. And</blockquote>
</details>

### [04:20] Tails Drive Everything


[🎧 Play snip - 2min️ (01:22 - 04:20)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/bc35cb89-390a-427a-863e-ca6f530056e2)


**Tails Drive Everything**

- 2024 highlighted a significant trend: outcomes are increasingly driven by extremes.
- Many experienced either exceptional success or significant failure, particularly in the stock market and specific careers.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>Which is, look, this is a very broad statement. It's meant to be broad. It might not apply to you. But I think for a lot of people, 2024 was either an exceptionally good year or an exceptionally bad year. And I think you see that dynamic play out in several areas of life. One, because this is kind of my domain, is in the stock market, where the S&P 500 or the NASDAQ had an absolutely bonanza year. You're up heavy double digits, 25%, 30%. The NASDAQ's up 30% year-to Absolutely incredible. Way ahead of the long-term averages. But if you dig down into actually what happened in those indexes, those big components of stocks. It's a much different story. So among the S&P 500, 500 of the largest companies in America, of course, on average, in aggregate, this was an incredible year. But I checked this morning, roughly half of the stocks in the index are down this year. In a year where the average went up 25%, half the components are down. And if you look at the bottom fifth, a pretty big chunk of American capitalism, you're talking about companies that are down 20% or more. And then at the top of the list, you have these absolute home run, knock it out of the park, into the stratosphere companies. Palantir, NVIDIA, of course, companies like Microsoft, whatnot, that have just had absolutely sensational runs and are now worth trillions of dollars. Broadcom just reached the trillion dollar club last week, and that's a company that I think the majority of Americans have never heard of. And this has always been the case, the idea that tails drive everything. A small percentage of companies or a small percentage of attempts at anything you do account for the majority of the returns. But it feels like it's much more accentuated these days. The Magnificent Seven, as they are called tech companies, the big mammoth tech companies, NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, Google, Facebook, those companies are now more than A third of the S&P 500. So it's 500 companies, a representative sample across diverse industries, but one third of it is seven tech companies. And that, of course, is a higher concentration than we've ever seen. There's always been a concentration at the top, but it's more than ever. And you can keep on going down the list of that dynamic in finance, where a small number of things drive the majority of outcomes. And on one hand, that's kind of an interesting academic observation of statistics and fat tales and whatnot. But I think it does something very important and potentially dangerous to people's psyches, where it creates a situation where your investing portfolio, your career, your industry, Your job security is either doing exceptionally well or very poorly.</blockquote>
</details>

### [06:01] All-or-Nothing Careers


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (04:20 - 06:01)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/1c444c2f-bca9-4ff7-a740-451d8fbfcf43)


**All-or-Nothing Careers**

- Many careers now follow an all-or-nothing trajectory, like YouTube creators who either make a fortune or nothing at all.
- This polarization extends to politics, where individuals experience the economy and culture as either exceptionally beneficial or detrimental.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>I thought about this the other day when I was thinking about YouTube creators, and it occurred to me as a broad sweeping statement that you cannot make a living as a YouTube creator. It's impossible to make a living as a YouTube creator. You can make a fortune. You can make an absolutely incredible fortune, but you cannot make a living. Like it either works or it doesn't. You either make zero dollars or you just have money flowing into your lap. And I think this is true for a lot of those kind of professions. Podcasts even, books, music creators, YouTube creators, those things either work or they don't. You cannot make a middling income. You can make a fortune or it just doesn't work. And more and more careers are like that. One other area that I think you saw this in 2024 was in politics, which of course, I'm stating something that everybody knows, is more divisive, more partisan, more on the tails than It's ever been. And I think at least one of the reasons for that is the country in general, the direction of the economy and culture and society and economics is either working exceptionally well for You or it's not working at all. It's tougher to have people who are just right in the middle and saying, oh, I earn a decent income and I'm happy with it. And it's like that year after year. Less so than it used to be, where either your career is working very well or it's not working whatsoever. Now, I'll say it for the millionth time, that's a broad, general, sweeping statement. Of course, there are people who are earning a nice income and have for years and will for years to come. But more so than ever, it's a tale-driven economy.</blockquote>
</details>
