### [44:16] Master the Hall Pass of Sales


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (42:56 - 44:16)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/4529b44f-65fe-4643-aba4-8e8120a2c80a)




### ✨ Summary
Effective salesmanship requires more than just techniques; it hinges on building genuine relationships throughout the organization. The concept of the 'hall pass' reflects the importance of familiarity and trust, where a salesperson becomes a recognizable presence from the security guard to the executives, thus enhancing their ability to engage with clients and prospects across the board. This approach creates a culture that fosters open communication and access, ultimately driving sales success.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Pat Dorsey</b><br/><br/>I think for any VMS company that's under managed, you just drop Mark Leonard in. Um, I mean, he's got a more nuanced understanding of what drives a good VMS business than anyone I've ever met. Um, I had the great good fortune to meet him one-on at their offices one time, and then I saw him speak to a class of Columbia students about a year and a half ago, and those were both master Classes. And there's some people you meet with in any industry. I mean, for Mark Leonard, it's VMS Software, who, it's just, you're getting chapter and verse, right? So there's one, the CEO of one company we own currently. I still remember he visited us not long ago, and he talked about it as a salesperson at a company. And in this case, it was software. It could be anything. He said, your goal as a salesperson is to get the hall pass. Your goal is to show up and be able to walk around that building and talk to anybody you want. You want to be so familiar with the security guard, with the secretary, with everybody. You want the hall pass. And I was like, that's a great way to think about a sales culture. I mean, it's a great way to think about when you're talking to companies and assessing whether they can sell well or not, you know, how are people getting out on the street? How well are they not going to do? They have the hall pass. It was just kind of a cool way of framing it.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>Very cool. So coming back to your process.</blockquote>
</details>

### [14:18] Cars as a Status Symbol


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (13:16 - 14:18)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/41b00bbc-a998-4778-820d-cd1f5037f22f)


**Cars as a Status Symbol**

- In 1906, Woodrow Wilson, then president of Princeton University, remarked that the automobile fueled socialistic feeling due to its association with the arrogance of wealth. 
- Owning a car back then was similar to owning a private jet today, a symbol of affluence.


#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>He writes, quote, in the year 1906, Woodrow Wilson, who was then president of Princeton University, said, nothing has spread socialistic feeling in this country more than the automobile, And added that it offered a picture of the arrogance of wealth. So back in the day, if you had a car, that was like the equivalent of a private jet. It was like, oh, you fancy snooty people with your cars. That was what it felt like back then. And do we do the same thing today with new technologies? You know, it wasn't that long ago when the iPhone first came out that that was the expensive, you know, tech phone. And then it could, of course, went down to the masses. Kevin Kelly from Wired Magazine, he has this saying that I love this idea where he says, if you want to look at what ordinary people will be doing 20 years from now, look at what rich people Are doing today. Look at some of the products that they have, the technologies that they're using. Odds are, historically speaking, that those will eventually make their way down into the rest of society.</blockquote>
</details>

Teresa reflects on how her childhood influences, education, and taking calculated risks have shaped her path, emphasising the importance of freedom and genuine care in defining success. She highlights the critical role of customer service, maintaining the human touch in technology, and shaping company culture during periods of change, including acquisitions. The conversation also explores her passion for real estate, personal development, and giving back to the community, offering practical advice for aspiring leaders. # #199 Esther Perel: Cultivating Desire (2019)


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #199 Esther Perel: Cultivating Desire (2019)
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)

<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Few episodes in The Knowledge Project's nine-year history have impacted people's relationships and lives more than when Esther Perel shared her infinite wisdom for the first time in 2019.  Shane and Esther discuss how the stories you tell yourself shape how you see the world (and what to do about that), the important conversations to have at the beginning of a relationship, the most common arguments couples have and how to prevent them, what to say to a partner if the relationship isn't working out, the relationship between desire, love, and pleasure, and so much more. Plus, the conversation starts out on an interesting note: Esther Perel shares stories about her parents surviving the Holocaust and how their experiences shaped her childhood and continue to shape her life today.  Esther Perel is a couples therapist and New York Times best-selling author. Esther’s refreshingly frank approach to topics like sex, intimacy, divorce, and fidelity has made her one of the most unique and sought-after voices on modern relationships. 1. Foundational conversations in a relationship should cover age difference, shared goals, traditional values, expectations, and visions for life to ensure compatibility.
2. Balancing closeness and separateness in a relationship is crucial for its success.
3. Establishing regular couple rituals like weekly meetings or monthly date nights fosters connection and strengthens relationships.


## Snips


### [28:24] Foundation of Relationship Conversations


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (27:04 - 28:25)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/281b81e9-c2d7-4fdf-9b4c-13420acc9fb0)




### ✨ Summary
The initial conversations in a relationship should focus on foundational aspects such as age difference, shared goals like living arrangements, family planning, and career coordination. It is crucial to discuss traditional values, expectations, and visions for life to ensure compatibility. Furthermore, conversations about balancing closeness and separateness in the relationship are essential to its success.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>And the conversations that you have are about that. But there are a few basic ones you want to have in the beginning. This couple, they have 20 years apart. They're young as a couple. They're not necessarily both young in age. She has more experience than him, even though she's 20 years younger in terms of relationships. He's actually quite new at this, at a more kind of committed long -term relationship. Where do they want to live? They're both foreigners. Do they want to have a family together? How will they arrange their professional lives together? You know, in this instance, he comes from a rather traditional family where she's used to come home and there's food on the table. Well, is that the woman you picked? Is that being discussed between the two of you or is that an assumption? And if it's an assumption, you only make a statement when the food is not on the table. And she says, why don't you cook on occasion? So about people's values, it's about people's expectations, it's about people's vision for life. What do they look for in life, and is there a compatibility about that? I think one of the big conversations that accompanies every relationship is about closeness and separateness.</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:10:37] Regular Couple Rituals Ensure Stronger Relationships


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:09:11 - 01:10:35)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/33e38933-71e8-49a3-b124-f9d5aa3eb3c2)




### ✨ Summary
Couples who establish regular rituals, such as meeting weekly for a drink or having a monthly date night, create dedicated time for each other, fostering a sense of connection and importance. These rituals contribute to stronger relationships, emphasizing that the quality of the couple's relationship is crucial for the family's survival in contemporary times.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Massive. I mean, that's been studied. And we know that couples that have rituals, every Wednesday at five o 'clock we meet in a bar and have a drink. Every Tuesday we have lunch. Every month we leave a night away. Depends on what they can afford, what they can do. But those who have that ritual in place, which really states no matter what else goes on in our life, we have a dedicated time that is ours where we check in with each other, whatever we Do. You know, we go rowboating, whatever, whatever. But it's very clear that those who've done that for like decades on end, now, you know, you could say it's because they did it that they are stronger or it's because they're stronger that They do it. I think those things interact with each other. But it's, I don't know if I would do it morning and night, it depends on what other people are living in the homes. But I know that if people have a regularly dedicated time that says, we matter, and it's not everything else comes first, and we come last, which is what is often the case for many couples, That that makes a huge difference. And why does it matter? Because is the first time in the history of human relationships that the quality of the relationship in the couple is what will determine if the family will survive.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Oh, that's interesting.</blockquote>
</details>

# A Few Laws of Getting Rich


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd3t3ozftmdmh3i.cloudfront.net%2Fstaging%2Fpodcast_uploaded_nologo%2F43542795%2F798c62188974e978.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: A Few Laws of Getting Rich
- Show: The Morgan Housel Podcast
- Owner / Host: Morgan Housel
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/3e1d4c48-0a97-46f3-93ea-852406088748)
- Episode publish date: 2023-10-12
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Measuring wealth is easy. You just count it up. Measuring some of the downsides of wealth is so much harder and more nuanced. They can be so nuanced and hard to measure that many people won’t even believe they exist. A  downside  to wealth? How could that possibly be? <br/>>  Let me propose that the absurdity of talking about the downside of wealth is part of why wealth doesn’t tend to make people as happy as they thought it would. <br/>>  When the benefits of money are so obvious but the downsides are so subtle, the downsides you didn’t anticipate can be more jarring than the benefits you expected. <br/>>  I want more money, of course. Almost everyone does, albeit for different reasons. <br/>>  This is not an anti-wealth list -- just a collection of subtle downsides that are easy to ignore, and so common you may as well call them the only true laws of getting rich.
</details>

- Show notes link: [open website](https://podcasters.spotify.com/pod/show/morgan-housel/episodes/A-Few-Laws-of-Getting-Rich-e320omv)
- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Episode AI notes


1. The allure of making a lot of money can blind individuals to the reality that wealth does not guarantee happiness.
2. Money cannot fix relationship issues, make people like you more, or bring fulfillment.
3. The dream of financial success can feel good, but once achieved, can lead to depression.
4. Admiration for success may actually be envy, as people tend to like others more when they are striving, rather than when they have achieved success.


## Snips


### [05:54] The Illusion of Wealth and Happiness


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (04:34 - 05:59)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/3392ae34-be86-4113-83a2-725b6a78ff99)




### ✨ Summary
The allure of making a lot of money can blind individuals to the reality that wealth does not guarantee happiness. Money cannot fix relationship issues, make people like you more, or bring fulfillment. The dream of financial success can feel good, but once achieved, can lead to depression. Furthermore, admiration for success may actually be envy, as people tend to like others more when they are striving, rather than when they have achieved success.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>Would you rather make a million dollars a year and have none of those things? It's so obvious, if that was your choice, which one of those you should pick. And of course, look, you can be poor and miserable, or you could be rich and happy. But only the rich are aware of how tenuous that relationship can be, the relationship between and happiness. Gaining money probably will not fix your marriage. It didn't make your friends like you more. It didn't make you more fulfilled. So what used to be a comforting optimism about what money could do for you in the future is suddenly replaced by the stark reality of what it cannot do for you. Sometimes the dream is what feels good. And once you've hit it, the dream is gone and you actually become depressed. Malcolm Forbes once summed this up well. He said, quote, by the time we've made it, we've had it. All right, number two. What you think is admiration of your success may actually be envy. I mentioned this quote before, but it's a great one. The rapper Drake once said, quote, people like you more when you are working towards something, not when you have it. I think it can be hard to tell when that transition takes place. And it's common for a rich person to think that they are being admired by other people when in fact they are actually being envied. Author</blockquote>
</details>



---

<summary>Show notes</summary>
> This episode will transform how you think about style, aspiration, and the art of knowing what people want before they know it themselves. From working in department stores to advising Steve Jobs on Apple's retail strategy when it didn’t have retail at all, Drexler's career traces the evolution of American retail itself: from local shops to mall dominance, from catalog to digital, from mass market to personalization. Whether you’re an aspiring entrepreneur, a retail enthusiast, or someone looking to build a brand that stands the test of time, Mickey shares invaluable insights on what separates truly successful brands from the rest.  Mickey Drexler is the chairman of Alex Mill. Before that, he was the CEO of J. Crew and sat on the Board of Directors of Apple. He founded Old Navy and Madewell, and served as the CEO of Gap from 1983–2002. ### [01:07] Untitled

---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>Let me tell you a quick story, one that I think about quite a bit. On his way to be sworn in as the most powerful man in the world, Franklin Delano Roosevelt had to be lifted out of his car and carried up the stairs. Francis Perkins, who campaigned with Roosevelt and later became Secretary of Labor, said that the most remarkable thing about Franklin Roosevelt's paralysis was how little its Hindrance seemed to bother him. Roosevelt once told her, quote, if you can't use your legs and they bring you milk when you wanted orange juice, you learn to say that's all right and you drink it. So here is why I like that story, because I think this is a very useful and overlooked skill, which is what this episode is about today. And that is accepting a certain degree of hassle and nonsense when reality demands it. When the situation you are in demands that you have to accept hassle and nonsense and setback and pain and uncertainty, that you accept it. That is an incredible skill that can do you a lot of good in life.</blockquote>
</details>

# Pat Dorsey - Buying Companies With Economic Moats - [Invest Like the Best, EP.51]


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fmegaphone.imgix.net%2Fpodcasts%2Fef669774-cccd-11ed-889b-c36caad6646f%2Fimage%2F158efdddfb983d2678b3530d484e8aa2.jpg%3Fixlib%3Drails-4.3.1%26max-w%3D3000%26max-h%3D3000%26fit%3Dcrop%26auto%3Dformat%2Ccompress&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: Pat Dorsey - Buying Companies With Economic Moats - [Invest Like the Best, EP.51]
- Show: Invest Like the Best with Patrick O'Shaughnessy
- Owner / Host: Colossus
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/42aa6c3c-c680-4cbf-a74c-cd57ded9e8a5)
- Episode publish date: 2017-08-22
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> My guest this week is Pat Dorsey, who was the longtime director of equity research at Morningstar, where he specialized in economic moats: sources of sustained competitive advantage that allow a few companies to deliver huge returns over time. Several years ago he left Morningstar to form his own asset management firm, Dorsey asset management, and build a portfolio of companies with wide moats like those he studied at Morningstar. And while moats are critical, equally important is how companies allocate the capital generated--or made possible--by the existence of the moat.  <br/>>   A special thank you to Brian Bares who introduced me to Pat, and to Will Thorndike--an earlier guest on the show. In the vast majority of conversations you hear on this show, I'm meeting the guest for the first time. I mention this to encourage you to connect me with anyone whose story or way of looking at the world might resonate. Always feel free to contact me with ideas.  <br/>>   Pat and I begin our discussion with the key differences between the sell side and the buy side, and then discuss all aspects of moats and capital allocation. <br/>>    <br/>>   For comprehensive show notes on this episode go to  http://investorfieldguide.com/dorsey <br/>>   For more episodes go to  InvestorFieldGuide.com/podcast .<br/>>   To get involved with Project Frontier, head to  InvestorFieldGuide.com/frontier .<br/>>   Sign up for the book club, where you’ll get a full investor curriculum and then 3-4 suggestions every month at  InvestorFieldGuide.com/bookclub .<br/>>   Follow Patrick on Twitter at  @patrick_oshag <br/>>    <br/>>   Show Notes<br/>>   2:23 – (First question) – Transition from the sell side to the buy side and the biggest surprise <br/>>   3:40 – What is a moat <br/>>   5:16 – What part of the stock market universe has a moat <br/>>   6:57 – Pat’s framework for identifying moat, starting with intangibles <br/>>   8:32 – The power of brands <br/>>   9:44 – what chance does an upstart have to come in and usurp a well-established brand   <br/>>   12:24 – Switching costs as part of the framework for identifying a moat <br/>>   14:55 – The third component of identifying a moat, network effects, and what businesses should do to effectively build one <br/>>   17:29 – Last component, cost advantages/economies of scale <br/>>   19:29 – How do you analyze these four components into an investing framework that can be built into an actual strategy <br/>>   21:13 – How does Pat think about this from a mis-pricing standpoint <br/>>   23:37  – How does Pat incorporate current price of a company in consideration for future returns when pricing a moat <br/>>   25:39 – How should a company with a moat operate to protect that characteristic, especially when it comes to their capital allocation <br/>>   26:51 – Which characteristic of a moat does Pat find most intriguing <br/>>   30:35 – What makes for good and smart capital allocation <br/>>   35:58 – What is Pat’s process for identifying the best investment opportunities <br/>>   38:38 – What are good economics when looking ## Episode AI notes


1. Pat Dorsey emphasizes the importance of understanding VMS businesses to enhance management effectiveness.
2. Mark Leonard's insights illustrate the significance of fostering a strong sales culture within a company.
3. The 'hall pass' philosophy highlights the need for salespeople to cultivate relationships across all organizational levels to improve communication and access.
4. Familiarity within the company enhances sales efficacy and serves as a measure of the organization's sales capability.
5. Identifying business opportunities requires a structured approach, starting with a clear formulation of potential strategies.


## Snips


### [44:24] Master Salesmanship: The Hall Pass Philosophy


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (43:04 - 44:28)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/114e5e3d-9b1b-4059-9b79-28b0193a41d3)




### ✨ Summary
A nuanced understanding of VMS businesses can elevate management effectiveness, as exemplified by Mark Leonard. His insights demonstrate the importance of creating a strong sales culture, which can be metaphorically framed as obtaining a 'hall pass' within a company. This concept emphasizes the need for salespeople to build relationships across all levels, enabling open communication and access throughout the organization. Such familiarity not only enhances selling efficacy but also serves as a marker for assessing a company's overall sales capability. Furthermore, identifying opportunities involves a structured approach, starting with a concise idea formulation that outlines potential business strategies.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>

# Lucky vs. Repeatable


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd3t3ozftmdmh3i.cloudfront.net%2Fstaging%2Fpodcast_uploaded_nologo%2F43542795%2F798c62188974e978.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: Lucky vs. Repeatable
- Show: The Morgan Housel Podcast
- Owner / Host: Morgan Housel
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/b0fb9259-d2cd-4ce3-99eb-57b2193878df)
- Episode publish date: 2024-04-11
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Luck plays such a big role in the world. But few people want to talk about it. If I say you got lucky, I look jealous. If I tell myself that I got lucky, I feel diminished.  Maybe a better way to frame luck is by asking: what isn’t repeatable?  And maybe better yet: The way to get luckier is to find what’s repeatable.
</details>

- Show notes link: [open website](https://podcasters.spotify.com/pod/show/morgan-housel/episodes/Lucky-vs--Repeatable-e320omr)
- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Episode AI notes


1. Emulate repeatable skills for success: Focus on learning and mimicking skills that are repeatable rather than trying to replicate aspects of success that are not repeatable.
2. Avoid trying to mimic non-repeatable parts of success: It is like a middle-aged person dressing as a teenager to be cool, which is not sustainable.
3. Dolo's Law of Irreversibility in evolution emphasizes that once a trait is lost in a species, it cannot be regained due to the complexity of the original path that led to its development.


## Snips


### [03:51] Emulate Repeatable Skills for Success


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (02:48 - 03:52)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0e1a3374-82af-45dd-ae14-c3ce8ff63aca)

Learning from someone includes emulating repeatable skills, not aspects of success that are not repeatable. Trying to mimic non-repeatable parts of success is akin to a middle-aged person dressing like a teenager to be cool. Dolo's Law of Irreversibility in evolution highlights that once a trait is lost in a species, it cannot be gained back due to the complexity of the original path that led to that trait's development.


---

<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>It's so important to know the difference between the two when you're attempting to learn from someone. You want to try to emulate skills that are repeatable. Attempting to copy the parts of someone's success that are not repeatable is the equivalent of like a 56-year dressing like a teenager and expecting to be cool. Look, it may have worked at one time, but it's not going to work for you now, okay? There's a law in evolution called Dolo's Law of Irreversibility. It basically says that once a species loses a trait, it will never gain that trait back because the path that gave it that trait in the first place was so complicated that it cannot and Never will be replicated. So say an animal has horns and then it evolves to lose those horns. The</blockquote>
</details>

## Episode AI notes


1. The importance of earning what you desire rather than taking shortcuts, as shown by the tragic story of the Donner party
2. The common pattern of success leading to complacency and downfall, exemplified by the rise and fall of Sears
3. The significance of focusing on tasks with the most impact in content creation to drive success, such as guest booking and branding
4. The crucial role of personal responsibility and simplicity in investing, challenging the belief that more effort always leads to better outcomes
5. The influence of individual experiences on perspectives, demonstrating how unique circumstances shape beliefs and decisions on investments and politics
6. The concept of conceptual inertia and generational shifts in acceptance of new ideas, highlighting the slow process of integrating new beliefs into societal norms
7. The symbolic nature of spending on luxury items as a representation of personal progress and success, serving as a signal of overcoming struggles and achieving growth

### [58:32] Success and Complacency: The Rise and Fall of Sears

### ✨ Summary
Sears was once a dominant retailer like Walmart or Apple of its time, successful due to their scrappiness and fear of competitors. However, when they became big, they stopped innovating, ignored the rise of Walmart, and eventually went bankrupt. This pattern of success leading to complacency and downfall is common in both companies and individuals, as success often breeds a mindset of relaxation and decreased effort.

And they were like, we're Sears. Nobody compete with us. They stopped innovating. They ignored this upcomer that was chasing them called Walmart, completely ignored it. And by the time that they realized what had happened, it was way too late. They were gone. ### [01:45:38] Conceptual Inertia and Generational Shifts in Acceptance


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:43:49 - 01:45:40)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/080da05f-73ef-45f8-8206-40611a71843b)




### ✨ Summary
Conceptual inertia, the slow movement of assumptions about the world, leads to a delay in accepting new ideas even after they are proven. It takes generational shifts for new beliefs to infuse into scientific discourse as old generations need to pass for new ideas to take hold, as illustrated by the example of the acceptance of the earth not being at the center of the solar system. This phenomenon is akin to the concept that science progresses not just with new discoveries but when the old generation holding onto the past beliefs fades away. The observation that many of the richest men have had multiple divorces provides an insight into the complexity of personal happiness and societal admiration.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Chris Williamson</b><br/><br/>With a historian of existential risk. So he was looking at how scientific knowledge had progressed over time and how humanity's awareness of our ability to go extinct through or not through our own hands developed. And he taught me this term called conceptual inertia, which is, um, you even bring it up in Same As Ever, where you say, there are murmurings amongst some of the scientists that maybe The Earth isn't at the center of the solar system, but most of them are a little bit scared to do it. But even after it was proven and accepted, and people were stopped being burned at the stake by the church for suggesting that this was the case, it still took like two generations for That to actually infuse into the scientific discourse. Why? Well, because of conceptual inertia, because assumptions about the world move very slowly.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>Culture's able to sort of adapt quite quickly, but when it's a foundational thing, it takes a very long time. You almost have to wait for the generation who accepted it to die. Yes, that's it. It's a constant, it's like the Max Planck quote. Science does not progress by new discoveries. It progresses when the old generation who used to believe something eventually dies, and the new generation who didn't come up with the old dogma is now in charge.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Chris Williamson</b><br/><br/>You tweeted a little while ago that there are 13 divorces among the 10 richest men in the world. Seven of the top 10 have been divorced at least once. What's the lesson to learn from that?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>One, it's a very small sample size. This is not like, you know, like hardcore statistics, but a variable that is so important to your lifetime happiness, like whether or not you get divorced, among a group of people who Are so admired by society, oh, I wish I could be one of the richest people in the world, has always been so fascinating to me. And</blockquote>
</details>

Luck plays a significant role in finding a life partner due to the limited timeframe most people have to get married. People often settle for a partner within this narrow window. Young individuals tend to prioritize the wrong qualities in a partner, such as beauty and money, instead of focusing on building a strong friendship. This perspective is akin to investing, where many seek quick high returns, similar to the excitement of being with an attractive spouse or investing in a trendy stock. However, the key to long-term success lies in simplicity and consistency, like maintaining a stable index fund for decades.

I do think there is a sense though, that a lot of people, particularly when they're young, particularly in their early to mid twenties, are obviously maximizing for the wrong variables. Guys are maximizing for beauty and women will tend to maximize for money and prestige. That's a very generalized statement that is not black and white. So if you're offended by that, I'm not talking about you. But obviously what's going to work in the long run is just like, are you best friends with this person? That's it. That's it. I think there's analogy to investing here where a lot of investors want to maximize for the variable. How do I earn the highest returns this year? And in many ways that is like maximizing free beauty. It's like super fun. It's super exciting right now. ### [01:24] The Wisdom of Listening and Observing

### ✨ Summary
John D. Rockefeller, known as the most successful businessman of all time, demonstrated the power of listening and observing in the workplace. Despite being a recluse, he made himself inaccessible and remained quiet, allowing others to talk while he remembered everything. When he did speak, he put everything in its proper place, showing the value of thoughtful communication. Rockefeller's favorite poem emphasized the importance of being observant and speaking wisely, highlighting the wisdom in remaining silent to see more.


---

He spent a lot of time just by himself. He rarely spoke to other people, and he deliberately made himself inaccessible and remained quiet if you happen to catch his attention at the company. A refinery worker who occasionally had Rockefeller's Ear, at work, once said, quote, he lets everybody else talk while he sits back and says nothing. But he seems to remember everything. And when he does begin to speak, he puts everything in its proper place. A And there are so many examples of Rockefeller doing just that. One of Rockefeller's favorite poems that he recited very often at work said, quote, A wise old owl lived in an oak. The more he saw, the less he spoke. The</blockquote>
</details>

# #193 Dr. Jim Loehr: Change the Stories You Tell Yourself


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #193 Dr. Jim Loehr: Change the Stories You Tell Yourself
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)

- Episode publish date: 2024-04-25
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> What if reaching the next level of success wasn't determined by another skill, degree, or course but by something that changed on the  inside ?  That's what Dr. Jim Loehr believes, and in this episode, he reveals everything he knows about mental toughness and winning the mind game. Shane and Loehr discuss the radical importance of the stories you tell yourself—including how they can damage your kids—and how to change the negative stories you believe. Loehr also shares the best reflection questions to ask yourself to reveal personal blindspots, the importance of rituals for calming anxiety and performing under pressure, and how the best in the world use their recovery time effectively.   Dr. Jim Loehr is a world-renowned performance psychologist and author of 16 books. From his more than 30 years of experience and applied research, Dr. Loehr believes the single most important factor in successful achievement, personal fulfillment, and life satisfaction is the strength of one’s character. Dr. Loehr possesses a masters and doctorate in psychology and is a full member of the American Psychological Association.  Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business. Sign up here:   https://fs.blog/newsletter/   Follow Shane on twitter at:   https://twitter.com/ShaneAParrish   Access your transcript today:   https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/dr-jim-loehr-193/  <br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

And also the story that I'm telling myself, where's that story enabling me and powering me and where is it limiting me and getting in the way? And being a little bit more conscious about that. And I think the way that I'll achieve that is probably through journaling and trying to do a little bit more of that. And I've always been a proponent of journaling and especially writing. And for those of you who are longtime listeners, there's something to the power of that that adds the reflection angle. And I think Jim, when he mentioned that it helps rewire your brain, I think what it's really doing is helping you reflect on things in your own writing. Some of the other insights I took away from that is action reduces stress. You need a compelling reason to get better energy, but energy and time, this was another insight that really stood out to me, energy and time towards an objective. And you can think of energy as almost a lead domino or you can think of energy as a huge input into what's happening. And I think energy needs to come from you. And it comes from a compelling reason. That's one way that we get energy. Curiosity is another way to get energy. Curiosity might be the fire that never goes out. We also get energy through our physiology in terms of sleep and eating healthy and we get energy through our relationships. ## Episode AI notes


1. Exponential value proposition growth in marketplaces can lead to a win-win situation for all parties involved by increasing drivers, improving pick-up times, and enhancing consumer value.
2. The concept of increasing returns, whether linear or exponential, is crucial for achieving exponential growth in the market value proposition.
3. The value-based strategy framework by Brandon Berger and Stewart emphasizes factors like consumer willingness to pay, price, cost, and willingness to sell in order to maximize economic profit and increase consumer surplus.
4. Learning by doing is essential for improving productivity across industries, as demonstrated by rights law where cost per unit decreases by 20% for every doubling of cumulative output.


## Snips


### [20:31] Exponential Value Proposition Growth in Marketplace Dynamics


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (19:12 - 20:29)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/c77e6c29-6911-4993-9f11-e7ab6321ae36)




### ✨ Summary
In marketplace dynamics, exponential value proposition growth can occur when increasing the number of drivers leads to quicker pick-up times, enhancing the consumer value. This attracts more users, prompts more drivers to join, ultimately improving coverage, pick-up times, and driver earnings. This positive cycle creates a win-win situation for all involved parties, illustrating a rare but existent phenomenon of increasing returns in marketplaces.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Michael J. Mauboussin</b><br/><br/>The perfect example is Uber, obviously, where David Sachs had this incredible napkin picture, which just showed the loop. But it's pretty simple and it's the answer to your question, which is if you have more drivers, pick up times fall. And if pick up times fall, the consumer value proposition gets better and so more people want to use it. And if more people want to use it in more places, the availability spreads because drivers start popping up to meet the need. And so you end up with more coverage, quicker pick up times, more money for the drivers themselves. And there's literally a win for all sides in the system. And those are rare. I think those are rare. When I push entrepreneurs to think about this and it gets to your increasing or marginal utility, but I just say, as you penetrate a supplier base, one side of a marketplace, whatever, Is the value proposition to the other side going up? And ideally, it would be going up exponentially, which is super hard. It's arguable there are levels of increasing returns. Like you could come up with some kind of scale or index because some of them are more linear and some of them can go exponential. But I think they're rare. They're so rare in fact that some people think they don't exist, but I'm pretty certain they do.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [20:45] Exponential Value Proposition Growth in Marketplaces


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (19:27 - 20:50)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/9c5de513-2af9-4484-b41a-f2e101647fbd)




### ✨ Summary
In marketplaces, as pick up times decrease, the consumer value proposition improves, leading to increased demand and availability. This results in more coverage, quicker pick up times, and higher earnings for drivers, creating a win-win situation for all involved. Achieving exponential growth in the value proposition is rare but essential in penetrating the market. The concept of increasing returns is crucial, although it can be linear or exponential, with the goal being exponential growth. The value-based strategy framework by Brandon Berger and Stewart helps in understanding these concepts by focusing on elements like consumer willingness to pay, price of the product or service, company costs, and willingness to sell.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Michael J. Mauboussin</b><br/><br/>And if pick up times fall, the consumer value proposition gets better and so more people want to use it. And if more people want to use it in more places, the availability spreads because drivers start popping up to meet the need. And so you end up with more coverage, quicker pick up times, more money for the drivers themselves. And there's literally a win for all sides in the system. And those are rare. I think those are rare. When I push entrepreneurs to think about this and it gets to your increasing or marginal utility, but I just say, as you penetrate a supplier base, one side of a marketplace, whatever, Is the value proposition to the other side going up? And ideally, it would be going up exponentially, which is super hard. It's arguable there are levels of increasing returns. Like you could come up with some kind of scale or index because some of them are more linear and some of them can go exponential. But I think they're rare. They're so rare in fact that some people think they don't exist, but I'm pretty certain they do.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Bill Gurley</b><br/><br/>I think a framework that really helps also flesh all these concepts out is called value based strategy. It's a famous framework by Brandon Berger and Stewart. But the way to think about it is almost like a linear stick. ### [21:46] Value-Based Strategy and Consumer Surplus


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (20:29 - 21:50)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/21b549dd-8553-4c5a-b2de-cca1340d857f)




### ✨ Summary
The value-based strategy framework by Brandon Berger and Stewart highlights the importance of factors like willingness to pay, price, cost, and willingness to sell. Economic profit is the difference between price and cost, while network effects can increase the value of a good or service by raising willingness to pay. This increase can lead to more consumer surplus if prices are not raised, resulting in happier customers. The key now lies in constantly increasing willingness to pay to build consumer surplus and maintain future pricing potential.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Bill Gurley</b><br/><br/>I think a framework that really helps also flesh all these concepts out is called value based strategy. It's a famous framework by Brandon Berger and Stewart. But the way to think about it is almost like a linear stick. So at the very top would be willingness to pay of the consumer and then the price of the good or service and then the cost to the company and then willingness to sell. Your economic profit as a company is difference between price and cost. But again, these elements of willingness to pay and willingness to supply are very important and willingness to sell very important. And just to recast what Bill said, because when he explained over to me when I was first learning about it, this very much is the way it came into me. So what happens when network effects, network effects by definition mean the value of the good or service increases more people use that good or service. The willingness to pay goes up. So then you say to yourself, okay, if willingness to pay is going up and I don't raise the price of the good or service, that's more consumer surplus. More consumer surplus means happier customers and they stick around. And it's very powerful. On the other hand, if I do need to raise prices because willingness to pay has gone up, I have a little bit of headroom to do that. In fact, I used to think about pricing power as the sort of key issue. I no longer believe that's the way to think about it. I think the way to think about it is am I increasing as a company? Am I increasing willingness to pay every single day? Again, either adding to consumer surplus or giving myself future potential pricing power if I need it.</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [01:05:44] Learning by Doing and Rights Law


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:04:03 - 01:05:44)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/7d79f796-c92b-4934-bfe1-1ac232d0d258)




### ✨ Summary
The more you do something, the better you get at it and become more productive - this concept is crucial across industries, exemplified by rights law where for every doubling of cumulative output, the cost per unit decreases by 20%. This model has been successfully applied to technologies like lithium batteries and solar energy, showcasing that cost dynamics can be accurately predicted by models such as Moore's law.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Bill Gurley</b><br/><br/>Ken Arrow won the Nobel Prize for his work on equilibrium markets and general theory of equilibrium. Ken Arrow was also, interestingly, a very early participant at seminars at the Santa Fe Institute. So notwithstanding, he was sort of a general equilibrium guy. He actually was one of the early guys sort of encouraging work in complex systems. And as a side note, I'll just mention that he was in his 90s at the time, but we were just sort of standing around having a coffee and he goes, really proud of the general equilibrium theory Work. He kind of got us our whole profession off the track of understanding complex systems as really vital to markets and economy. So I thought that was kind of an interesting observation. The reason I bring up Ken Arrow is that notwithstanding his Nobel Prize, I think his most cited paper is actually about learning by doing, which is 1962. And the idea is pretty straightforward is the more you do something, the better you get at it and you become more productive. And so why is this important for all of us now? Well, let's just think about different industries. You'll mention a moment ago, solar. Solar is a fascinating one. If you do, you have more solar facility increase your output, your costs can go down. So the classic formulation is something called rights law named after Sky Tp, right. And rights law says that for every doubling of cumulative output, your cost per unit goes down by 20%. He wrote that paper back in the 1930s, 90 years ago, roughly speaking. And it turns out, by the way, lithium batteries for automobiles, perfect rights law, cost per solar unit, almost perfect for rights law. And in fact, a number of scientists at Santa Fe Institute tested 60 different technologies to see which model best predicted the actual cost dynamics. The most famous one being, of course, Moore's law.</blockquote>
</details>

# History's Seductive Beliefs


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fd3t3ozftmdmh3i.cloudfront.net%2Fstaging%2Fpodcast_uploaded_nologo%2F43542795%2F798c62188974e978.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: History's Seductive Beliefs
- Show: The Morgan Housel Podcast
- Owner / Host: Morgan Housel
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/63c62e6a-7f60-4b84-8a1a-c539719716dd)
- Episode publish date: 2024-06-11
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> My deepest forecasting belief is that you can better understand the future if you focus on the behaviors that never change instead of the events that might.<br/>>  And those behaviors have a common denominator: They follow the path of least resistance of people trying to simplify a complex world into a few stories that make sense and make them feel good about themselves.<br/>>  Simple stories, feel-good stories. Those are some of history’s most seductive beliefs, and they always will be.<br/>>  Here are a few that stick out.
</details>

- Show notes link: [open website](https://podcasters.spotify.com/pod/show/morgan-housel/episodes/Historys-Seductive-Beliefs-e320onl)
- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Episode AI notes


1. Personal experience is a powerful teacher that can shape beliefs and perspectives more effectively than advice from others.
2. Diverse backgrounds influence how individuals perceive various issues, creating a need for personal experience to gain essential life lessons.
3. Unprecedented events play a significant role in shaping future outcomes, and historical analysis can provide insights but is not a foolproof method for predicting the future.


## Snips


### [10:40] Learning from Personal Experience


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (09:25 - 10:42)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/0ca21f20-6311-4f9f-8c22-6df036c6b825)




### ✨ Summary
Personal experience is the most convincing teacher as individuals often struggle to learn lessons from previous generations until they experience them firsthand. Empathy and open-mindedness are valuable, but nothing compares to the impact of unique circumstances one lives through. It is essential to question personal beliefs and views to determine if they are true or simply seem logical based on individual life experiences.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>More than a century ago, Harry Truman said, quote, The next generation never learns anything from the previous one until it's brought home with a hammer. I've wondered why the next generation can't profit from the generation before, but they never do until they get knocked in the head by experience. Here's at least one reason why that happens. No lesson is more persuasive than the one that you have personally experienced firsthand. You can try to be empathetic and open-minded to other people's views. But when you're trying to figure out how the world works, nothing makes more sense than unique circumstances of what you've lived through firsthand. So, of course, I have views about politics and different social dynamics. I have views of the economy. And I think it's important for myself and everyone to ask, do I believe those things because they're true? Or because they make the most sense given the life that I've happened to live in this generation, in this country, born to my parents? Is it true or does it just make sense given what I've seen? The</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [13:09] Diverse Experiences Shape Perspectives


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (11:47 - 13:12)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/2a1254f6-b565-48bf-9e93-c42e86d5e6fd)




### ✨ Summary
Diverse backgrounds lead to significant experience gaps, influencing how individuals perceive risk, inflation, and other issues. This results in ongoing surprises, challenges in discerning others' wisdom, and the necessity for each generation to individually acquire essential life lessons through personal experience.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>There are massive experience gaps between different nations, different socioeconomic classes, different races, industries, religions, educations, on and on and on. So the person who grew up in poverty thinks about risk and reward in ways that the child of a wealthy banker cannot even fathom, even if he tried. The person who grew up when inflation was high is scared of inflation in a way that the person who grew up with stable prices just isn't. And when you go through these issues, it leads to all kinds of different disparities. One is that we are constantly surprised by events that have been happening forever. One other is that it's hard to distinguish people who have experienced something you haven't from people who aren't smart enough to understand your own experiences. A third is that topics like risk and greed and fear are not the kind of things that we can learn about and master as a society like we did, say, agriculture. As my friend Michael Batnick says, some lessons have to be experienced firsthand before they can be understood. And every generation has to learn on its own, over and over again. So</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [17:10] Unprecedented Events Determine Future Outcomes


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (15:49 - 17:14)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/95ff8917-b905-4a79-bd8f-221966822055)




### ✨ Summary
In many fields, particularly business and investing, the most significant events that shape future outcomes are those that had never occurred before they did. Key economic shifts like the Great Depression, World War II, interest rate collapses, and globalization had no prior occurrences to serve as direct predictions. History is essentially an analysis of changes and although it provides insights, assuming that gathering data and reading history books will predict the future is alluring but flawed.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Morgan Housel</b><br/><br/>Fact, in most fields, especially business and investing, the most important events that changed everything and determined the majority of future outcomes are things that had never Happened until they did. The most important economic events of the last century are probably the Great Depression, World War II, the 40-year collapse in interest rates, and maybe globalization. And while each of those had some sort of ancestors that you could point to that were somewhat analogous, anyone predicting what those events eventually did to the world could be brushed Aside by those who pointed out, say, that negative interest rates had never happened before. A nationwide housing bubble and bust had never happened before. A weapon like the atomic bomb that could deter future wars had never happened before. And then all those things did happen, and they totally changed the world. So all history is a study of what has changed. It's a study of change, but is often used as a guide to the future. And that irony is easy to overlook. Because the idea that if we gather enough data and read enough history books, we'll acquire a map of the future, is so seductive.</blockquote>
</details>



---



# #195 Morgan Housel: Get Rich, Stay Rich


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #195 Morgan Housel: Get Rich, Stay Rich
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/7625b467-8d5c-467a-af36-746baa4d0dc5)
- Episode publish date: 2024-05-23
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> The skills it takes to  get  rich are drastically different from the skills it takes to  stay  rich. Few understand this phenomenon more than Morgan Housel. He's identified unique lessons about wealth, happiness, and money by studying the world's richest families and learning what they did to build their wealth and just how quickly they squandered it all.  In this conversation, Shane and Housel discuss various aspects of risk-taking, wealth accumulation, and financial independence. Morgan explains the importance of understanding personal financial goals and the dangers of social comparison, lets everyone in on his personal financial “mistake” that instantly made him sleep better at night, and why the poorest people in the world disproportionately play the lottery—and why it makes sense that they do. They also touch on the influence of upbringing on financial behaviors, the difference between being rich and wealthy, and the critical role of compounding in financial success. Of course, we can’t have a writer as good as Morgan Housel on the podcast and not ask him about his process, so Housel concludes with insights into storytelling, his writing processes, and the importance of leading by example in teaching financial values to children.  Morgan Housel is a partner at Collaborative Fund. Previously, he was an analyst at The Motley Fool. He is a two-time winner of the Best in Business Award from the Society of American Business Editors and Writers and was selected by the Columbia Journalism Review for the Best Business Writing anthology. He's the author of two books:  The Psychology of Money  and  Same as Ever .  Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business. Sign up here:   https://fs.blog/newsletter/   Follow Shane on twitter at:   https://twitter.com/ShaneAParrish   Access your transcript today:   https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/morgan-housel-195/  <br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

- Export date: 2026-03-26T13:24


## Episode AI notes


1. Money should pass between parents and kids to provide financial support without hindering children's ability to carve their own path.
2. Maximizing returns in investing requires endurance and focusing on the long term, not just short-term gains.
3. Consistent writing and receiving feedback play a crucial role in improving writing skills over time.

### [01:20:06] How to Increase Your Returns This Year


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:18:46 - 01:20:06)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/1d2810c7-e2bc-4992-8122-be356063fa25)




### ✨ Summary
Maximizing the variable that matters in investing is time and endurance, not just focusing on short-term returns. By earning average returns for an above average period of time, one can achieve exceptional results. Compounding effectively depends on the power of time, with the exponent being the key factor for significant outcomes. Instead of solely concentrating on increasing returns for a single year, it is crucial to prioritize endurance and focus on the long term. The investing industry often emphasizes short-term returns, but the real leverage lies in endurance. Having a wide funnel and a tight filter approach in selecting reading materials can aid in exploring a variety of topics and gaining valuable insights.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>You're doing it for.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>It's not even the growth, it's the duration that matters.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Yes. So I said this earlier, how I think about my own investing philosophy. If I can be average for an above average period of time, that leads to a way above average result. It's not about like, what are the returns that I can earn this year? If I can earn 8% returns for 50 years, the results are ridiculous. The results are absurd. And so maximizing the variable that matters, which is time and endurance, you know, all compounding is effectively is returns to the power of time. And so if you understand math, the exponent there is what's doing all the heavy lifting. Like maximize for that. But where is all of the effort in the investing industry? It's in the smaller number. It's in returns. How do I increase my returns this year? I think when you understand all the power, all the wealth, all the leverage is in the endurance, just focus on that before you think about anything else.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>That's a really powerful way to think about it. Let's switch gears and talk about reading and writing. How do you select what you read?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>I heard this idea, I think it was from Patrick O 'Shaughnessy many years ago who said, you want a wide funnel and a tight filter. And I think what he means by that, what I took away from that is I will start reading any book on any topic that looks even mildly interesting to</blockquote>
</details>

### [01:29:52] Continuous Writing Improvement


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:28:33 - 01:29:55)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/89082e48-14f4-41bf-adec-9e0f98b0cebd)




### ✨ Summary
Consistent writing and the courage to face unfiltered feedback from readers play a critical role in improving writing skills. By writing thousands of blog posts over a decade and receiving direct feedback, one can significantly enhance their writing abilities. Testing ideas on platforms like Twitter can help refine concepts before turning them into more substantial content like blog posts or books. Just like comedians who test jokes in small clubs to understand what truly resonates, writers can iterate and improve by engaging with their audience and refining their craft.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Did you learn to write? You didn't even go to high school.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>When I was at The Motley Fool for 10 years, that was a 10 -year period where I was sometimes writing three posts per day, three articles per day, doing that every day for almost a decade. I wrote thousands and thousands of blog posts. And when you write online, people are merciless about the feedback they give you. The readers in the comment sections are on Twitter will tell you in no uncertain terms, this article was shit and you did terrible job. Or they'll say this was really good. I really enjoyed it. So having that level of constant feedback and doing that thousands of times over a decade will turn anyone into a much better writer than they were when they started. So that was that was really what it was for me. It's a combination of of quantity and fierce, unvarnished feedback from readers. Do you test ideas? I think in some ways you test ideas in Twitter, and if they work, you can turn those ideas into a blog post. And if the blog post worked, you can turn it into a book idea or book chapter. That's kind of the natural progression for a lot of these things. And just like it's very true in comedy too, even the best comedians, the world -class comedians don't necessarily know what's funny until they've tested it. And this is why George Carlin, Chris Rock, Jerry Seinfeld, they test their new jokes in tiny clubs.</blockquote>
</details>

# Dev Ittycheria - The Database Evolution - [Invest Like the Best, EP.373]

## Episode metadata

<summary>Show notes</summary>
> My guest today is  Dev Ittycheria . Dev is the CEO of MongoDB, the developer data platform with tens of thousands of customers in 100 different countries. He joined the company as CEO in 2014, taking it public in 2017, and is now approaching a decade of leading MongoDB to become a go-to choice for the most sophisticated organizations around the world. We discuss Dev’s philosophy for constructing an exceptional enterprise sales organization, why he feels a leader must be incredibly judgemental to drive excellence, and how he plans to guide MongoDB through another technological transition. ## Episode AI notes


1. Recruiting goes beyond background and skills to understand values, work ethic, and motivations
2. Understanding someone's values, beliefs, and measuring grit are crucial in determining the right fit for a business
3. Assessing psychological factors is essential for long-term success in recruiting


## Snips


### [38:36] Understanding the Psychological Edge in Recruiting


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (37:21 - 38:36)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/8125a8a2-fadc-479b-a966-3b9d0931234d)




### ✨ Summary
Recruiting should delve beyond an individual's background and skills to understand what drives them, their values, work ethic, and motivations. Assessing someone's values, beliefs in hard work, and measuring grit are crucial in determining the right fit for a business. By comprehending the underlying psyche of a person, recruiters can ensure they are getting the right individual onboard, as human nature and psychological factors play a crucial role in long-term success.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Patrick O'Shaughnessy</b><br/><br/>Can you explain your philosophy of recruiting? It sounds like that is a key thing that you've always focused on in your businesses. And I know it is at Mongo too. What do you think is the most important part of how you think about getting the right people into the business? Anything that you've done that you feel is especially impactful?</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Dev Ittycheria</b><br/><br/>I think one of the things about recruiting, most people make the common mistake of just focusing on what the person's background experience is, where they work, what skills they had, And maybe like what successes that they can talk about. I think a big part of recruiting is trying to figure out what makes a person tick. What is it about them? What is it about their upbringing? What drives them? What motivates them in terms of who they are and where they want to go and what they want to be? And I think if you don't really understand that underlying psyche of the person, how do you know what you're really getting? It's one thing to say like, hey, do you have experience building product? Do you have experience selling enterprise software? But how do you assess someone's values? Do you believe in the value of hard work? How do you measure grit? One of your common guests that I love made a statement that I completely agree with was Jeremy Giffon, who talked about that the only enduring edge in life is psychological as human nature Doesn't change. That was like words out of my mouth.</blockquote>
</details>

# #196 Brent Beshore: Business Brilliance and Happiness at Home


<img src="https://wsrv.nl/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsupercast-storage-assets.b-cdn.net%2Fchannel%2F131%2Fartwork%2Flarge-fbdcfe63533c4167b8c407b82b6db5e3.jpg&w=200&h=200" width="200" alt="Cover" />


## Episode metadata
- Episode title: #196 Brent Beshore: Business Brilliance and Happiness at Home
- Show: [Members Only] The Knowledge Project  (🔓 for Markus Binder)
- Owner / Host: Shane Parrish
- Episode link: [open in Snipd](https://share.snipd.com/episode/9cb12668-443e-42d6-af64-3a3723fc8043)
- Episode publish date: 2024-06-06
<details>
<summary>Show notes</summary>
> Succeeding in both life and business is very difficult. The skills needed to scale a company often clash with those required to cultivate a thriving home life. Yet, Brent Beshore seems to have cracked the code—or at least he's actively working on it. In this conversation, he spills his secrets on excelling in both arenas.  This episode is split into two parts: the first 45 minutes covers life and how to be a better person. Brent opens up about the evolution of his marriage, physical health, and inner life. The rest of the episode focuses on business. Shane and Beshore discuss private equity, how to hire (and when to fire) CEOs, incentives, why debt isn’t a good thing in an unpredictable world, stewardship versus ownership, and why personality tests are so important for a functional organization.  After beginning his career as an entrepreneur, Brent Beshore founded Permanent Equity in 2007 and leads the firm as CEO. He works with investors and operators to evaluate new investment opportunities.  Stay up to date by signing up for Brain Food, our weekly newsletter that comes out each Sunday with timeless insights for life and business. Sign up here:   https://fs.blog/newsletter/   Follow Shane on twitter at:   https://twitter.com/ShaneAParrish   Access your transcript today:   https://fs.blog/knowledge-project-podcast-transcripts/brent-beshore-196/  <br/>> <br/>> <br/>>              <br/>> <br/>>     📢 Share   |<br/>> <br/>>     ⚙️ Manage Subscription
</details>

## Episode AI notes


1. Careers often end tragically when individuals are promoted beyond their capabilities and cannot humble themselves to step back.
2. Acknowledging and understanding the dynamics of control in relationships is crucial for forming genuine connections.
3. Habits define who we are and require focus; adding multiple habits at once is difficult.
4. Daily habits like reading, praying, expressing love, and asking loved ones how they feel most loved are important.
5. There are three main approaches to handling conflict: moving against, moving towards, and moving away, which interact in conflict resolution.


## Snips


### [00:00] Careers die by suicide, not homicide


[🎧 Play snip - 25sec️ (00:00 - 00:25)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/4b7b8662-f08b-43c1-b514-8b76b43f260d)




### ✨ Summary
Careers often end tragically when individuals are promoted beyond their capabilities and cannot humble themselves to step back. Their pride and identity become intertwined with their title and position, leading to a downfall known as the Peter Principle.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Careers often die by suicide, not by homicide. Over and over and over again, I've watched this tragedy happen, which is the Peter Principle. Someone rises and they rise beyond their abilities and then they can't take a step back. Their pride, their ego, their identity is rooted now in their title, in their position. And you say to them, we love you. We think you're</blockquote>
</details>



---


### [14:42] Real Relationships Require Mutual Control


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (13:21 - 14:44)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/75bbc956-1809-47db-8ee9-9ddb00d96e93)




### ✨ Summary
Acknowledging and understanding the dynamics of control in relationships is crucial. The idea of always being in control can hinder forming genuine connections, as mutual ceding of control is essential for true friendships and relationships to blossom.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Or something very small happened and I'm triggered into this like spiral. That's weird. And what I would do in the past is I would just kind of like shove it down, right? Like, oh, I don't know. I don't get it, but life's weird and whatever. Got to go on. Yeah, you got to go on. And so anyway, through this series of, of sessions, and I mean, I've done a lot now, like probably 25 or 30 of these three hour sessions. So a lot of time. We got down to one of the sessions and it was actually probably about four or five months ago, out of the blue, it was kind of seemingly unconnected. And we were talking about friendship and my counselor said, do you always pay? And I said, yeah, of course I always pay. She was like, hmm, I got out of the last hundred times that you shared a meal with somebody. How many times did you pay? And I was like, like a hundred. And she kind of sat back and I was kind of proud of myself, right? Like, I'm a good person. I pay for the bill. I care about people, right? This is like the thing I told myself in my head. I do the exact same thing. Yeah. And by the way, those motivations are, I think, real and true. They aren't bad. But she said, yeah, I wouldn't be your friend. And I remember just being rocked by it. Like, you wouldn't be my friend. She goes, I was like, why? And like totally broke my paradigms. And she said, because in a friendship, in a real relationship, you cede control to the other person, the other person concedes control to you. It's not you're always controlling.</blockquote>
</details>

### [47:42] The Value of Good Habits

### ✨ Summary
Habits define who we are; they require focus and are hard to change. Adding multiple habits at once is difficult. While not naturally inclined towards habits, with age, the speaker appreciates the value of good habits. Daily habits include starting the day by reading and praying, expressing love and acknowledgment to family, and asking loved ones how they feel most loved.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>

What, how we spend our days is how we spend our lives. Like our habits are who we are habits are very sticky and hard to change. They require a tremendous amount of focus and it's very difficult to add multiple habits at once is what I found. I'm also not a personality that loves habits. I don't love structure. I really enjoy the variety of life. And so in many ways, like, habits great on me, but I've really come as I get older to appreciate the value of good habits. And so the habits I try and I would say these are most days, almost all days. I get up and the first thing I do is read and pray. Um, the, I always try to tell my kids and my wife that I see them and I know them and I love them and that there's nothing they can do to ever move outside of my love for them. Um, um, one of the questions I love asking my kids now is what do I do when you feel most loved?</blockquote>

### [01:03:31] Different Approaches to Handling Conflict


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (01:02:10 - 01:03:32)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/2b9a1b77-67f9-4032-bd66-17dd88794966)




### ✨ Summary
There are three main approaches to handling conflict: moving against by being confrontational, moving towards by glossing over the issue to maintain peace, and moving away by isolating oneself. These approaches interact with each other and are interesting to observe in conflict resolution.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Like it's, it's like very much like a forced submission, right? So this is somebody who gets up in your face, who's yelling or who's like, kind of like talking down to you. Like a very, very confrontational, right? That's kind of like one phase. And by the way, again, we do all three of these. We just depends on the order, but we all have an order to how we do these things. And then by the way, how they interact with one another is super interesting as well. The second one is move towards the person. So the first one's move against, second one's move towards. Move towards is we need to be okay. I'm not okay unless we're okay. Let's just gloss over whatever's happened. It's okay, it'll be fine. Let's just water under the bridge, let's just move on. Right? It's avoidant. Well, it's avoidant in a way that is, it feels very relational, but it's actually not. Because it's not for the good of the other, it's actually for their good. They want to have things be okay. But it actually doesn't address the underlying issue at all. And then the third one is move away. So this is to isolate. And so if you watch all of your conflict will follow that pattern.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 2</b><br/><br/>Talk to me about debt and your thoughts on debt and the optionality it gives you and when it's appropriate, when it's not appropriate.</blockquote><br/><blockquote><b>Speaker 1</b><br/><br/>Is not a source of return, it is an amplifier return. So it makes good things be great. It can take mediocre things and destroy them.</blockquote>
</details>

## Episode AI notes


1. Embrace Contrarian Thinking for Innovation: Walt Disney combined television programming with the amusement park industry, despite skepticism and reluctance from major networks like CBS and NBC. This demonstrates the value of thinking beyond conventional problem-solving and innovating in unconventional ways.
2. Challenging the Status Quo and Pursuing a Vision: Disney's decision to create both a television show and an amusement park challenged the norm and highlighted the significance of pursuing a vision that goes beyond just solving problems. Despite facing skepticism, he maintained a visionary perspective.
3. The importance of embracing contrarian thinking and pursuing a vision is evident in Walt Disney's approach to launching Disneyland. By thinking beyond conventional solutions and challenging the status quo, Disney was able to create something revolutionary that changed the world.


## Snips


### [11:56] Challenging the Status Quo and Pursuing a Vision


[🎧 Play snip - 1min️ (10:36 - 11:57)](https://share.snipd.com/snip/699878b1-d489-4fad-b97c-a8ebd6203cdb)




### ✨ Summary
Walt Disney's decision to create both a television show and an amusement park was an unconventional and contrarian move, as CBS and NBC were not willing to invest in the amusement park industry due to its negative reputation at the time. Despite facing skepticism and questions about his decision, Disney maintained a visionary perspective, emphasizing the importance of pursuing a vision beyond just solving problems.


---




#### 📚 Transcript
<details>
<summary>Click to expand</summary>
<blockquote><b>David Senra</b><br/><br/>He says, oh, you want a TV show? That's good. You have to. I'll make you a deal. I'll give you television programming if you help pay for my park. He had offered that deal because at this time in history, CBS and NBC were a lot larger. ABC was like a distant third. And so CBS and NBC had turned down this deal. But pointing this out is so important because it couldn't be more contrarian. What Walt Disney was doing could not have been more contrarian. So CBS and NBC wanted a Disney television show too, but not enough to get involved in the amusement park industry, which in 1955 had become widely perceived as both dangerous and disreputable. And so this question that everybody's asking him, his brother's asking him, it says right here, his wife's like, why are you building an amusement park? Everybody inside and outside his company is going to ask him this question. There's actually a phenomenal, he has got a phenomenal response. And then the lesson behind, if you read between the lines of what he's saying, is a phenomenal lesson to keep in mind and to learn too. I'll get there later on. But right before the park opens, a journalist is asking him, like, why are you doing this? And the way I would describe Walt Disney's view on Disneyland and what he was doing. I heard of this interview from Elon Musk a few years ago that was fascinating. You know, they were trying to, same thing. Journalists were interviewing him, trying to get behind like, why are you doing Tesla? Why are you doing SpaceX? Why are you doing all this stuff? And he says, this is what Elon said that I think could be applied to Disney's mindset as well. Elon Musk said, I think life must be more than just solving problems.</blockquote>
</details>

### [11:57] Embrace Contrarian Thinking for Innovation
